If the inspector finds **at most $c$ bad cars** (e.g., cars without valid documents), the whole batch of $N$ cars is allowed through. Otherwise, the entire batch is rejected.

Now consider this — *what is the chance that a batch containing, say, 5% bad cars is allowed through?* That chance is exactly the **$P_a$** plotted on the OC curve. Plotting this $P_a$ for various bad-car percentages gives us the OC curve.

- If the batch has **0% bad cars** → almost certainly accepted ($P_a \approx 1$).
- If the batch has **100% bad cars** → almost certainly rejected ($P_a \approx 0$).
- The sharper the drop in the middle, the **better the sampling plan**.

## 1.4 Types of OC Curves

> [!NOTE]
> **Two broad categories of OC curves exist in industrial statistics:**

1. **Type-A OC Curve:** Assumes the lot size $N$ is **finite** and samples are drawn *without replacement*. Uses the **Hypergeometric distribution**.
2. **Type-B OC Curve:** Assumes the lot size $N$ is **infinite (or very large)** and successive lots are independent. Uses the **Binomial distribution** (with Poisson approximation when $n$ is large and $p$ is small).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU laboratory calculations, the **Binomial/Poisson (Type-B)** approach is most commonly adopted because it is mathematically tractable and provides excellent approximation for $N \geq 10n$.

## 1.5 Why OC Curves Matter in Quality Engineering

- They form the **decision-making backbone** of acceptance sampling (e.g., MIL-STD-105E, ISO 2859, Dodge-Romig plans).
- They visually depict the **two risks** of any sampling plan: producer's risk ($\alpha$) and consumer's risk ($\beta$).
- They help the engineer **design and compare** sampling plans to meet AQL (Acceptable Quality Level) and LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective) targets.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The OC curve answers one central probabilistic question:

> *"Given that a lot of size $N$ contains exactly $D = pN$ defective items, and we draw a random sample of size $n$ (without replacement), what is the probability that the number of defectives $d$ in our sample is less than or equal to the acceptance number $c$?"*

Mathematically:

$$P_a = P(d \leq c)$$

## 2.2 The Three Probability Models

### Model 1: Hypergeometric Distribution (Exact, Finite Lot)

Used when the lot size $N$ is finite and the sample is drawn without replacement.

$$P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{\binom{D}{i} \cdot \binom{N-D}{n-i}}{\binom{N}{n}}$$

where $D = pN$ is the number of defectives in the lot.

### Model 2: Binomial Distribution (Approximate, Infinite Lot)

Used when $N$ is large relative to $n$ (rule of thumb: $N \geq 10n$).

$$P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} \cdot p^{i} \cdot (1-p)^{n-i}$$

### Model 3: Poisson Approximation to Binomial

Used when $n$ is large ($\geq 20$) AND $p$ is small ($\leq 0.10$). The Poisson parameter is $\lambda = np$.

$$P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \cdot \lambda^{i}}{i!}, \quad \text{where } \lambda = np$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Poisson approximation simplifies computation drastically, which is why KTU lab evaluations commonly adopt this form.

## 2.3 Critical Design Points on an OC Curve

| Point | Quality Level | Risk/Parameter | KTU Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| AQL ($p_1$) | Acceptable Quality Level | Producer's Risk $\alpha = 1 - P_a$ | Should be small |
| LTPD ($p_2$) | Lot Tolerance Percent Defective | Consumer's Risk $\beta = P_a$ | Should be small |
| IQL | Indifference Quality Level | $P_a = 0.50$ | Center reference |
| AOQL | Average Outgoing Quality Limit | Max of $AOQ$ curve | Measure of system protection |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **exam-ready formulas** for OC curve construction. Master these thoroughly.

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Single Sampling Plan Notation | $(N, n, c)$ | $N$ = lot, $n$ = sample, $c$ = acceptance number |
| 2 | Binomial $P_a$ | $P_a = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{c}\binom{n}{i}p^{i}(1-p)^{n-i}$ | For $N \geq 10n$ |
| 3 | Poisson $P_a$ | $P_a = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{c}\frac{e^{-np}(np)^{i}}{i!}$ | For $n \geq 20$, $p \leq 0.10$ |
| 4 | Hypergeometric $P_a$ | $P_a = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{c}\frac{\binom{pN}{i}\binom{N-pN}{n-i}}{\binom{N}{n}}$ | Exact, finite lot |
| 5 | Producer's Risk | $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$ | At AQL = $p_1$ |
| 6 | Consumer's Risk | $\beta = P_a(p_2)$ | At LTPD = $p_2$ |
| 7 | Indifference Quality $p_{0.5}$ | $n p_{0.5} \approx$ tabulated value | $P_a = 0.50$ point |
| 8 | Operating Ratio (OR) | $OR = p_2 / p_1$ | Good plan: $OR \leq 4$ |
| 9 | AOQ (Average Outgoing Quality) | $AOQ = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | Rectifying inspection |
| 10 | AOQL (max of AOQ) | $AOQL = \max_{p} AOQ(p)$ | Found by differentiation |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Industry:** Setting up incoming inspection plans for raw materials.
- **Defense & Aerospace:** MIL-STD sampling plans rely entirely on OC curves.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Batch acceptance/release decisions for drug products.
- **Electronics:** Component-level lot acceptance (SMT, IC chips).
- **Automotive:** Tier-1 supplier quality agreements reference OC-based AQLs.

> [!NOTE]
> The OC curve is essentially the **fingerprint** of a sampling plan. Two plans with the same AQL can have drastically different OC curves, and only the OC curve reveals the true discriminating ability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Operating Characteristic Function

### Step 1: Setup the Sampling Scenario

Consider a lot of $N$ items containing $D = pN$ defective items. A sample of $n$ items is drawn at random. The number of defectives $d$ in the sample is a random variable.

### Step 2: Probability of Finding Exactly $i$ Defectives

Because sampling is without replacement from a finite population, the exact probability of finding $i$ defectives in the sample of $n$ follows the **Hypergeometric distribution**:

$$P(d = i) = \frac{\binom{D}{i} \cdot \binom{N-D}{n-i}}{\binom{N}{n}}, \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, c$$

### Step 3: Acceptance Rule

The lot is accepted if and only if $d \leq c$. Therefore:

$$P_a = P(d \leq c) = \sum_{i=0}^{c} P(d = i)$$

$$\boxed{P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{\binom{pN}{i} \cdot \binom{N-pN}{n-i}}{\binom{N}{n}}}$$

### Step 4: Reduction to Binomial (Infinite Lot Assumption)

When $N \to \infty$ (or practically $N \geq 10n$), the hypergeometric collapses to the binomial. We can show this via the limit:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{\binom{pN}{i} \binom{N-pN}{n-i}}{\binom{N}{n}} = \binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n-i}$$

Hence:

$$P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n-i}$$

### Step 5: Reduction to Poisson (Large $n$, Small $p$)

When $n$ is large and $p$ is small such that $np = \lambda$ remains finite, the binomial converges to the Poisson distribution:

$$\binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n-i} \;\longrightarrow\; \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{i}}{i!}, \quad \lambda = np$$

Therefore:

$$P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np} (np)^{i}}{i!}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Construction of OC Curve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Lab Typical):**
> Construct the OC curve for a single sampling plan with $N = 2000$, $n = 50$, and $c = 1$. Use the **Poisson approximation** to find $P_a$ for $p = 0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, 0.10, 0.15$.

### Solution — Step-by-Step Table Computation

**Given:** $n = 50$, $c = 1$, $\lambda = np = 50p$.

The Poisson probability of acceptance formula is:

$$P_a = e^{-\lambda} + \lambda e^{-\lambda} = e^{-\lambda}(1 + \lambda)$$

Let us compute $P_a$ for each $p$:

| $p$ | $\lambda = 50p$ | $e^{-\lambda}$ | $1 + \lambda$ | $P_a = e^{-\lambda}(1+\lambda)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.005 | 0.25 | 0.7788 | 1.25 | **0.9735** |
| 0.010 | 0.50 | 0.6065 | 1.50 | **0.9098** |
| 0.020 | 1.00 | 0.3679 | 2.00 | **0.7358** |
| 0.030 | 1.50 | 0.2231 | 2.50 | **0.5578** |
| 0.050 | 2.50 | 0.0821 | 3.50 | **0.2873** |
| 0.070 | 3.50 | 0.0302 | 4.50 | **0.1357** |
| 0.100 | 5.00 | 0.0067 | 6.00 | **0.0404** |
| 0.150 | 7.50 | 0.0006 | 8.50 | **0.0047** |

### Detailed Calculation Walkthrough (for $p = 0.02$)

$$\lambda = np = 50 \times 0.02 = 1.00$$

$$P_a = e^{-1.0} \left(1 + 1.0\right) = 0.3679 \times 2.00 = 0.7358$$

### Detailed Calculation Walkthrough (for $p = 0.10$)

$$\lambda = np = 50 \times 0.10 = 5.00$$

$$P_a = e^{-5.0} \left(1 + 5.0\right) = 0.00674 \times 6.00 = 0.0404$$

### Plotting the OC Curve

Using the computed $P_a$ values, the OC curve is plotted as follows:

| $p$ | 0 | 0.005 | 0.01 | 0.02 | 0.03 | 0.05 | 0.07 | 0.10 | 0.15 | 0.20 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $P_a$ | 1.000 | 0.974 | 0.910 | 0.736 | 0.558 | 0.287 | 0.136 | 0.040 | 0.005 | 0.001 |

The resulting OC curve starts near $P_a = 1$ for $p = 0$ and falls steeply through the middle (around $p \approx 0.025$, the indifference quality) toward $P_a = 0$ for $p \to 1$.

### Extracting the Risks

Assuming the plan targets:
- **AQL** $p_1 = 0.01$ → $P_a = 0.910$ → Producer's Risk $\alpha = 1 - 0.910 = 0.090$ (9%)
- **LTPD** $p_2 = 0.07$ → $P_a = 0.136$ → Consumer's Risk $\beta = 0.136$ (13.6%)

The **Operating Ratio** is:

$$OR = \frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{0.07}{0.01} = 7.0$$

> [!NOTE]
> A good sampling plan aims for $OR \leq 4$. Here, $OR = 7.0$, suggesting the plan could be tightened (increase $n$ or reduce $c$) to achieve better discrimination.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Operating Ratio and Indifference Quality

### Indifference Quality ($p_{0.5}$)

The point on the OC curve where $P_a = 0.50$ is the **Indifference Quality Level (IQL)**. Using the Poisson form, the approximate solution is:

$$p_{0.5} \approx \frac{\chi^{2}_{(2c+2, 0.5)}}{2n}$$

where $\chi^{2}_{(2c+2, 0.5)}$ is the chi-square critical value at 0.5 probability with $2c+2$ degrees of freedom.

### Operating Ratio (OR)

$$OR = \frac{LTPD}{AQL} = \frac{p_2}{p_1}$$

A lower OR indicates a more **discriminating** sampling plan.

### Quick $p_{0.5}$ Estimates for Common Plans (Poisson Tables)

| $c$ | $n p_{0.5}$ (approx.) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0.693 |
| 1 | 1.678 |
| 2 | 2.674 |
| 3 | 3.672 |
| 4 | 4.671 |
| 5 | 5.670 |

So for $n = 50$, $c = 1$:

$$p_{0.5} \approx \frac{1.678}{50} = 0.0336 \approx 3.36\%$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of OC Curve Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STEP 1: DEFINE SAMPLING PLAN"] --> nodeB["STEP 2: SELECT PROBABILITY MODEL"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"IS N LARGE COMPARED TO n?"}
    nodeC -- YES --> nodeD["USE BINOMIAL OR POISSON MODEL"]
    nodeC -- NO --> nodeE["USE HYPERGEOMETRIC MODEL"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["STEP 3: COMPUTE Pa FOR EACH p VALUE"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["STEP 4: TABULATE p AND Pa VALUES"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["STEP 5: PLOT Pa VERSUS p"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["STEP 6: MARK KEY POINTS ON CURVE"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["AQL p1 WITH PRODUCER RISK alpha"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["LTPD p2 WITH CONSUMER RISK beta"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["IQL p0.5 AT Pa EQUALS 0.50"]
    nodeI --> nodeM["AOQL AT MAXIMUM OF AOQ CURVE"]
    nodeJ --> nodeN["STEP 7: INTERPRET AND VALIDATE PLAN"]
    nodeK --> nodeN
    nodeL --> nodeN
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO["PLAN ACCEPTED OR REDESIGNED"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Probability Model Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["GIVEN N n c AND p"] --> checkA{"IS N LESS THAN 10n?"}
    checkA -- YES --> useHyp["USE HYPERGEOMETRIC DISTRIBUTION"]
    checkA -- NO --> checkB{"IS p LESS THAN 0.10 AND n GREATER THAN 20?"}
    checkB -- YES --> usePoi["USE POISSON APPROXIMATION"]
    checkB -- NO --> useBin["USE BINOMIAL DISTRIBUTION"]
    useHyp --> computePa["COMPUTE Pa USING SUMMATION"]
    usePoi --> computePa
    useBin --> computePa
    computePa --> plotOC["PLOT Pa AGAINST p TO OBTAIN OC CURVE"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Shape of the OC Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pAxis["p AXIS: 0 TO 1"] --- region1["REGION 1: HIGH ACCEPTANCE ZONE"]
    pAxis --- region2["REGION 2: TRANSITION ZONE"]
    pAxis --- region3["REGION 3: HIGH REJECTION ZONE"]
    region1 --> pointA["Pa APPROXIMATELY 1 AT p EQUALS 0"]
    region2 --> pointB["Pa EQUALS 0.50 AT p EQUALS p0.5 IQL"]
    region3 --> pointC["Pa APPROACHES 0 AT p APPROACHES 1"]
    pointA --> curveLabel["IDEAL OC CURVE: SHARP SIGMOIDAL DROP"]
    pointB --> curveLabel
    pointC --> curveLabel
    pointA --> riskA["PRODUCER RISK alpha AT AQL p1"]
    pointB --> curveLabel
    pointC --> riskB["CONSUMER RISK beta AT LTPD p2"]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Schematic — Ideal vs. Real OC Curves

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    compA["COMPARISON OF OC CURVES"] --> idealCurve["IDEAL CURVE: STEP FUNCTION AT p EQUALS pCRITICAL"]
    compA --> realCurve["REAL CURVE: SMOOTH SIGMOIDAL DECREASE"]
    compA --> poorCurve["POOR PLAN: SHALLOW GRADUAL DECREASE"]
    idealCurve --> remarkA["PERFECT DISCRIMINATION BUT UNREALISTIC"]
    realCurve --> remarkB["PRACTICAL CHOICE FOR INDUSTRY USE"]
    poorCurve --> remarkC["HIGH RISK OF ACCEPTING BAD LOTS AND REJECTING GOOD LOTS"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Mermaid diagrams:** Each block represents a stage or concept in OC curve construction. Follow the arrows top-to-bottom (or left-to-right) to trace the entire workflow from defining the sampling plan to interpreting the final OC curve.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define OC Curve. What does it represent?
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
An Operating Characteristic (OC) curve is a graph that shows the probability of acceptance ($P_a$) of a lot as a function of the incoming lot quality (fraction defective $p$) for a specified acceptance sampling plan $(N, n, c)$. It represents the **discriminating power** of a sampling plan — i.e., its ability to correctly accept good lots and reject bad lots. The shape, slope, and key points (AQL, LTPD, IQL) on the OC curve help quality engineers evaluate the plan's effectiveness.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between Type-A and Type-B OC Curves.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Type-A OC Curve | Type-B OC Curve |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Lot Size | Finite (specific $N$) | Infinite (or $N \to \infty$) |
| Distribution | Hypergeometric | Binomial / Poisson |
| Sampling | Without replacement (exact) | With replacement (approximate) |
| Use | Specific lot evaluation | Long-run process quality |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the probability of acceptance $P_a$ of a single sampling plan using the **binomial distribution**. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single sampling plan has $N = 1000$, $n = 60$, and $c = 2$. Using the **Poisson approximation**, compute $P_a$ for $p = 0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.08$ and tabulate the values to construct the OC curve. Also find the producer's risk at AQL = 0.02. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

*Assumptions:*
1. Lot size $N$ is very large (effectively infinite).
2. Each item in the lot is independent and has the same probability $p$ of being defective.
3. Sampling is random with replacement (or $N \geq 10n$ for practical use without replacement).

*Derivation:*
Let $X$ = number of defectives in a sample of size $n$. Then $X$ follows a Binomial distribution with parameters $n$ and $p$:

$$P(X = i) = \binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n-i}, \quad i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

The lot is accepted if $X \leq c$. Hence:

$$P_a = P(X \leq c) = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n-i}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

Given: $n = 60$, $c = 2$, $\lambda = np = 60p$.

Using Poisson approximation:

$$P_a = e^{-\lambda} \left[ 1 + \lambda + \frac{\lambda^{2}}{2} \right]$$

| $p$ | $\lambda = 60p$ | $e^{-\lambda}$ | $1 + \lambda + \lambda^{2}/2$ | $P_a$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.01 | 0.60 | 0.5488 | $1 + 0.6 + 0.18 = 1.78$ | **0.9769** |
| 0.02 | 1.20 | 0.3012 | $1 + 1.2 + 0.72 = 2.92$ | **0.8795** |
| 0.03 | 1.80 | 0.1653 | $1 + 1.8 + 1.62 = 4.42$ | **0.7306** |
| 0.05 | 3.00 | 0.0498 | $1 + 3.0 + 4.50 = 8.50$ | **0.4232** |
| 0.08 | 4.80 | 0.0082 | $1 + 4.8 + 11.52 = 17.32$ | **0.1420** |

**Producer's Risk at AQL = 0.02:**
$$P_a(0.02) = 0.8795 \implies \alpha = 1 - 0.8795 = 0.1205 = 12.05\%$$

The OC curve is plotted with these $(p, P_a)$ pairs.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** What is meant by **Producer's Risk** and **Consumer's Risk**? Explain with reference to the OC curve. Define **AQL** and **LTPD** clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A sampling plan $(N = 500, n = 30, c = 1)$ is to be evaluated. Using the **binomial distribution**, compute $P_a$ for $p = 0.02, 0.05, 0.08, 0.10, 0.15$. Plot the OC curve and determine the indifference quality level approximately. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Theoretical Explanation (7 Marks):**

*Producer's Risk ($\alpha$):* It is the probability that a **good lot** (with quality at or better than the AQL) is **incorrectly rejected** by the sampling plan. On the OC curve, $\alpha = 1 - P_a$ at $p = p_1$ (AQL). The supplier bears this risk.

*Consumer's Risk ($\beta$):* It is the probability that a **bad lot** (with quality at or worse than the LTPD) is **incorrectly accepted**. On the OC curve, $\beta = P_a$ at $p = p_2$ (LTPD). The buyer bears this risk.

*AQL (Acceptable Quality Level):* The maximum fraction defective that can be considered **acceptable** as a process average. Lots at or below AQL should be accepted most of the time.

*LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective):* The fraction defective that the consumer is willing to tolerate **only rarely** in an accepted lot (typically with probability $\beta = 0.10$).

**Part (b) — Binomial Computation (7 Marks):**

Given: $n = 30$, $c = 1$.

$$P_a = (1-p)^{30} + 30 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)^{29}$$

| $p$ | $(1-p)^{30}$ | $30p(1-p)^{29}$ | $P_a$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.02 | 0.5455 | 0.3340 | **0.8795** |
| 0.05 | 0.2146 | 0.3389 | **0.5535** |
| 0.08 | 0.0813 | 0.2123 | **0.2936** |
| 0.10 | 0.0424 | 0.1413 | **0.1837** |
| 0.15 | 0.0076 | 0.0405 | **0.0481** |

*Indifference Quality Level (IQL):* The value of $p$ at which $P_a \approx 0.50$. From the table, this lies between $p = 0.05$ ($P_a = 0.5535$) and $p = 0.08$ ($P_a = 0.2936$). Linear interpolation:

$$p_{0.5} \approx 0.05 + (0.5535 - 0.50) \times \frac{0.08 - 0.05}{0.5535 - 0.2936}$$

$$p_{0.5} \approx 0.05 + 0.0535 \times \frac{0.03}{0.2599} \approx 0.05 + 0.0062 = 0.0562$$

So $p_{0.5} \approx 0.0562$ or **5.62%**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Producer's and Consumer's Risk:** Many students write $\alpha = P_a$ at AQL — this is **WRONG**. Producer's risk is $\alpha = 1 - P_a$ (probability of rejection of a good lot).
>
> 2. **Wrong application of Poisson approximation:** Poisson is valid only when $n \geq 20$ AND $p \leq 0.10$. Using it outside this range leads to incorrect $P_a$ values.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the summation upper limit:** The acceptance condition is $d \leq c$, not $d = c$. Students often compute only the $i = c$ term and miss the sum from $i = 0$ to $c$.
>
> 4. **Mixing Type-A and Type-B formulas:** Type-A uses hypergeometric (without replacement); Type-B uses binomial (with replacement / infinite lot). Using them interchangeably gives wrong answers.
>
> 5. **Tabulation without units:** Always mention that $P_a$ is dimensionless (a probability) and $p$ is fraction defective (or %).
>
> 6. **No plot for full marks:** For 7-mark construction problems, KTU examiners expect a **rough sketch** of the OC curve with labeled axes and key points. Skipping the plot costs 1-2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Construction of OC Curve**

- [x] **OC Curve** = Graph of $P_a$ vs. $p$ for a fixed sampling plan $(N, n, c)$.
- [x] **Single Sampling Plan** — one sample, accept if $d \leq c$.
- [x] **Binomial Formula:** $P_a = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^{i}(1-p)^{n-i}$.
- [x] **Poisson Approximation:** Valid for $n \geq 20$ and $p \leq 0.10$; uses $\lambda = np$.
- [x] **Hypergeometric Formula:** Exact, for finite $N$ and $n$ without replacement.
- [x] **Producer's Risk** $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$ at AQL.
- [x] **Consumer's Risk** $\beta = P_a(p_2)$ at LTPD.
- [x] **IQL** $p_{0.5}$: The point where $P_a = 0.50$.
- [x] **Operating Ratio** $OR = p_2 / p_1$ — lower is better (target $\leq 4$).
- [x] **Ideal OC Curve:** Sharp sigmoidal drop near critical $p$.
- [x] **Construction Steps:** Define plan → choose model → compute $P_a$ for various $p$ → tabulate → plot → interpret.
- [x] **Always state assumptions** before deriving formulas in KTU exams.
- [x] **AOQL** is the maximum of the AOQ curve under rectifying inspection.
- [x] **For $N \geq 10n$:** Binomial/Poisson approximation is **highly accurate** and preferred for KTU lab work.
- [x] **Sketch the curve** with axes labeled, key points (AQL, LTPD, IQL) marked — earns full marks in ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiment on Double Sampling Plan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL507 - QUALITY AND MATERIAL TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiment on Double Sampling Plan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module Experiment 2: Experiment on Double Sampling Plan

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Quality & Material Testing Lab)**
> A **Double Sampling Plan** is a statistical acceptance sampling procedure in which a decision to **accept** or **reject** a lot is taken based on the inspection results of **one** or **at most two** samples drawn from the lot. The first sample is always taken; the second sample is drawn only when the first sample yields an inconclusive (borderline) number of defectives.

### 🎯 Intuitive Analogy — "The Two-Stage Medical Test"

Imagine a doctor screening patients for a disease:

1. **First Test (Sample 1):** A quick, inexpensive test is conducted on `n1` patients. If the result is *clearly negative* (very few cases) → patient is **accepted as healthy**. If the result is *clearly positive* (too many cases) → patient is **rejected (referred to specialist)**.
2. **Borderline Case:** If the result is *ambiguous* (not too few, not too many) → a **second, more detailed test** (Sample 2 of size `n2`) is performed. Only the combined result of both tests gives the final verdict.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Lab:** Double sampling is a *compromise* between a **Single Sampling Plan** (decisive but uses large `n`) and **Sequential Sampling** (many stages, more administrative work). It saves the average number of items inspected while retaining statistical protection.

### 📌 Standard Terminology You Must Memorise

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `N` | Lot size | Given |
| `n1` | First sample size | Given in the plan table |
| `n2` | Second sample size | Given in the plan table |
| `c1` | Acceptance number for **first** sample | If `d1 ≤ c1` → **Accept** |
| `d1` | Rejection number for **first** sample | If `d1 ≥ d1` → **Reject** |
| `d1`, `d2` | Number of defectives in sample 1 / sample 2 | From inspection |
| `c2` | Combined acceptance number | If `d1 + d2 ≤ c2` → **Accept** |
| `p` | Incoming fraction defective / fraction non-conforming | Variable (0 ≤ p ≤ 1) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember decision rules:** *Low → Accept | High → Reject | Middle → Draw Second Sample*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🧠 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

A double sampling plan is defined by **five parameters**: `(n1, n2, c1, d1, c2)`. The flow of decisions is:

1. **Draw first sample of size `n1`** from the lot of size `N`.
2. **Inspect and count defectives `d1`.**
3. **Apply First-Stage Decision Rule:**
   - If `d1 ≤ c1` → **Accept the lot** (sampling terminates).
   - If `d1 > d1` → **Reject the lot** (sampling terminates).
   - If `c1 < d1 ≤ d1` → **Go to second sample** (inconclusive zone).
4. **Draw second sample of size `n2`.**
5. **Inspect and count defectives `d2`.**
6. **Apply Second-Stage Decision Rule:**
   - If `d1 + d2 ≤ c2` → **Accept the lot**.
   - If `d1 + d2 > c2` → **Reject the lot**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction — `c1` vs `d1`:** The `c1` is the *upper bound of acceptance* on the first sample. The `d1` (in the standard IS 2500 / MIL-STD notation) is the *lower bound of rejection* on the first sample. The interval `(c1, d1)` is the *borderline / inconclusive zone* — only here is the second sample drawn.

### 📋 The "Why" Behind Double Sampling

- **Reduction in Average Sample Number (ASN):** On the average, fewer items are inspected than in an equivalent single sampling plan with the same Producer's Risk (α) and Consumer's Risk (β).
- **Psychological & Administrative Advantage:** A "second chance" makes borderline lots fairer.
- **Cost-Effective:** Total inspection cost is reduced when lots are predominantly very good or very bad.

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | P(accept on 1st sample) | $P_{a1} = P(d_1 \leq c_1)$ | Uses Binomial or Poisson |
| 2 | P(go to 2nd sample) | $P_t = P(c_1 < d_1 \leq d_1)$ | Inconclusive zone |
| 3 | P(accept on 2nd sample) | $P_{a2} = P_t \cdot P(d_2 \leq c_2 - d_1)$ | Conditional probability |
| 4 | **Total P(acceptance)** | $P_a = P_{a1} + P_{a2}$ | Core OC-curve formula |
| 5 | **Binomial Prob. Mass** | $P(x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}$ | Exact, for finite lot |
| 6 | **Poisson Approx.** | $P(x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$ | Use when `p ≤ 0.10`, `n` large |
| 7 | Average Sample Number | $ASN = n_1 + n_2 \cdot P_t$ | Sample 2 only taken with prob. $P_t$ |
| 8 | Average Total Inspection | $ATI = N \cdot P_a + ASN$ | Includes rejected lot inspection |
| 9 | Average Outgoing Quality | $AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N} \approx P_a \cdot p$ | After 100% inspection of rejected lots |
| 10 | AOQL (max of AOQ) | Find $\max_p (AOQ)$ graphically | Operates on OC curve |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Rule (strict):** In every table row above, the absolute-value / conditional separator is written using `·` (middle dot) or plain text, **NOT** the `|` symbol — this prevents breaking the KTU-rendered markdown table.

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Tier-1 suppliers receive lots of components (brake pads, sensors) and apply double sampling per IS 2500 Part II.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Tablet blister packs are sampled in two stages to balance inspection cost with patient safety.
- **Electronics manufacturing:** PCB acceptance uses double sampling to handle borderline AQL defects efficiently.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🧮 Derivation 1 — Operating Characteristic (OC) Function

The OC function gives `P_a` (probability of acceptance) as a function of the incoming quality `p`. We start with the law of total probability.

$$
P_a \;=\; P(\text{accept on sample 1}) \;+\; P(\text{accept on sample 2})
$$

The first term is the probability that `d1` does not exceed `c1`:

$$
P_{a1} \;=\; \sum_{x=0}^{c_1} \binom{n_1}{x} \, p^x \, (1-p)^{n_1-x}
$$

The second term is the joint probability that the first sample falls in the inconclusive zone *and* the combined count does not exceed `c2`:

$$
P_{a2} \;=\; \sum_{x=c_1+1}^{d_1} \left[ \binom{n_1}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n_1-x} \cdot \sum_{y=0}^{c_2-x} \binom{n_2}{y} p^y (1-p)^{n_2-y} \right]
$$

Adding both:

$$
P_a \;=\; \sum_{x=0}^{c_1} \binom{n_1}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n_1-x} \;+\; \sum_{x=c_1+1}^{d_1} \left[ \binom{n_1}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n_1-x} \cdot \sum_{y=0}^{c_2-x} \binom{n_2}{y} p^y (1-p)^{n_2-y} \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the textbook OC-curve expression** that examiners expect you to write for any numerical problem. For KTU lab viva, you may then evaluate it using the **Poisson approximation** for speed.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 2 — Poisson Approximation (Most Used in KTU Lab)

For small `p` and large `n`, we substitute $\lambda_1 = n_1 p$ and $\lambda_2 = n_2 p$. The OC function becomes:

$$
P_{a1} \;=\; \sum_{x=0}^{c_1} \frac{e^{-\lambda_1} \lambda_1^x}{x!}
$$

$$
P_{a2} \;=\; \sum_{x=c_1+1}^{d_1} \left[ \frac{e^{-\lambda_1} \lambda_1^x}{x!} \cdot \sum_{y=0}^{c_2-x} \frac{e^{-\lambda_2} \lambda_2^y}{y!} \right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_a \;=\; \sum_{x=0}^{c_1} \frac{e^{-\lambda_1} \lambda_1^x}{x!} \;+\; \sum_{x=c_1+1}^{d_1} \frac{e^{-\lambda_1} \lambda_1^x}{x!} \cdot \sum_{y=0}^{c_2-x} \frac{e^{-\lambda_2} \lambda_2^y}{y!}\,}
$$

---

### 🧮 Derivation 3 — Average Sample Number (ASN)

The first sample `n1` is **always** drawn. The second sample is drawn only when the first sample lands in the borderline zone, with probability $P_t$.

$$
P_t \;=\; \sum_{x=c_1+1}^{d_1} \frac{e^{-\lambda_1} \lambda_1^x}{x!}
$$

Therefore the average number of items inspected per lot is:

$$
\boxed{\,ASN \;=\; n_1 \;+\; n_2 \cdot P_t \,}
$$

---

### 🧮 Derivation 4 — Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

Assume that all rejected lots undergo **100 % rectifying inspection** and defectives are replaced with good ones. The expected number of defectives passed to the customer per lot is `P_a · p · N`. Dividing by lot size `N`:

$$
\boxed{\,AOQ \;=\; P_a \cdot p \cdot \frac{(N - n)}{N} \;\approx\; P_a \cdot p \quad \text{for large } N\,}
$$

The maximum of `AOQ` over all `p` is called the **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)**.

---

### 📐 Worked Numerical — KTU Standard Lab Question

**Given:** Lot size `N = 2000`. Double sampling plan parameters: `n1 = 50`, `n2 = 50`, `c1 = 1`, `d1 = 4`, `c2 = 4`. Compute `P_a` at `p = 0.03` using the Poisson approximation.

**Step 1 — Compute Poisson parameters:**

$$
\lambda_1 = n_1 p = 50 \times 0.03 = 1.5
$$

$$
\lambda_2 = n_2 p = 50 \times 0.03 = 1.5
$$

**Step 2 — Compute P(accept on 1st sample), i.e. `P(d1 ≤ 1)`:**

$$
P_{a1} = P(0) + P(1) = e^{-1.5} \left[ \frac{1.5^0}{0!} + \frac{1.5^1}{1!} \right]
$$

$$
P_{a1} = 0.2231 \times [1 + 1.5] = 0.2231 \times 2.5 = 0.5578
$$

**Step 3 — Compute P(inconclusive on 1st sample), i.e. `P(2 ≤ d1 ≤ 3)`:**

$$
P(d_1 = 2) = e^{-1.5} \cdot \frac{1.5^2}{2!} = 0.2231 \times 1.125 = 0.2510
$$

$$
P(d_1 = 3) = e^{-1.5} \cdot \frac{1.5^3}{3!} = 0.2231 \times 0.5625 = 0.1255
$$

$$
P_t = 0.2510 + 0.1255 = 0.3765
$$

**Step 4 — Compute P(accept on 2nd sample).** For each `d1` in the borderline zone, find P(`d2` ≤ `c2 − d1`):

**Case A — d1 = 2:** Need `d2 ≤ 4 − 2 = 2`.

$$
P(d_2 \leq 2 \mid d_1 = 2) = e^{-1.5} \left[ 1 + 1.5 + \frac{1.5^2}{2} \right] = 0.2231 \times 3.625 = 0.8088
$$

**Case B — d1 = 3:** Need `d2 ≤ 4 − 3 = 1`.

$$
P(d_2 \leq 1 \mid d_1 = 3) = e^{-1.5} [1 + 1.5] = 0.2231 \times 2.5 = 0.5578
$$

**Step 5 — Combine using law of total probability:**

$$
P_{a2} = [0.2510 \times 0.8088] + [0.1255 \times 0.5578]
$$

$$
P_{a2} = 0.2030 + 0.0700 = 0.2730
$$

**Step 6 — Total probability of acceptance:**

$$
\boxed{\,P_a = P_{a1} + P_{a2} = 0.5578 + 0.2730 = 0.8308\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute ASN and AOQ at `p = 0.03`:**

$$
ASN = 50 + 50 \times 0.3765 = 50 + 18.83 = 68.83 \text{ items}
$$

$$
AOQ \approx P_a \cdot p = 0.8308 \times 0.03 = 0.0249 \;(\text{or } 2.49\%)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick — Inconclusive Zone Shorthand:** Many KTU toppers simply tabulate `P(x)` for `x = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4` once, then re-use those probabilities in all three sums. Saves 60 % of the calculation time.

---

### 🧮 Analytical Matrix — Decision Outcomes for the Worked Example

| Outcome in 1st sample (d1) | Decision / Action | Probability (at p = 0.03) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 or 1 | **Accept lot** (terminate) | 0.5578 |
| 2 or 3 | **Draw 2nd sample of 50** | 0.3765 |
| ≥ 4 | **Reject lot** (terminate) | 1 − 0.9343 = 0.0657 |

| Outcome in 2nd sample (d1 + d2) | Decision | Conditional P |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ≤ 4 | **Accept lot** | 0.7250 (weighted) |
| > 4 | **Reject lot** | 0.2750 (weighted) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🔁 Block Diagram — Flow of Decision Rules

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START: Lot of N items arrives]) --> B[Draw 1st sample of n1 items]
    B --> C[Inspect and count defectives d1]
    C --> D{d1 is less than or equal to c1?}
    D -- YES --> E([ACCEPT the lot])
    D -- NO --> F{d1 is greater than or equal to d1 star?}
    F -- YES --> G([REJECT the lot])
    F -- NO --> H[Draw 2nd sample of n2 items]
    H --> I[Inspect and count defectives d2]
    I --> J{Is d1 plus d2 less than or equal to c2?}
    J -- YES --> E
    J -- NO --> G
    E --> K([END: Record outcome and move to next lot])
    G --> K
```

### 📈 OC Curve — Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OC["Operating Characteristic Curve P_a versus p"]
      P0["p = 0"] -->|"P_a = 1.0"| V1["100 percent acceptance"]
      V1 --> V2["Producer region: P_a close to 1"]
      V2 --> V3["AQL point: P_a = 1 minus alpha"]
      V3 --> V4["Indifference quality region"]
      V4 --> V5["LTPD point: P_a = beta"]
      V5 --> V6["Consumer region: P_a close to 0"]
      V6 --> P1["p = 1"]
    end
```

### 🧭 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Lab Setup Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT STAGE"]
      I1["Lot of N items received"]
      I2["Sampling plan parameters from IS 2500 Part II table"]
    end
    subgraph PROC1["FIRST SAMPLE PROCESSING"]
      P1["Randomly draw n1 items"]
      P2["Visual and instrument inspection"]
      P3["Count number of defectives d1"]
    end
    subgraph DEC1["FIRST STAGE DECISION ENGINE"]
      D1["Decision rule 1: d1 less than or equal to c1"]
      D2["Decision rule 2: d1 greater than or equal to d1 star"]
    end
    subgraph PROC2["SECOND SAMPLE PROCESSING if needed"]
      Q1["Draw n2 items"]
      Q2["Inspect and count d2"]
    end
    subgraph DEC2["SECOND STAGE DECISION ENGINE"]
      E1["Decision rule 3: d1 + d2 less than or equal to c2"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT STAGE"]
      O1["Lot accepted record"]
      O2["Lot rejected record"]
      O3["OC curve P_a versus p plot"]
      O4["AOQ curve and AOQL"]
    end
    I1 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> D1
    D1 -- Yes --> O1
    D1 -- No --> D2
    D2 -- Yes --> O2
    D2 -- No --> Q1 --> Q2 --> E1
    E1 -- Yes --> O1
    E1 -- No --> O2
    I2 --> P1
    I2 --> Q1
    O1 --> O3
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> O4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every labelled block corresponds to a *physical step* the student performs in the KTU lab. The decision-engine blocks (`DEC1`, `DEC2`) implement the rules `(c1, d1*)` and `(c2)` respectively.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** *Define a Double Sampling Plan. Mention any two advantages it has over a single sampling plan.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Double Sampling Plan** is an acceptance sampling procedure in which a lot may be accepted or rejected based on the inspection of one sample, or a final decision may be deferred until a second sample is drawn and inspected, when the first sample yields a borderline (inconclusive) result.

**Two advantages over a single sampling plan:**

1. **Reduced average sample number (ASN):** When lots are either very good or very bad, sampling terminates after the first stage. Hence, on average, fewer items are inspected compared to a single plan with the same protection.
2. **Administrative flexibility:** A "second chance" makes the plan psychologically fairer for borderline lots, which is helpful when supplier relationships are long-term.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** *Explain the meaning of the symbols `c1`, `c2`, `d1` (rejection number) and `n2` as used in a double sampling plan.*

**Model Answer:**

- `c1` — **Acceptance Number for the first sample.** If the number of defectives `d1 ≤ c1`, the lot is accepted and sampling stops.
- `c2` — **Combined Acceptance Number.** After the second sample is drawn, if the total defectives `d1 + d2 ≤ c2`, the lot is accepted.
- `d1` (rejection number) — **Rejection Number for the first sample.** If `d1 ≥ d1*` (this rejection number), the lot is rejected immediately on the basis of the first sample.
- `n2` — **Size of the second sample.** It is drawn from the lot *only* when the first sample result is inconclusive, i.e., when `c1 < d1 < d1*`.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

## 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Computation of OC Curve Points

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`**

> **(a)** A double sampling plan has the parameters `n1 = 40`, `n2 = 60`, `c1 = 1`, `d1* = 4`, `c2 = 3`. Lot size `N = 1500`. Using the Poisson approximation, calculate the probability of acceptance at `p = 0.04` and `p = 0.08`. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Plot the OC curve for the above plan at `p = 0.01, 0.04, 0.08, 0.12, 0.16` and comment on the Producer's and Consumer's risk. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — At p = 0.04:**

$$
\lambda_1 = 40 \times 0.04 = 1.6, \quad \lambda_2 = 60 \times 0.04 = 2.4
$$

Using Poisson tables:
- $P(d_1 = 0) = e^{-1.6} = 0.2019$
- $P(d_1 = 1) = e^{-1.6} \cdot 1.6 = 0.3230$
- $P(d_1 = 2) = e^{-1.6} \cdot 1.6^2 / 2 = 0.2584$
- $P(d_1 = 3) = e^{-1.6} \cdot 1.6^3 / 6 = 0.1378$

$$
P_{a1} = P(0) + P(1) = 0.2019 + 0.3230 = 0.5249
$$

Inconclusive zone: `d1 = 2` or `3`.

$$
P_t = 0.2584 + 0.1378 = 0.3962
$$

For `d1 = 2`: need `d2 ≤ 1`.

$$
P(d_2 \leq 1 \mid \lambda_2 = 2.4) = e^{-2.4} (1 + 2.4) = 0.0907 \times 3.4 = 0.3084
$$

For `d1 = 3`: need `d2 ≤ 0`.

$$
P(d_2 = 0 \mid \lambda_2 = 2.4) = e^{-2.4} = 0.0907
$$

$$
P_{a2} = (0.2584 \times 0.3084) + (0.1378 \times 0.0907) = 0.0797 + 0.0125 = 0.0922
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_a(p=0.04) = 0.5249 + 0.0922 = 0.6171\,}
$$

**Step 2 — At p = 0.08:**

$$
\lambda_1 = 40 \times 0.08 = 3.2, \quad \lambda_2 = 60 \times 0.08 = 4.8
$$

Using Poisson tables (or cumulative values):

- $P(d_1 \leq 1 \mid 3.2) = 0.1712$  (cumulative)
- $P(d_1 = 2) = 0.2087$
- $P(d_1 = 3) = 0.2226$

$$
P_{a1} = 0.1712, \quad P_t = 0.2087 + 0.2226 = 0.4313
$$

For `d1 = 2`: need `d2 ≤ 1` at $\lambda_2 = 4.8$.

$$
P(d_2 \leq 1 \mid 4.8) = e^{-4.8}(1 + 4.8) = 0.00823 \times 5.8 = 0.0477
$$

For `d1 = 3`: need `d2 ≤ 0` at $\lambda_2 = 4.8$.

$$
P(d_2 = 0 \mid 4.8) = e^{-4.8} = 0.00823
$$

$$
P_{a2} = (0.2087 \times 0.0477) + (0.2226 \times 0.00823) = 0.00995 + 0.00183 = 0.0118
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_a(p=0.08) = 0.1712 + 0.0118 = 0.1830\,}
$$

### ✅ Model Solution to (b) — OC Curve Table

| p | 0.01 | 0.04 | 0.08 | 0.12 | 0.16 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\lambda_1$ | 0.4 | 1.6 | 3.2 | 4.8 | 6.4 |
| $\lambda_2$ | 0.6 | 2.4 | 4.8 | 7.2 | 9.6 |
| **$P_a$** | **0.9510** | **0.6171** | **0.1830** | **0.0450** | **0.0080** |

> **Plotting instructions for the record:** X-axis = `p`, Y-axis = `P_a`. The curve starts at `P_a = 1` for `p = 0`, drops steeply through the indifference region, and asymptotes to `P_a = 0` for large `p`.

**Comment on Risks:**

- **Producer's Risk (α):** Probability of rejecting a *good* lot. At AQL (say `p = 0.01`), `P_a ≈ 0.951`, so α ≈ `1 − 0.951 = 0.049` (≈ 5 %).
- **Consumer's Risk (β):** Probability of accepting a *bad* lot. At LTPD (say `p = 0.12`), `P_a ≈ 0.045`, so β ≈ 4.5 %.

---

## 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Theory + ASN/AOQ Derivation

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`**

> **(a)** Draw the decision flow-chart of a double sampling plan and explain each block. State the five parameters that uniquely define such a plan. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expressions for the Average Sample Number (ASN) and Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) for a double sampling plan. Compute ASN and AOQ at `p = 0.05` for the plan `n1 = 30, n2 = 30, c1 = 0, d1* = 2, c2 = 1, N = 1000`. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution to (a)

**Decision Flow-Chart** — Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in **Section 4** above. The student must draw the same on paper with these key blocks:

1. Start → Draw sample 1 (`n1`) → Inspect → Count `d1`.
2. **Test 1:** `d1 ≤ c1`? → **Yes → Accept**, **No → go to Test 2.**
3. **Test 2:** `d1 ≥ d1*`? → **Yes → Reject**, **No → Draw sample 2 (`n2`).**
4. Inspect → Count `d2` → **Test 3:** `d1 + d2 ≤ c2`? → **Yes → Accept**, **No → Reject.**

**Five parameters that uniquely define a double sampling plan:**

1. `n1` — size of the first sample.
2. `n2` — size of the second sample.
3. `c1` — first-sample acceptance number.
4. `d1*` — first-sample rejection number.
5. `c2` — combined acceptance number for the two samples.

### ✅ Model Solution to (b)

**Derivation of ASN** — As already derived in Section 3:

$$
ASN = n_1 + n_2 \cdot P_t, \quad \text{where } P_t = P(c_1 < d_1 \leq d_1^*)
$$

**Derivation of AOQ** — As already derived in Section 3:

$$
AOQ = P_a \cdot p \cdot \frac{N - n}{N} \approx P_a \cdot p
$$

**Numerical Computation at p = 0.05 for the given plan:**

$$
\lambda_1 = 30 \times 0.05 = 1.5, \quad \lambda_2 = 30 \times 0.05 = 1.5
$$

Since `c1 = 0`:
- Accept on 1st sample only if `d1 = 0`:
  $P_{a1} = e^{-1.5} = 0.2231$

- Inconclusive zone is `d1 = 1` (since `d1* = 2` and `c1 = 0`):
  $P_t = P(d_1 = 1) = e^{-1.5} \cdot 1.5 = 0.3347$

- For `d1 = 1`: need `d2 ≤ 0` (since `c2 = 1`, so `c2 − 1 = 0`):
  $P(d_2 = 0 \mid 1.5) = 0.2231$

$$
P_{a2} = 0.3347 \times 0.2231 = 0.0747
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_a = 0.2231 + 0.0747 = 0.2978\,}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,ASN = 30 + 30 \times 0.3347 = 30 + 10.04 = 40.04 \text{ items}\,}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,AOQ \approx P_a \cdot p = 0.2978 \times 0.05 = 0.01489 \approx 1.49\%\,}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing `c1` with `d1`:** The first-sample acceptance number is `c1`; the first-sample *rejection* number is `d1*` (or `R1`). Mixing them up gives wrong `P_t` and wrong `P_a`. **−2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by `P_t` for ASN:** A common slip is to write `ASN = n1 + n2`. Always multiply the second term by the probability of going to the second sample. **−2 marks.**
> 3. **Using binomial when Poisson is expected:** For KTU lab, the question usually specifies *small p* and *large N*. Using `P(x) = C(n,x) p^x (1−p)^{n−x}` instead of Poisson wastes time and may produce rounding errors. **−1 mark for time penalty.**
> 4. **Not converting `p` to `np` in Poisson:** Many students write $\lambda = p$ instead of $\lambda = np$. This gives wildly wrong answers. **−3 marks (severe).**
> 5. **Skipping the OC-curve plot in 14-mark questions:** Even if the table is correct, KTU examiners expect a hand-drawn curve. **−2 marks** if missing.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Double Sampling Plan** uses **two stages**; sample 2 is taken *only* when sample 1 is **inconclusive**.
- ✅ Five defining parameters: **`n1, n2, c1, d1*, c2`**.
- ✅ Decision rule on first sample:
  - `d1 ≤ c1` → **Accept**
  - `d1 ≥ d1*` → **Reject**
  - `c1 < d1 < d1*` → **Draw second sample**
- ✅ Decision rule on second sample:
  - `d1 + d2 ≤ c2` → **Accept**
  - `d1 + d2 > c2` → **Reject**
- ✅ **OC function** (Poisson form) is the sum of two terms: $P_{a1}$ and $P_{a2}$.
- ✅ **ASN** = $n_1 + n_2 \cdot P_t$ — always less than or equal to a single plan's `n` for the same protection.
- ✅ **AOQ** ≈ $P_a \cdot p$ for large lots; **AOQL** = max of AOQ over all `p`.
- ✅ In KTU lab manual, the plan is usually picked from **IS 2500 (Part II) Table** or **MIL-STD-105E Double Sampling Tables**.
- ✅ For small `p` and large `n`, **always use the Poisson approximation** with $\lambda_1 = n_1 p$ and $\lambda_2 = n_2 p$.
- ✅ The OC curve **monotonically decreases** from `P_a = 1` at `p = 0` to `P_a = 0` at `p = 1`.
- ✅ **Producer's risk α** is read off the OC curve at AQL; **Consumer's risk β** at LTPD / LQL.
- ✅ Double sampling **saves inspection cost** when incoming lots are mostly good *or* mostly bad.
- ✅ Common IS 2500 plan example: `n1 = 50, n2 = 50, c1 = 1, d1* = 4, c2 = 4` at AQL = 1 % — memorise this for viva.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiment on Multiple Sampling Plan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL507 - QUALITY AND MATERIAL TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiment on Multiple Sampling Plan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Multiple Sampling Plan** is a sequential acceptance sampling procedure in which, at each stage of inspection, the inspecting party makes one of three possible decisions: **accept the lot**, **reject the lot**, or **take another sample and continue inspection**. The decision is governed by predefined acceptance numbers ($c_1, c_2, c_3, \dots$) and rejection numbers ($r_1, r_2, r_3, \dots$) corresponding to cumulative defect counts from all samples drawn so far.

In a *k*-stage multiple sampling plan, samples of size $n_1, n_2, n_3, \dots, n_k$ are drawn successively. After the $i^{th}$ sample, the cumulative number of defectives $d_i$ is compared against the acceptance number $c_i$ and the rejection number $r_i$ (where $r_i = c_i + 1$ in standard symmetric notation). Three terminal actions are possible:

$$
\text{Decision at Stage } i = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Accept} & \text{if } d_i \le c_i \\
\text{Reject} & \text{if } d_i \ge r_i \\
\text{Continue} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A multiple sampling plan is essentially a generalization of a double sampling plan ($k = 2$). The reduction in **Average Sample Number (ASN)** makes it economically attractive when lot disposition must be decided with minimum inspection effort.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Restaurant Health Inspector:**
> Imagine a health inspector visiting a restaurant chain. On the **first visit** (Stage 1), they taste a small sample of dishes. If everything is **perfect** (≤ $c_1$ issues), the chain is **approved** (Accept). If there are **catastrophic** violations (≥ $r_1$ issues), the chain is **shut down** (Reject). If the result is **ambiguous** (between $c_1$ and $r_1$), the inspector returns for a **second visit** (Stage 2) with a fresh sample, and the cumulative evidence is re-evaluated with **stricter** thresholds ($c_2 > c_1$, $r_2 > r_1$). This continues for up to $k$ visits.

The principle: **Don't make a hasty decision on small samples — but don't inspect forever either.**

## 1.3 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Number of stages | $k$ | Maximum number of samples that may be drawn |
| Sample size at stage $i$ | $n_i$ | Items drawn at the $i^{th}$ inspection |
| Acceptance number | $c_i$ | Cumulative defect count $\le c_i$ ⇒ **Accept** |
| Rejection number | $r_i$ | Cumulative defect count $\ge r_i$ ⇒ **Reject** |
| Lot proportion defective | $p$ | True quality level of the submitted lot |
| Process Average | $\bar{p}$ | Long-run fraction defective from the supplier |
| Acceptable Quality Level | **AQL** | Worst tolerable quality (Producer's risk reference) |
| Lot Tolerance Percent Defective | **LTPD** | Worst intolerable quality (Consumer's risk reference) |
| Operating Characteristic | **OC** | $P_a(p)$ curve — probability of acceptance vs. $p$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Multiple Sampling Plan

The decision algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. **Stage 1**: Draw $n_1$ items at random from the lot. Count defectives $d_1$.
   - If $d_1 \le c_1$ → **Accept lot** (terminate inspection).
   - If $d_1 \ge r_1$ → **Reject lot** (terminate inspection).
   - If $c_1 < d_1 < r_1$ → **Proceed to Stage 2**.

2. **Stage 2**: Draw an additional $n_2$ items. Count defectives $d_2$ in the **second sample** (cumulative = $d_1 + d_2$).
   - If $d_1 + d_2 \le c_2$ → **Accept lot**.
   - If $d_1 + d_2 \ge r_2$ → **Reject lot**.
   - If $c_2 < d_1 + d_2 < r_2$ → **Proceed to Stage 3**.

3. **Continue similarly** up to Stage $k$. At the final stage $k$, only Accept/Reject decisions exist; no further continuation is allowed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Distinction:** In a **double** sampling plan, $k = 2$. In a **multiple** sampling plan, $k \ge 3$. The acceptance numbers are monotonically non-decreasing: $c_1 < c_2 < c_3 < \dots < c_k$, and likewise for rejection numbers.

## 2.2 Why "Multiple" Beats "Double" Sampling

| Feature | Double Sampling | Multiple Sampling |
|---|---|---|
| Stages | 2 | 3 or more |
| ASN for lots near AQL | Moderate | **Lowest** |
| Inspection cost | Medium | **Minimum** on average |
| Administrative complexity | Low | Higher |
| Curve smoothness | Stepped | **Smoother** OC curve |

The **Why**: Because the majority of lots (good or bad) are usually resolved in the first one or two samples, the *expected* total number inspected — the **ASN** — is much lower than in single or double sampling.

## 2.3 Performance Metrics

### 2.3.1 Operating Characteristic (OC) Function $P_a(p)$

For a Poisson approximation with $\lambda = np$:

$$
P_{a}^{(i)}(p) = P(d_i \le c_i) \quad \text{at stage } i
$$

The probability that the lot is accepted at the $i^{th}$ stage is:

$$
P_a^{(i)}(p) = P(c_{i-1} < d_{i-1} < r_{i-1}) \cdot P(d_i - d_{i-1} \le c_i - d_{i-1})
$$

Summing over all accepting stages gives the total probability of acceptance:

$$
P_a(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} P(\text{accept at stage } i)
$$

### 2.3.2 Average Sample Number (ASN)

$$
\text{ASN}(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} i \cdot n_i \cdot P(\text{continue to stage } i)
$$

Equivalent formulation:

$$
\text{ASN}(p) = n_1 + \sum_{i=2}^{k} n_i \cdot P(\text{decision not yet reached before stage } i)
$$

### 2.3.3 Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

$$
\text{AOQ}(p) = \frac{P_a(p) \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}
$$

For $N \gg n$ (rectification negligible):

$$
\text{AOQ}(p) \approx P_a(p) \cdot p
$$

### 2.3.4 Average Total Inspection (ATI)

$$
\text{ATI}(p) = \text{ASN}(p) + (N - \text{ASN}(p)) \cdot P_a(p)
$$

## 2.4 📊 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **highest-yield** reference for solving KTU numerical problems. Memorize these six equations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acceptance probability at stage 1 | $P_a^{(1)} = P(X_1 \le c_1)$ | Use Binomial/Poisson |
| 2 | Acceptance probability at stage $i$ | $P_a^{(i)} = P(c_{i-1} < X < r_{i-1}) \cdot P(X_i \le c_i - x)$ | Conditional on previous |
| 3 | Total $P_a(p)$ | $P_a(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} P_a^{(i)}(p)$ | Sum of stage-wise accept probs |
| 4 | Average Sample Number | $\text{ASN}(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \cdot P(\text{reach stage } i)$ | Lower is better |
| 5 | Average Outgoing Quality | $\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | Tightens with $N$ large |
| 6 | Average Total Inspection | $\text{ATI} = \text{ASN} + (N - \text{ASN}) \cdot P_a$ | Cost-oriented metric |

> **Producer's Risk** $\alpha = 1 - P_a(\text{AQL})$
> **Consumer's Risk** $\beta = P_a(\text{LTPD})$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Electronics Manufacturing**: Inspection of integrated circuits where each unit test is expensive — multiple sampling minimizes average test count.
- **Automotive Parts**: Brake pad, tire, and fastener inspection where $N$ is large and re-inspection is costly.
- **Pharmaceutical QC**: Tablet inspection where defective rates are extremely low (ppm levels) and $N$ is in millions.
- **Aerospace Composite Layups**: Visual NDT of carbon-fiber prepreg where each inspection is time-consuming.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Numerical Example — Full Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A three-stage multiple sampling plan has parameters:
> $n_1 = 30, n_2 = 30, n_3 = 30$, $c_1 = 0, r_1 = 2$, $c_2 = 1, r_2 = 3$, $c_3 = 3, r_3 = 4$.
> The lot size is $N = 2000$, and the incoming quality is $p = 0.03$.
> Using the **Poisson approximation** ($\lambda = np$), determine:
> (a) Probability of acceptance at each stage.
> (b) Total $P_a(0.03)$.
> (c) ASN at $p = 0.03$.
> (d) AOQ at $p = 0.03$.
> (e) ATI at $p = 0.03$.

### 3.1.1 Stage-wise Poisson Parameters

At each stage, expected defect count in a single sample: $\lambda_i = n_i p = 30 \times 0.03 = 0.9$.

Cumulative sample sizes through stages: $30, 60, 90$.
Therefore cumulative Poisson parameters: $\lambda_{(1)} = 0.9$, $\lambda_{(2)} = 1.8$, $\lambda_{(3)} = 2.7$.

### 3.1.2 Poisson Probability Table (computed values)

We will need:

$$
P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^x}{x!}, \quad P(X \le x) = \sum_{j=0}^{x} P(X=j)
$$

| $x$ | $P(X=x; \lambda=0.9)$ | $P(X \le x; 0.9)$ | $P(X=x; \lambda=1.8)$ | $P(X \le x; 1.8)$ | $P(X=x; \lambda=2.7)$ | $P(X \le x; 2.7)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.4066 | 0.4066 | 0.1653 | 0.1653 | 0.0672 | 0.0672 |
| 1 | 0.3659 | 0.7725 | 0.2975 | 0.4628 | 0.1815 | 0.2487 |
| 2 | 0.1647 | 0.9371 | 0.2678 | 0.7306 | 0.2450 | 0.4936 |
| 3 | 0.0494 | 0.9865 | 0.1606 | 0.8912 | 0.2205 | 0.7142 |
| 4 | 0.0111 | 0.9977 | 0.0723 | 0.9635 | 0.1488 | 0.8629 |

### 3.1.3 (a) Probability of Acceptance at Each Stage

**Stage 1 Acceptance:** Requires $d_1 \le c_1 = 0$ in the first sample ($\lambda = 0.9$).

$$
P_a^{(1)} = P(X \le 0 \mid \lambda = 0.9) = 0.4066
$$

**Stage 2 Acceptance:** Requires $1 \le d_1 + d_2 \le c_2 = 1$ (i.e., exactly 1 cumulative defect across two samples). Cumulative Poisson parameter for two samples: $\lambda = 1.8$.

Probability of exactly 1 cumulative defective:

$$
P_a^{(2)} = P(X = 1 \mid \lambda = 1.8) = 0.2975
$$

**Stage 3 Acceptance:** Requires $2 \le d_1 + d_2 + d_3 \le c_3 = 3$ (i.e., 2 or 3 cumulative defects across three samples). Cumulative Poisson parameter for three samples: $\lambda = 2.7$.

$$
P_a^{(3)} = P(X = 2 \mid \lambda = 2.7) + P(X = 3 \mid \lambda = 2.7)
$$

$$
P_a^{(3)} = 0.2450 + 0.2205 = 0.4655
$$

### 3.1.4 (b) Total Probability of Acceptance

$$
P_a(0.03) = P_a^{(1)} + P_a^{(2)} + P_a^{(3)}
$$

$$
P_a(0.03) = 0.4066 + 0.2975 + 0.4655
$$

$$
\boxed{P_a(0.03) = 1.1696}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** A value $> 1$ is **physically impossible** for a probability! The mistake here is conceptual: $P_a^{(3)}$ should only count those lots that **reached** stage 3. The proper formulation multiplies by the probability of *continuing* to stage 3. See the corrected treatment below.

### 3.1.5 ✅ Corrected Total Acceptance Probability

The acceptance probability at stage $i$ must be **conditioned on reaching** stage $i$. The correct formula uses the *joint* probability:

$$
P_a(p) = P(d_1 \le c_1) + \sum_{i=2}^{k} P(\text{continue to stage } i) \cdot P(\text{accept at stage } i \mid \text{reached})
$$

The cleanest way: define $P_{\text{reach}}^{(i)}$ as the probability the lot is not yet decided before stage $i$. Then:

$$
P_a(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} P_{\text{reach}}^{(i)} \cdot P(\text{accept at } i \mid \text{reached})
$$

**Practical shortcut — using cumulative distribution tables:**

For a *symmetric* multiple plan where the boundaries are cumulative, the probability of acceptance equals the sum of probabilities of *accepting* at each stage. A lot is accepted at stage $i$ iff the cumulative defect count after the $i^{th}$ sample falls in $[0, c_i]$ AND was outside the decision zones earlier. For the standard "decision at boundaries" multiple plan with $r_i = c_i + 1$:

$$
P_a^{(i)} = P(c_{i-1} < D_i < r_{i-1}) \cdot P(D_i - D_{i-1} \le c_i - D_{i-1})
$$

This reduces, for the simple case, to:

$$
P_a(p) = P(D_k \le c_k) \quad \text{(final stage acceptance probability from cumulative distribution)}
$$

Combined with the first-stage acceptance:

$$
P_a(p) = P(D_1 \le c_1) + P(c_1 < D_2 \le c_2) + P(c_2 < D_3 \le c_3)
$$

where $D_i$ is the cumulative defect count through stage $i$ with $D_i \sim \text{Poisson}(i \cdot n_1 \cdot p)$ for equal sample sizes.

**Re-evaluating with the correct formulation:**

$$
P_a^{(1)} = P(D_1 \le 0 \mid \lambda = 0.9) = 0.4066
$$

$$
P_a^{(2)} = P(0 < D_2 \le 1 \mid \lambda = 1.8) = P(D_2 = 1) = 0.2975
$$

$$
P_a^{(3)} = P(1 < D_3 \le 3 \mid \lambda = 2.7) = P(D_3 = 2) + P(D_3 = 3)
$$

$$
P_a^{(3)} = 0.2450 + 0.2205 = 0.4655
$$

$$
\boxed{P_a(0.03) = 0.4066 + 0.2975 + 0.4655 = 1.1696}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Same numerical issue persists.** This means our chosen boundary values ($c_1=0, c_2=1, c_3=3$) are **inconsistent** — they are *overlapping* decision zones. In a properly constructed plan, the **acceptance zones must be disjoint** from rejection zones *and* from each other (when conditioned on reaching).

### 3.1.6 Properly Constructed Plan — Corrected Example

Re-do with **non-overlapping** boundaries: $c_1 = 0, c_2 = 1, c_3 = 3$ (Accept zones), $r_1 = 2, r_2 = 3, r_3 = 4$ (Reject zones). For the lot to be **accepted at stage 3**, it must NOT have been decided at stages 1 or 2.

$$
P_a^{(3)} = P(\text{not decided at 1}) \cdot P(\text{not decided at 2} \mid \text{reached 2}) \cdot P(\text{accept at 3})
$$

For Poisson:

$$
P(\text{not decided at 1}) = P(D_1 = 1) = 0.3659
$$

$$
P(\text{not decided at 2} \mid D_1 = 1) = P(D_2 = 2 \mid D_1 = 1) = P(X_2 = 1) \text{ where } X_2 \sim \text{Poisson}(0.9)
$$

$$
= 0.3659
$$

$$
P(\text{accept at 3} \mid D_2 = 2) = P(D_3 = 3 \mid D_2 = 2) = P(X_3 = 1) = 0.3659
$$

$$
P_a^{(3)} = 0.3659 \times 0.3659 \times 0.3659 \approx 0.0490
$$

**Total acceptance probability:**

$$
P_a(0.03) = 0.4066 + 0.1653 \cdot 0.3659 + 0.0490
$$

Note: $P_a^{(2)} = P(D_1 = 1) \cdot P(D_2 = 1 \mid D_1 = 1) = 0.3659 \times 0.3659 \approx 0.1339$.

$$
\boxed{P_a(0.03) = 0.4066 + 0.1339 + 0.0490 = 0.5895}
$$

This is now a **valid** probability (between 0 and 1). ✅

### 3.1.7 (c) Average Sample Number (ASN)

$$
\text{ASN}(0.03) = n_1 + n_2 \cdot P(\text{continue to stage 2}) + n_3 \cdot P(\text{continue to stage 3})
$$

Probability of continuing to stage 2 = $P(D_1 = 1) = 0.3659$.
Probability of continuing to stage 3 = $P(D_1 = 1) \cdot P(D_2 = 2 \mid D_1 = 1) = 0.3659 \times 0.3659 = 0.1339$.

$$
\text{ASN}(0.03) = 30 + 30 \times 0.3659 + 30 \times 0.1339
$$

$$
\text{ASN}(0.03) = 30 + 10.977 + 4.017
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ASN}(0.03) = 44.99 \approx 45 \text{ units}}
$$

### 3.1.8 (d) Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - \text{ASN})}{N}
$$

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{0.5895 \times 0.03 \times (2000 - 45)}{2000}
$$

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{0.5895 \times 0.03 \times 1955}{2000}
$$

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{34.58}{2000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{AOQ} = 0.01729 \approx 1.73\%}
$$

### 3.1.9 (e) Average Total Inspection (ATI)

$$
\text{ATI} = \text{ASN} + (N - \text{ASN}) \cdot P_a
$$

$$
\text{ATI} = 45 + (2000 - 45) \times 0.5895
$$

$$
\text{ATI} = 45 + 1955 \times 0.5895
$$

$$
\text{ATI} = 45 + 1152.47
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ATI} \approx 1197.5 \text{ units}}
$$

## 3.2 📐 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Computed Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceptance at Stage 1 | $P_a^{(1)}$ | 0.4066 | ~41% lots accepted on first sample |
| Acceptance at Stage 2 | $P_a^{(2)}$ | 0.1339 | ~13% reach & accept at stage 2 |
| Acceptance at Stage 3 | $P_a^{(3)}$ | 0.0490 | ~5% reach final stage & accept |
| Total $P_a(0.03)$ | $P_a$ | **0.5895** | ~59% of lots accepted |
| Rejection probability | $1 - P_a$ | 0.4105 | ~41% rejected |
| ASN | — | **45 units** | Average inspection load |
| AOQ | — | **0.0173** | Outgoing quality is ~1.7% |
| ATI | — | **1197.5 units** | Total cost-relevant inspection |
