```mermaid
    F --> G[Lower Platen]
    B --> H[Tensile Failure along vertical diametral plane]
    H --> I[Reading Pmax on gauge]
    I --> J[Apply Formula fst = 2P divided by pi L D]
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix — Acceptance vs Rejection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[28-day Cube Result fc] --> B{fc greater than or equal to fck plus 1.65 sigma}
    B -- YES --> C[BATCH ACCEPTED]
    B -- NO --> D{Any individual cube below fck minus 4 MPa}
    D -- YES --> E[BATCH REJECTED]
    D -- NO --> F[Conditional: Increase QC, Retest 3 cores]
    F --> G{Retest passes}
    G -- YES --> C
    G -- NO --> E
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The flow above mirrors **IS 456: 2000, Clause 16.1** conformity criteria and is a **favourite 14-mark KTU theory question** in lab-based viva and written exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Performance Test on Finished Products" in the context of a Quality and Material Testing Laboratory. List any two Indian Standard codes relevant to this test.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Performance Test on Finished Products** is the laboratory evaluation of the hardened, dimensionally-stable engineering properties (compressive, tensile, flexural, durability) of fully cured construction units such as concrete cubes, cylinders, beams, bricks, and paver blocks to verify conformity with design specifications.

Relevant IS codes (any two):

1. **IS 516 (Part 1/Sec 1): 2018** — Methods of tests for strength of concrete.
2. **IS 5816: 1999** — Splitting tensile strength of concrete.
3. **IS 1077: 1992** — Common burnt clay building bricks.
4. **IS 15658: 2006** — Precast concrete paving blocks.

*(Distribution: 2 marks definition + 1 mark codes)*

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the formula and IS code reference for the Split Cylinder Tensile Strength test of concrete.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Formula:

$$
f_{st} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi \times L \times D}
$$

Where:
- $P$ = maximum compressive line load at failure (N)
- $L$ = length of cylinder (mm)
- $D$ = diameter of cylinder (mm)

**IS Code Reference:** **IS 5816: 1999 — Method of Test for Splitting Tensile Strength of Concrete.**

*(Distribution: 2 marks formula + 1 mark IS code)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length ESE Module Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In numerical questions, students **consistently lose 2–3 marks** by:
> 1. Mixing up units (kN vs N; cm² vs mm²).
> 2. Forgetting the conversion factor (1 N/mm² = 1 MPa).
> 3. Citing the **wrong IS code section** (e.g., saying "IS 516" when the question explicitly tests splitting — use IS 5816).
> 4. Not reporting the result to **2 decimal places** with **units**.
> 5. Skipping the **acceptance/rejection conclusion** — without it, the answer is *incomplete for full marks*.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain, with neat sketch, the **standard procedure for determining the compressive strength of hardened concrete** as per IS 516. Mention the size of specimen, rate of loading, capping details, and reporting format. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A concrete cube of size **150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm** failed at a load of **725 kN** in a 2000 kN CTM. Calculate the compressive strength. Also state, with justification, whether this cube **individually qualifies** for an **M30 grade** design mix (assumed standard deviation $\sigma = 4.5$ MPa). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Procedure for Compressive Strength (IS 516)**

**Step 1 — Specimen preparation (1 mark):**
- Standard cube mould size = **150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm** (cubes of 100 mm are allowed for aggregate ≤ 20 mm, but conversion factor 0.95 applies).
- After 24 h, demould and **submerge in clean potable water** at **27 ± 2 °C** until testing.
- Test age = **7 days and 28 days** (3 specimens per age per mix).

**Step 2 — Surface preparation & capping (1 mark):**
- Surfaces must be **dry and free from sand/loose particles**.
- Apply **sulphur-capping** or **plaster of Paris capping** (3–5 mm thick) OR use **unbonded neoprene pads** (hardness 60–70 IRHD) for capping.

**Step 3 — Centring in CTM (1 mark):**
- Place the cube on the **lower platen** with **capped faces in contact**.
- Align axis of specimen with axis of the platen.

**Step 4 — Rate of loading (2 marks):**
- Apply load **continuously and uniformly** at a rate of:
$$
\text{Rate} = 140 \text{ kg/cm}^2/\text{min} \quad \approx \quad 13.72 \text{ N/mm}^2/\text{min}
$$
- The failure must occur in **15 to 30 seconds** of load application.

**Step 5 — Failure observation (1 mark):**
- Note **maximum load $P_{max}$** sustained.
- Observe failure pattern (satisfactory cone pattern = uniform load distribution).

**Step 6 — Reporting (1 mark):**
- Compute $f_c = P_{max} / A$.
- Report to **nearest 0.5 MPa** (or as required).
- Report **average of 3 specimens**.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical + Acceptance Check**

**Given:**
- Cube size = $150 \times 150 \times 150$ mm
- $P = 725$ kN $= 725{,}000$ N
- Target grade = M30, so $f_{ck} = 30$ MPa
- $\sigma = 4.5$ MPa

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area** (1 mark)

$$
A = 150 \times 150 = 22{,}500 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2: Compressive strength** (1.5 marks)

$$
f_c = \dfrac{725{,}000}{22{,}500} = 32.22 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Target mean strength $f_m$** (1.5 marks)

$$
f_m = f_{ck} + 1.65 \sigma = 30 + 1.65 \times 4.5 = 30 + 7.425 = 37.43 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Individual-cube acceptance** (2 marks)

> A single-cube result is acceptable if it lies within $\pm 15\%$ of the mean of 3 cubes; for our single-cube, we compare against the *acceptable lower bound* of $f_m$ (typically $f_{ck} - 4$ MPa, per IS 456):
> Lower acceptable limit = $30 - 4 = 26$ MPa
> Upper acceptable limit = generally not bounded for a single cube, but the average must reach $f_m$.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{f_c = 32.22 \text{ MPa} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Single cube is ABOVE the lower acceptance limit (26 MPa); however, batch-level acceptance requires the 3-cube average to meet } f_m = 37.43 \text{ MPa, which CANNOT be confirmed from a single-cube reading. Therefore, the cube individually passes, but batch acceptance is pending further data.}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the **Flexural Strength Test of Hardened Concrete Beam** as per IS 516. Compare **two-point loading** and **centre-point loading** systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A concrete beam of size **150 mm × 150 mm × 700 mm** is tested under two-point loading with an **effective span of 600 mm**. The two loads are applied at $L/3$ from each support. The maximum load observed was **45 kN**. Compute the **flexural strength (modulus of rupture)**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Flexural Test Procedure**

**Specimen (1 mark):**
- Size: **150 mm × 150 mm × 700 mm** (for aggregate ≤ 38 mm)
- Cast, demould, water-cure at 27 ± 2 °C for 7/28 days.

**Test Setup (2 marks):**
- The beam is supported on two **steel rollers** (diameter ≥ 38 mm) and loaded through two **upper rollers** (when two-point method is used).
- **Effective span = 3 × depth (3d)** for two-point loading.
- Side surfaces must be **clean, smooth, and free from sand projection**.

**Rate of loading (1 mark):**
- Apply load continuously at: **180 kg/cm²/min** of extreme fibre stress (i.e., the loading rate is increased such that the extreme fibre stress rises at this rate).
- Equivalent: ~1.0–1.5 kN/sec for 150 mm beams.

**Two-point vs Centre-point loading (2 marks):**

| Feature | Two-Point Load | Centre-Point Load |
|---|---|---|
| Loading pattern | Two equal point loads at $L/3$ from supports | Single load at mid-span |
| Moment distribution | Constant (pure bending) in middle third | Linear, max only at centre |
| Failure location | Anywhere in middle third — reliable | Exactly at mid-span (local crushing possible) |
| Formula | $f_b = \dfrac{PL}{b d^2}$ | $f_b = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b d^2}$ |
| IS 516 preference | **Preferred (recommended)** | Permitted but secondary |

**Reporting (1 mark):**
- If fracture occurs in the **middle third** (tension face), use the formula directly.
- If fracture occurs in the **outer third** (within $L/3$ from support), use the modified formula $f_b = \dfrac{3 P a}{b d^2}$, where $a$ = distance from fracture section to nearest support.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical**

**Given:**
- $b = 150$ mm, $d = 150$ mm
- Effective span $L = 600$ mm
- $P_{max} = 45$ kN $= 45{,}000$ N
- Loading: two-point (loads applied at $L/3$)

**Step 1: Apply two-point flexural formula** (3 marks)

$$
f_b = \dfrac{P L}{b d^2}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values** (2 marks)

$$
f_b = \dfrac{45{,}000 \times 600}{150 \times (150)^2}
$$

$$
f_b = \dfrac{27{,}000{,}000}{150 \times 22{,}500} = \dfrac{27{,}000{,}000}{3{,}375{,}000}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate** (1.5 marks)

$$
f_b = 8.00 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):**

$$
\boxed{f_b = 8.00 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Acceptable flexural strength for M40+ grade concrete (typical } f_b \approx 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}} \text{ for design).}}
$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision Block — Memorize Before Entering the Lab Exam Hall!**

- **Core definitions:**
  - *Finished Product* = cured, dimensionally stable, ready-for-use construction unit.
  - *Performance Test* = validation of design strength through destructive testing per IS codes.

- **Cube test (IS 516):**
  - Size **150 mm**, load rate **140 kg/cm²/min**, age **7 & 28 days**, **3 specimens per age**.
  - Formula: $f_c = P / A$.

- **Cylinder split (IS 5816):**
  - Formula: $f_{st} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi L D}$.
  - Standard cylinder: **150 mm Ø × 300 mm**.

- **Beam flexure (IS 516):**
  - Two-point: $f_b = \dfrac{P L}{b d^2}$.
  - Centre-point: $f_b = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b d^2}$.
  - If fracture is outside the middle third, use $f_b = \dfrac{3 P a}{b d^2}$.

- **Brick tests (IS 3495):**
  - Compression: $f = P / (L \times B)$.
  - Water absorption: $W\% = \dfrac{W_{sat} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100 \leq 15\%$.
  - Capping with Plaster of Paris mandatory (frog upward).

- **IS 456 acceptance:**
  - $f_m = f_{ck} + 1.65 \sigma$ (target mean strength).
  - No individual cube result should fall below $f_{ck} - 4$ MPa.
  - Reject the batch if any single result is below this lower bound.

- **Numerical hygiene:**
  - Convert kN → N by **× 1000** *before* substitution.
  - Keep units in **N and mm** to get strength in **N/mm² (= MPa)**.
  - Always write the **final answer with units and 2 decimal places**.

- **Common viva traps:**
  - "*Why 150 mm cube?* → Standard reference size with proven correlation to $f_{ck}$."
  - "*Why capped?* → To ensure uniform stress distribution and avoid eccentric loading."
  - "*Why $1.65\sigma$?* → 95% confidence interval for one-tailed normal distribution."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and Experiment on Universal Testing Machine (UTM) to Determine Tensile Characteristics of Ferrous Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIEL507 - QUALITY AND MATERIAL TESTING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Study and Experiment on Universal Testing Machine (UTM) to Determine Tensile Characteristics of Ferrous Materials ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** is a versatile, hydraulically or mechanically actuated precision testing apparatus capable of performing multiple mechanical tests — tensile, compression, bending, shear, and hardness — on engineering materials through interchangeable fixtures (grips). In the context of **PCIEL507 – Quality and Material Testing Lab**, Module Experiment 2 specifically focuses on the **tensile characterization of ferrous materials** (mild steel, medium carbon steel, high carbon steel, cast iron, and stainless steel specimens) under uniaxial static loading as per **IS 1608 / ASTM E8 / ISO 6892-1** standards. The **tensile test** is a **destructive mechanical testing** procedure in which a standardized specimen of known cross-sectional area is subjected to a gradually increasing axial tensile load until fracture, while simultaneously recording the load-elongation response. This test yields the fundamental design properties used universally in strength of materials and machine design. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is mapped to **CO2 – "Apply standard testing procedures to evaluate mechanical properties of engineering materials"** and tests the cognitive level **L3 (Apply)** under Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are pulling apart a piece of **chewing gum** or a **rubber band** slowly. Initially, it stretches easily with little force (this is the **elastic region**). If you release it, it springs back to its original length. Now imagine pulling a **chocolate bar** (like a KitKat) — it bends, then breaks suddenly without much warning (this is **brittle behavior**, typical of **cast iron**). A **mild steel rod**, on the other hand, behaves like a **tough toffee** — it stretches noticeably, then "gives way" gradually before finally snapping. A **Universal Testing Machine** is essentially a **mechanized, instrumented, and standards-compliant version of this pulling action** — but with extreme precision. It records *how much force* was applied (y-axis in kN) versus *how much the specimen stretched* (x-axis in mm) at every millisecond, and from this single graph (the **stress-strain curve**), engineers extract **all** the critical design properties of the material. ### 1.3 Ferrous Materials — What Makes Them Special? **Ferrous materials** are metals whose primary alloying element is **iron (Fe)**. The presence of **carbon (C)** as the interstitial alloying element fundamentally alters the mechanical response. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Metric Standards Used in Lab:** > - Force: **kilonewton (kN)** > - Stress: **Newton per square millimeter (N/mm²) = Megapascal (MPa)** > - Strain: **dimensionless (mm/mm)** or **percentage (%)** > - Gauge Length: **millimeter (mm)** > - Cross-sectional Area: **square millimeter (mm²)** | Ferrous Material | Typical Carbon Content | Tensile Behavior | |---|---|---| | **Mild Steel** | 0.05 – 0.25 % C | Ductile, distinct yield point | | **Medium Carbon Steel** | 0.25 – 0.60 % C | Higher strength, less ductile | | **High Carbon Steel** | 0.60 – 1.0 % C | Hard, strong, brittle tendency | | **Cast Iron** | 2.0 – 4.0 % C | Brittle, low ductility | | **Stainless Steel** | < 0.08 % C + Cr/Ni | Corrosion resistant, ductile | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic of a Tensile Test — Step by Step 1. **Specimen Preparation:** A standardized **round or flat tensile specimen** is machined to dimensions per **IS 1608 (proportional specimen)**. Typical gauge length $L_0$ is related to cross-sectional area $A_0$ as $L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0}$ (proportional specimen). 2. **Mounting:** The specimen is fixed between the **upper (stationary) grip** and the **lower (moving) grip** of the UTM, ensuring axial alignment to prevent bending stresses. 3. **Extensometer Attachment:** A precise **extensometer** (mechanical, optical, or electronic) is clamped onto the gauge length to record elongation accurately up to the yield point. 4. **Loading:** A gradual, monotonic tensile load is applied at a **constant strain rate** (typically 0.00025 to 0.0025 s⁻¹ as per ISO 6892-1). 5. **Data Acquisition:** The load cell and extensometer continuously feed data to the **digital data acquisition system (DAS)**, generating a real-time **load-elongation curve**. 6. **Post-Fracture Measurement:** After fracture, the two halves are reassembled, and the **final gauge length ($L_f$)** and **final cross-sectional area at fracture ($A_f$)** are measured using a **vernier caliper** (least count 0.02 mm) and **micrometer** (least count 0.01 mm). 7. **Engineering Stress-Strain Curve Conversion:** The raw load-elongation data is mathematically converted into the **engineering stress ($\sigma$)** vs **engineering strain ($\varepsilon$)** curve. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why a proportional specimen?** It ensures the test results are **geometry-independent** and comparable across different laboratories worldwide. - **Why an extensometer?** Direct measurement of grip separation includes **machine compliance** and **slippage at grips** — the extensometer measures *only* the deformation in the gauge length. - **Why measure $A_f$ after fracture?** The reduction in area is a direct indicator of the material's **ductility** and **triaxial stress state** at the fracture point. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are board-exam essential. Memorize SI units, formula form, and physical meaning.** | # | Property | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A_0}$ | **MPa** (N/mm²) | Internal resistance per unit original area | | 2 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{L - L_0}{L_0}$ | dimensionless or **%** | Relative elongation | | 3 | Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity) | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{P \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}$ | **GPa** | Stiffness — resistance to elastic deformation | | 4 | Yield Stress (0.2 % Offset Method) | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{P_y}{A_0}$ at $\varepsilon = 0.002$ offset | **MPa** | Stress at which plastic deformation begins | | 5 | Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) | $\sigma_{uts} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0}$ | **MPa** | Maximum load divided by original area | | 6 | Breaking Stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{P_f}{A_0}$ | **MPa** | Load at fracture divided by original area | | 7 | True Stress at Fracture | $\sigma_{true,f} = \dfrac{P_f}{A_f}$ | **MPa** | Load at fracture divided by *actual* fractured area | | 8 | True Stress at UTS | $\sigma_{true,uts} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_{uts}}$ | **MPa** | True stress considering necking | | 9 | Percentage Elongation (% Elongation) | $\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | **%** | Ductility indicator | | 10 | Percentage Reduction in Area (% RA) | $\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | **%** | Ductility indicator (more reliable than %EL) | | 11 | Modulus of Resilience | $U_r = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma_y \cdot \varepsilon_y = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$ | **MJ/m³** (N·mm/mm³) | Energy absorbed per unit volume in elastic region | | 12 | Modulus of Toughness | $U_t = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | **MJ/m³** | Total energy absorbed per unit volume up to fracture | | 13 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\dfrac{\Delta d / d_0}{\Delta L / L_0}$ | dimensionless | Lateral strain to axial strain ratio | | 14 | Proportional Gauge Length | $L_0 = k \sqrt{A_0}$, where $k = 5.65$ (short) or $11.3$ (long) | **mm** | Standard specimen geometry | | 15 | Strain Rate | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$ | **s⁻¹** | Rate of deformation | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU Board:** > - For **mild steel**, yield point is identified by the **upper yield point (UYP)** and **lower yield point (LYP)** phenomenon — sudden load drop. > - For **medium/high carbon steel and non-ferrous metals**, yield stress is determined by the **0.2 % offset method** since there is no distinct yield point. > - **Cast iron** exhibits negligible plastic deformation — it fractures in the **elastic region itself** (no yielding). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The tensile test data drives **every** design decision in mechanical, civil, aerospace, and structural engineering: - **Shaft design** in power transmission uses $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_{uts}$ for factor-of-safety calculations. - **Pressure vessel codes (ASME Section VIII)** mandate minimum $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_{uts}$, and $\%EL$ for plate materials. - **Sheet metal forming** operations (automotive, aerospace) rely on $\%EL$ and $\%RA$ as formability indices. - **Failure analysis** post-fracture reconstructs the stress-strain history using true stress-true strain data. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Load-Elongation to Stress-Strain Curve The raw output of the UTM is a **Load (P) vs Elongation ($\Delta L$)** graph. To obtain the material's intrinsic properties (independent of specimen geometry), we normalize: **Step 1 — Define Engineering Stress** $$ \sigma = \frac{\text{Instantaneous Load}}{\text{Original Cross-sectional Area}} = \frac{P}{A_0} $$ *Logical reasoning:* Dividing the force by the area gives the **internal force per unit area**, which is the definition of stress. Using $A_0$ (not $A$) yields **engineering stress**, which is easier to compute and is the standard reported in datasheets. **Step 2 — Define Engineering Strain** $$ \varepsilon = \frac{\text{Change in Length}}{\text{Original Gauge Length}} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} $$ *Logical reasoning:* Strain is the **dimensionless ratio of deformation to original size**, ensuring geometry-independent comparison. **Step 3 — Linear-Elastic Region and Young's Modulus** In the linear elastic region (Hooke's Law), the stress-strain curve is a **straight line through the origin**: $$ \sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon $$ Solving for $E$: $$ E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{P / A_0}{\Delta L / L_0} = \frac{P \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L} $$ *Logical reasoning:* $E$ represents the **slope of the straight-line portion** of the $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ curve. A steeper slope means stiffer material. **Step 4 — 0.2 % Offset Yield Stress Derivation** For materials without a distinct yield point, draw a line parallel to the elastic line, offset by $\varepsilon = 0.002$ (i.e., 0.2 % strain). The stress at the intersection of this offset line with the actual $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ curve defines $\sigma_y$: $$ \varepsilon_{offset} = 0.002 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_y = E \cdot (\varepsilon - 0.002) $$ evaluated at the intersection point $(\varepsilon_y, \sigma_y)$. *Logical reasoning:* The 0.2 % offset is a **conventional definition** (not a physical transition) — it represents the stress that causes 0.2 % permanent plastic strain, which is small enough to be design-significant. **Step 5 — Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** $$ \sigma_{uts} = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0} $$ *Logical reasoning:* UTS is **not the stress at fracture**; it is the **maximum engineering stress** the material can sustain, which typically occurs at the **onset of necking** in ductile materials. **Step 6 — True Stress vs Engineering Stress at Fracture** When the specimen necks, the actual cross-section reduces to $A_f$. The true stress is: $$ \sigma_{true,f} = \frac{P_f}{A_f} $$ Assuming **constant volume during plastic deformation** ($A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$), the true strain at fracture is: $$ \varepsilon_{true,f} = \ln\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) $$ *Logical reasoning:* Plastic deformation occurs at near-constant volume because the material is incompressible at these strains. This relationship is fundamental to metal forming analysis. **Step 7 — Ductility Metrics** $$ \%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 $$ $$ \%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 $$ *Logical reasoning:* Both metrics indicate **how much the material can deform plastically before fracture**. $\%RA$ is considered more reliable because it is **less sensitive to gauge length selection**. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Exam Style) > **Problem:** A mild steel tensile specimen has an original diameter $d_0 = 12$ mm and gauge length $L_0 = 50$ mm. During testing: > - Load at yield point $P_y = 28$ kN > - Maximum load $P_{max} = 42$ kN > - Load at fracture $P_f = 32$ kN > - Final gauge length $L_f = 64$ mm > - Final diameter at fracture $d_f = 8.5$ mm > > Calculate: (a) Yield stress, (b) UTS, (c) % Elongation, (d) % Reduction in Area, (e) True fracture stress. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Original cross-sectional area** $$ A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_0^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (12)^2 = 113.10 \; \text{mm}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Final cross-sectional area at fracture** $$ A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} d_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (8.5)^2 = 56.75 \; \text{mm}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Yield stress** $$ \sigma_y = \frac{P_y}{A_0} = \frac{28 \times 10^3 \; \text{N}}{113.10 \; \text{mm}^2} = 247.57 \; \text{MPa} $$ **Step 4 — Ultimate Tensile Strength** $$ \sigma_{uts} = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{42 \times 10^3}{113.10} = 371.35 \; \text{MPa} $$ **Step 5 — Percentage Elongation** $$ \%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{64 - 50}{50} \times 100 = 28 \; \% $$ **Step 6 — Percentage Reduction in Area** $$ \%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{113.10 - 56.75}{113.10} \times 100 = 49.82 \; \% $$ **Step 7 — True Fracture Stress** $$ \sigma_{true,f} = \frac{P_f}{A_f} = \frac{32 \times 10^3}{56.75} = 563.88 \; \text{MPa} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** $\sigma_{true,f}$ (563.88 MPa) is significantly higher than the engineering breaking stress $\sigma_b = P_f/A_0 = 282.94$ MPa, demonstrating the **substantial triaxial stress concentration** within the necked region of a ductile material. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the Failure Type from Fracture Surface | Observation | Failure Type | Material Class | |---|---|---| | **Cup-and-cone** fracture with central fibrous zone + radial shear lips | Ductile failure | Mild steel, low/medium carbon steel | | **100 % flat, granular, perpendicular to load axis** | Brittle cleavage failure | Cast iron, hardened high carbon steel | | **45° shear lips only, no necking** | Quasi-brittle failure | High carbon steel (untempered) | | **Wood-grain or fibrous surface** | Anisotropic failure | Rolled/forged steel with fiber orientation | | **Granular with secondary cracks** | Brittle with stress corrosion | Stainless steel in corrosive environment | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture — UTM Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Hydraulic Power Pack] --> B[Load Frame Crosshead] B --> C[Upper Grip - Stationary] C --> D[Tensile Specimen] D --> E[Lower Grip - Moving Crosshead] E --> F[Load Cell] F --> G[Signal Conditioner] H[Extensometer on Gauge Length] --> G G --> I[Data Acquisition System DAS] I --> J[Digital Display - Load vs Elongation] I --> K[PC with Software - Stress vs Strain Plot] L[Manual Control Valves] --> A M[Strain Rate Controller] --> A ``` *Description of flow:* Hydraulic fluid from the power pack actuates the moving crosshead, applying tensile load on the specimen. The load cell measures force, the extensometer measures elongation, both signals are conditioned and processed by the DAS, producing a real-time digital plot of stress vs. strain. ### 4.2 Stress-Strain Curve for Mild Steel — Key Regions ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Origin O] --> B[Proportional Limit] B --> C[Elastic Limit] C --> D[Upper Yield Point UYP] D --> E[Lower Yield Point LYP] E --> F[Yield Plateau] F --> G[Strain Hardening Region] G --> H[Ultimate Tensile Strength UTS] H --> I[Necking Region] I --> J[Fracture Point] ``` *Region descriptions:* - **O to B:** Linear elastic, obeys Hooke's Law. - **C to D:** Upper yield point — sudden onset of plasticity. - **D to E:** Load drop to lower yield point. - **E to F:** Yield plateau — Luders bands propagate. - **F to G:** Strain hardening — material strengthens with deformation. - **G to H:** Peak load — UTS reached. - **H to I:** Necking — local cross-section reduces drastically. - **I to J:** Final fracture. ### 4.3 Specimen Geometry Diagram (IS 1608 Proportional Round Specimen) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SPEC [IS 1608 Tensile Specimen Geometry] direction TB S1[Head M16 Threaded] --> S2[Shoulder Radius R = 12 mm] S2 --> S3[Reduced Parallel Section Length Lc = 60 mm] S3 --> S4[Gauge Length Lo = 50 mm] S4 --> S5[Reduced Section Diameter d = 12 mm] end ``` *Standard nomenclature:* - $L_0$ — Original gauge length - $L_c$ — Length of reduced parallel section - $d$ — Diameter of reduced section - $R$ — Fillet radius at shoulder ### 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Identifying Tensile Properties from Test Data ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Start: Load vs Elongation Data] --> Q1{Is the
curve linear
at the start?} Q1 -->|Yes| A1[Calculate E from slope] Q1 -->|No| A2[Check extensometer calibration] A1 --> Q2{Is there a
sudden load drop
indicating yield?} Q2 -->|Yes - Mild Steel| B1[Identify UYP and LYP] Q2 -->|No| B2[Apply 0.2 percent offset method] B1 --> Q3{Is there a
peak load
before fracture?} B2 --> Q3 Q3 -->|Yes| C1[Calculate UTS = Pmax / A0] Q3 -->|No| C2[Brittle material - no UTS region] C1 --> D1[Measure Lf and Af post-fracture] D1 --> E1[Compute percent EL and percent RA] E1 --> END[Report all properties] C2 --> END ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** > **Q1. Define the following terms as applied to a tensile test on ferrous materials:** > (i) Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) > (ii) Percentage Elongation > (iii) Modulus of Resilience **Model Answer:** **(i) Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS):** It is defined as the **maximum engineering stress** that a material can withstand under uniaxial tensile loading, calculated as the **maximum load recorded during the test divided by the original cross-sectional area** of the specimen. Mathematically: $$ \sigma_{uts} = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0} $$ For mild steel, UTS typically ranges from **400 to 550 MPa**. **(ii) Percentage Elongation:** It is a measure of the **ductility** of the material, defined as the **percentage increase in the gauge length** of the specimen after fracture relative to its original gauge length: $$ \%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 $$ For ductile mild steel, $\%EL$ is typically **20 to 35 %**. **(iii) Modulus of Resilience:** It is the **strain energy absorbed per unit volume** of the material within the **elastic region**, represented graphically as the **area under the linear portion of the stress-strain curve** (a triangle): $$ U_r = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \cdot \varepsilon_y = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E} $$ Units: **MJ/m³** or **N·mm/mm³**. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplied Paper]** > **Q2. Differentiate between Engineering Stress and True Stress. Under what condition does true stress become significantly higher than engineering stress?** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Engineering Stress | True Stress | |---|---|---| | Definition | Load divided by **original** area | Load divided by **instantaneous (actual)** area | | Formula | $\sigma = P / A_0$ | $\sigma_{true} = P / A_i$ | | Plot shape | Peaks at UTS, then **falls** | **Monotonically increases** until fracture | | Validity | Valid only in **uniform deformation** region | Valid through **necking region** as well | | Use | Datasheets, design codes | Metal forming, fracture analysis | **Condition for divergence:** True stress becomes significantly higher than engineering stress **after the onset of necking** in ductile materials, when the local cross-sectional area $A_i$ reduces rapidly while the load $P$ also drops. At the fracture point, for a material with $50\,\%RA$, the true stress can be **nearly double** the engineering breaking stress. --- ### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Main Exam]** > **Question A (14 Marks):** > (a) **With a neat labeled diagram, describe the construction and working of a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) used for tensile testing of ferrous materials.** (7 Marks) > (b) **A cylindrical mild steel specimen of diameter 14 mm and gauge length 56 mm was tested in tension. The maximum load was 65 kN, load at fracture was 42 kN, final gauge length was 70 mm, and final diameter at neck was 9.8 mm. Calculate: (i) Ultimate Tensile Strength, (ii) Percentage Elongation, (iii) Percentage Reduction in Area, and (iv) True Fracture Stress.** (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction and Working of UTM:** A Universal Testing Machine consists of the following major components: 1. **Loading Frame:** A heavy rigid frame (typically two-column or four-column design) capable of withstanding the maximum test load (commonly 1000 kN, 2000 kN, or 5000 kN capacity in Kerala engineering colleges). 2. **Hydraulic Power Pack:** Contains an electric motor, hydraulic oil reservoir, and a variable delivery pump that generates high-pressure oil flow. 3. **Crossheads:** Two crossheads — an **upper stationary crosshead** fixed to the columns and a **lower movable crosshead** connected to the hydraulic ram. The specimen is held between these. 4. **Gripping Jaws:** Interchangeable wedge-type or threaded grips for round and flat specimens. For round specimens, threaded grips ensure positive axial alignment. 5. **Load Cell (Proving Ring or Load Cell):** A precision force-measuring device. Modern UTMs use strain-gauge-based load cells with accuracy better than **±1 %** of the indicated load. 6. **Extensometer:** A clip-on device that measures elongation over the gauge length. Modern electronic extensometers use **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** with least count **0.001 mm**. 7. **Control Panel and Data Acquisition System:** Contains load rate control valves, digital display, and a PC interface for plotting stress-strain curves. **Working Principle:** Hydraulic oil at controlled pressure is supplied to the cylinder below the moving crosshead, pushing it downward. This applies a **tensile pull** on the specimen held between the two crossheads. The load is measured by the load cell, and elongation by the extensometer. Signals are processed and plotted in real time. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $d_0 = 14$ mm, $L_0 = 56$ mm, $P_{max} = 65$ kN, $P_f = 42$ kN, $L_f = 70$ mm, $d_f = 9.8$ mm. **Step 1 — Original Area** $$ A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_0^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (14)^2 = 153.94 \; \text{mm}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Final Area at Fracture** $$ A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} d_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (9.8)^2 = 75.43 \; \text{mm}^2 $$ **(i) Ultimate Tensile Strength** $$ \sigma_{uts} = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{65 \times 10^3}{153.94} = 422.24 \; \text{MPa} $$ **(ii) Percentage Elongation** $$ \%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{70 - 56}{56} \times 100 = 25 \; \% $$ **(iii) Percentage Reduction in Area** $$ \%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{153.94 - 75.43}{153.94} \times 100 = 51.00 \; \% $$ **(iv) True Fracture Stress** $$ \sigma_{true,f} = \frac{P_f}{A_f} = \frac{42 \times 10^3}{75.43} = 556.80 \; \text{MPa} $$ **Final Results:** $\sigma_{uts} = 422.24$ MPa, $\%EL = 25\,\%$, $\%RA = 51.00\,\%$, $\sigma_{true,f} = 556.80$ MPa. The high $\%RA$ and $\%EL$ confirm that the specimen is **ductile mild steel** with significant necking before fracture. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary Exam]** > **Question B (14 Marks):** > (a) **Explain the engineering stress-strain curve for a mild steel specimen with the help of a neatly labeled diagram. Identify and define all salient points on the curve.** (7 Marks) > (b) **A medium carbon steel specimen of original diameter 13 mm and gauge length 65 mm showed the following results: load at 0.2 % offset yield = 38 kN, maximum load = 56 kN, final gauge length = 78 mm, final diameter at fracture = 8.2 mm. If the modulus of elasticity is 210 GPa, calculate: (i) 0.2 % offset yield stress, (ii) UTS, (iii) % Elongation, (iv) % Reduction in Area, and (v) Modulus of Resilience.** (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Engineering Stress-Strain Curve for Mild Steel:** The engineering stress-strain curve for mild steel exhibits the following characteristic points (from origin O to fracture F): | Point | Notation | Physical Significance | |---|---|---| | O | Origin | Unstressed state | | A | Proportional Limit | End of linear $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ region | | B | Elastic Limit | Maximum stress for full recovery on unloading | | C | Upper Yield Point (UYP) | Stress at which plastic deformation initiates suddenly | | D | Lower Yield Point (LYP) | Constant stress during Luders band propagation | | E | Yield Plateau End | Onset of strain hardening | | F | Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) | Maximum engineering stress; onset of necking | | G | Fracture Point | Final rupture of specimen | **Key Observations:** - The region **O to A** is **linear-elastic** (Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$). - Between **C and D**, the load **drops suddenly** — this is the **yield point phenomenon** unique to low-carbon steels, caused by **dislocation unlocking from Cottrell atmospheres** of interstitial carbon/nitrogen atoms. - The region **D to E** is the **yield plateau** where elongation occurs at nearly constant stress, accompanied by visible **Luders bands** propagating along the gauge length. - The region **E to F** is **strain hardening** — the material strengthens with further deformation. - After **F**, **necking** begins, and the cross-section reduces locally, causing the **engineering stress to drop** (even though true stress continues to rise). - The shaded area under the entire curve up to fracture represents the **modulus of toughness**. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $d_0 = 13$ mm, $L_0 = 65$ mm, $P_y = 38$ kN, $P_{max} = 56$ kN, $L_f = 78$ mm, $d_f = 8.2$ mm, $E = 210$ GPa = $210 \times 10^3$ MPa. **Step 1 — Areas** $$ A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} (13)^2 = 132.73 \; \text{mm}^2 $$ $$ A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} (8.2)^2 = 52.81 \; \text{mm}^2 $$ **(i) 0.2 % Offset Yield Stress** $$ \sigma_y = \frac{P_y}{A_0} = \frac{38 \times 10^3}{132.73} = 286.31 \; \text{MPa} $$ **(ii) Ultimate Tensile Strength** $$ \sigma_{uts} = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{56 \times 10^3}{132.73} = 421.95 \; \text{MPa} $$ **(iii) Percentage Elongation** $$ \%EL = \frac{78 - 65}{65} \times 100 = 20 \; \% $$ **(iv) Percentage Reduction in Area** $$ \%RA = \frac{132.73 - 52.81}{132.73} \times 100 = 60.22 \; \% $$ **(v) Modulus of Resilience** $$ U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E} = \frac{(286.31)^2}{2 \times 210 \times 10^3} = \frac{81,973.4}{420,000} = 0.1952 \; \text{MPa} = 0.1952 \; \text{MJ/m}^3 $$ **Final Results:** $\sigma_y = 286.31$ MPa, $\sigma_{uts} = 421.95$ MPa, $\%EL = 20\,\%$, $\%RA = 60.22\,\%$, $U_r = 0.1952$ MJ/m³. The $60.22\,\%RA$ indicates **excellent ductility** despite the higher carbon content. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:** > 1. **Unit Conversion:** Students frequently forget to convert kN to N before dividing by mm². Always remember: **1 kN = 1000 N**, and **1 N/mm² = 1 MPa**. Marks lost = 1–2 per sub-part. > 2. **Using $A_f$ instead of $A_0$** in UTS / yield stress calculation: UTS and yield stress are **engineering** quantities and must use **original** area. Using $A_f$ is a fundamental error (–2 marks). > 3. **Mixing up %EL and %RA:** %EL uses **gauge length change**; %RA uses **area change**. These are NOT interchangeable. > 4. **Forgetting to multiply by 100** in percentage calculations: A common error yielding "0.25" instead of "25 %". (–1 mark) > 5. **Misapplying the 0.2 % offset method:** Students often confuse it with the **proportional limit**. The offset line is **parallel** to the elastic line but **shifted by 0.002 strain** along the strain axis. > 6. **Forgetting extensometer removal before necking:** In an actual lab exam, examiners may ask why the extensometer is removed before fracture — answer: to **protect it from damage** during necking and fracture. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2** - **Standard Reference Codes:** IS 1608, ASTM E8, ISO 6892-1 govern tensile specimen geometry and procedure. - **Specimen Geometry:** Proportional specimen: $L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0}$ (short) or $L_0 = 11.3 \sqrt{A_0}$ (long). - **Three Critical Measured Quantities:** $P_y$ (or offset load), $P_{max}$, and post-fracture dimensions $L_f$ and $A_f$. - **Six Key Reported Properties:** Young's modulus $E$, yield stress $\sigma_y$, UTS $\sigma_{uts}$, breaking stress $\sigma_b$, $\%EL$, $\%RA$. - **Engineering vs True Stress:** Engineering uses $A_0$ (constant denominator); True uses instantaneous area $A_i$ (variable denominator). - **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ is valid **only in the linear elastic region**. - **Yield Identification:** Mild steel → UYP/LYP; medium/high carbon → 0.2 % offset; cast iron → no yield, fractures elastically. - **Necking** starts at UTS for ductile materials; final fracture occurs **after** maximum load. - **Constant Volume Assumption:** $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$ during plastic deformation (incompressibility). - **Fracture Surface:** Cup-and-cone = ductile; flat granular = brittle. - **Poisson's Ratio** for steels: $\nu \approx 0.29$ to $0.30$ (measured transversely using a transverse extensometer). - **Strain Rate Effect:** Higher strain rate → higher $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_{uts}$, lower $\%EL$ (strain-rate sensitivity). - **Safety in Lab:** Always use protective screens; never stand in line with the loading axis; verify grip alignment before applying load. - **Data Reporting Format:** Report all values to **3 significant figures** with **SI units** clearly stated.

topics.title: Study and Experiment on Universal Testing Machine (UTM) to Determine Tensile Characteristics of Non-Ferrous Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL507 - QUALITY AND MATERIAL TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and Experiment on Universal Testing Machine (UTM) to Determine Tensile Characteristics of Non-Ferrous Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — UNIVERSAL TESTING MACHINE (UTM) & TENSILE TESTING OF NON-FERROUS METALS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Testing Machine (UTM):** A Universal Testing Machine is a hydraulically or electromechanically operated, precision-grade load-testing apparatus capable of applying controlled uniaxial tensile, compressive, transverse (bending), and shear loads to standardized test specimens. By measuring the applied load and the corresponding deformation, it generates the **Stress–Strain Curve** of the material from which all fundamental mechanical properties are derived.

In the context of **non-ferrous materials** (e.g., aluminium, copper, brass, bronze), the UTM is used to determine parameters such as:
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)**
- **Proof Stress / Yield Strength (0.2% offset method — typical for non-ferrous metals)**
- **Percentage Elongation (% El)**
- **Percentage Reduction in Area (% RA)**
- **Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-ferrous metals** are metals/alloys that **do not contain iron (Fe) as a principal constituent**. Common engineering examples include **aluminium (Al)**, **copper (Cu)**, **brass (Cu-Zn)**, **bronze (Cu-Sn)**, and **titanium (Ti)**. Most non-ferrous metals exhibit a **gradual yielding behaviour** without a sharply defined yield point (unlike mild steel), making the **0.2% proof stress** the standard yield criterion per **IS 10182 (Reaffirmed 2009)**.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine stretching a **chocolate bar (like a Snickers)** slowly with both hands:
- At first, it stretches a little and returns to its original shape if you let go → this is the **elastic region**.
- Pull harder, and it begins to permanently stretch → this is the **plastic region**.
- Pull even more, and at some point, the chocolate starts getting *thinner in the middle* (necking). Eventually, it breaks.

The **UTM does exactly this**, but with mathematical precision. It pulls a precisely machined metal sample (a "dog-bone") while a computer (or dial gauge) plots **Force vs. Elongation**, which we then mathematically convert to **Stress vs. Strain**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Universal"?** Because the same machine — with different fixtures (grips/jigs) — can perform **tension, compression, bending, and shear** tests. The only thing that changes is the *attachment*, not the load frame.

### 1.1.3 Classification of UTM (KTU Board-Favourite)

| Type | Drive Mechanism | Loading Capacity | Typical Use |
|------|----------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Hydraulic UTM** | Hydraulic cylinder + oil pump | **100 kN – 3000 kN** | High-capacity metals, rebar |
| **Electromechanical UTM** | Servo motor + ball screw | **1 kN – 600 kN** | Precision, low loads, non-ferrous |
| **Manual / Screw-type** | Hand-cranked screw | **≤ 200 kN** | Educational labs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For tensile testing of **non-ferrous metals** in lab courses, an **electromechanical UTM (capacity ≈ 100–600 kN)** is the most common configuration, often fitted with **extensometers** for accurate strain measurement.

### 1.1.4 Standardized Test Specimens (KTU High-Yield)

For **non-ferrous metals**, tensile specimens are machined per **IS 10182: 1982 (Reaffirmed 2009)** / **ASTM E8/E8M**.

**Two standard proportional types:**

$$L_0 = k \sqrt{A_0}$$

where:
- $L_0$ = Original gauge length
- $A_0$ = Original cross-sectional area
- $k$ = Proportionality constant (**5.65** for round specimens)

| Specimen Type | Gauge Length $L_0$ | Diameter $d_0$ | Use |
|---------------|---------------------|------------------|------|
| Proportional | $5.65\sqrt{A_0}$ | As machined | Standard reference |
| Non-proportional (fixed) | **50 mm / 200 mm** | 12.5 mm / 20 mm | Convenient for lab |
| Flat (rectangular) | $5.65\sqrt{A_0}$ | Thickness × Width | Sheet/plate |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning:** Many students confuse **gauge length** ($L_0$) with **total length** of the specimen. **Always** distinguish: $L_0$ is the *measured* length over which elongation is recorded — typically marked on the specimen before testing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of UTM

A UTM applies an **axial, monotonically increasing load** to a specimen held between two grips. The load cell (or proving ring in older machines) measures the force, while an extensometer or cross-head displacement transducer measures deformation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Operating Equation:** The UTM essentially solves the inverse problem — from measured *force (F)* and *extension (ΔL)*, it derives the intrinsic material properties:
> - $\sigma = F / A_0$ → Stress (material property, geometry-independent)
> - $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ → Strain (dimensionless)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of a Tensile Test on UTM

**Step 1 — Specimen Preparation**
- Machine the non-ferrous sample (e.g., aluminium, brass) into the standard dog-bone shape.
- Measure **initial diameter** $d_0$ (using vernier caliper / micrometer) at three locations; record the mean.
- Compute **initial cross-sectional area**: $A_0 = \pi d_0^2 / 4$
- Mark the **gauge length** $L_0$ on the specimen (50 mm or 200 mm).

**Step 2 — Mounting the Specimen**
- Grip the specimen in the upper and lower jaws of the UTM.
- Ensure the **specimen axis is perfectly aligned with the load axis** to avoid bending stresses.
- Attach the **extensometer** to the gauge length.

**Step 3 — Apply Load Gradually**
- Increase load at a **constant strain rate** (typically $0.001$ to $0.005$ per second per IS 10182).
- Record load–extension data continuously.

**Step 4 — Through Failure**
- Continue loading until the specimen **fractures**.
- Record the **maximum load** ($F_{max}$) reached.
- After fracture, fit the two broken halves together carefully and measure:
  - **Final gauge length** $L_f$
  - **Final minimum diameter** $d_f$ at the neck

**Step 5 — Compute Properties** (see Section 3 for derivations)

## 2.3 Key Tensile Properties for Non-Ferrous Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "non-ferrous" behaves differently from mild steel:**
> Non-ferrous metals (e.g., Al, Cu, Brass) typically show:
> - **No sharply defined yield point** → use **0.2% proof stress**.
> - **Lower elastic modulus** than steel (Al ≈ 70 GPa vs Steel ≈ 200 GPa).
> - **Higher ductility** (Al alloys: 10–25% elongation).
> - **Strain-hardening region** dominates (no clear upper/lower yield plateau).

### 2.3.1 Stress–Strain Curve Schematic (for Non-Ferrous)

The characteristic stress-strain curve has these regions:

1. **Linear Elastic Region** — Hooke's Law valid: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$
2. **Proportional Limit** — End of linear region
3. **Elastic Limit** — Beyond which permanent deformation occurs
4. **0.2% Proof Stress (Yield Strength)** — Intersection of offset line with stress-strain curve
5. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** — Peak of the curve
6. **Necking Region** — Local reduction in area begins
7. **Fracture Point** — Specimen breaks

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in every KTU tensile-test calculation problem.**

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Initial cross-sectional area | $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4}$ | mm² | For round specimens |
| 1a | Rectangular cross-section | $A_0 = b_0 \times t_0$ | mm² | $b_0$=width, $t_0$=thickness |
| 2 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | N/mm² = MPa | **Always uses $A_0$**, not instantaneous area |
| 3 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{L - L_0}{L_0}$ | dimensionless (×100 for %) | |
| 4 | Young's Modulus | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}$ | GPa | Linear region only |
| 5 | Ultimate Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{UTS} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}$ | MPa | Peak load |
| 6 | Yield Strength (0.2% Proof) | From offset construction | MPa | For non-ferrous metals |
| 7 | % Elongation | $\% El = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | % | Measure $L_f$ post-fracture |
| 8 | % Reduction in Area | $\% RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | % | $A_f = \pi d_f^2 / 4$ |
| 9 | Proportional Gauge Length | $L_0 = 5.65\sqrt{A_0}$ | mm | Round specimens |
| 10 | Proving Ring Constant | $k = \dfrac{\text{Ring Constant}}{\text{Least Count}}$ | — | Dial gauge based UTMs |
| 11 | True Stress | $\sigma_T = \dfrac{F}{A_i}$ | MPa | $A_i$ = instantaneous area |
| 12 | True Strain | $\varepsilon_T = \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_i}\right) = \ln\left(\dfrac{L_i}{L_0}\right)$ | dimensionless | Constant volume assumption |

### 2.4.1 Boundary & Limit Conditions

- **Validity of Hooke's Law:** Only in the **linear elastic region** where the stress–strain curve is straight.
- **Strain rate dependency:** Non-ferrous alloys (especially at elevated temperatures) show strain-rate sensitivity; tests must be conducted at standard rate per **IS 10182**.
- **Necking onset:** Engineering stress *decreases* after UTS, but **true stress keeps increasing** due to area reduction.
- **Volume constancy** (plastic region): $A_i L_i = A_0 L_0$

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where do these UTM-derived properties matter in real engineering?**
> - **Aerospace**: Aluminium alloy tensile strength determines wing-skin thickness.
> - **Electrical**: Copper conductivity vs. strength trade-off for busbars.
> - **Civil**: Brass/copper fittings require specified % elongation for ductility.
> - **Quality control**: Batch acceptance testing of non-ferrous ingots per IS 1387.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Engineering Stress from Force–Area Relation

**Given:** An axial tensile force $F$ is applied on a circular specimen of diameter $d_0$.

**Step 1:** Initial cross-sectional area of the round specimen.

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi \, d_0^{\,2}}{4}$$

**Step 2:** By definition of engineering (nominal) stress — force per unit original area:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{F}{\dfrac{\pi \, d_0^{\,2}}{4}} = \frac{4F}{\pi \, d_0^{\,2}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This is the *load-carrying capacity per unit cross-sectional area*. It is **independent of the test specimen size** — a material constant.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Engineering Strain from Length Change

**Step 1:** At any instant, the specimen length is $L$. Initial gauge length is $L_0$.

**Step 2:** Extension (elongation):

$$\Delta L = L - L_0$$

**Step 3:** Engineering strain (dimensionless):

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$

**Step 4:** Percentage strain (commonly reported in non-ferrous testing):

$$\varepsilon\,(\%) = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Young's Modulus from Linear Elastic Region

**Step 1:** Within the linear elastic region, Hooke's Law applies:

$$\sigma = E\,\varepsilon$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the definitions of $\sigma$ and $\varepsilon$:

$$\frac{F}{A_0} = E \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $E$:

$$E = \frac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L} = \frac{4 F L_0}{\pi \, d_0^{\,2} \cdot \Delta L}$$

**Units check:**

$$E = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{mm}}{\text{mm}^2 \cdot \text{mm}} = \frac{\text{N}}{\text{mm}^2} = \text{MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For converting MPa to GPa: $1 \text{ GPa} = 1000 \text{ MPa}$. Typical values for non-ferrous metals:
> - Aluminium: $E \approx 70 \text{ GPa}$
> - Copper: $E \approx 110 \text{ GPa}$
> - Brass: $E \approx 100 \text{ GPa}$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Percentage Elongation (% El)

**Step 1:** Original gauge length: $L_0$.

**Step 2:** After fracture, the broken halves are carefully aligned (without forcing) and the final gauge length is measured: $L_f$.

**Step 3:**

$$\%\,\text{Elongation} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$$

**Typical values for non-ferrous metals:**

| Material | % Elongation (annealed) |
|----------|--------------------------|
| Pure Aluminium (1100) | 30 – 40 % |
| Al-Cu Alloy (2024-T3) | 15 – 20 % |
| Pure Copper (annealed) | 40 – 60 % |
| Brass (70/30) | 50 – 65 % |
| Bronze | 30 – 50 % |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Percentage Reduction in Area (% RA)

**Step 1:** Original area:

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi\, d_0^{\,2}}{4}$$

**Step 2:** Final minimum diameter at fracture (neck): $d_f$. Final area:

$$A_f = \frac{\pi\, d_f^{\,2}}{4}$$

**Step 3:**

$$\%\,\text{RA} = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{d_0^{\,2} - d_f^{\,2}}{d_0^{\,2}} \times 100$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — 0.2% Proof Stress (For Non-Ferrous Yield Criterion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 0.2% Proof Stress?** Non-ferrous metals (e.g., Al, Cu, Brass) do **not** exhibit a sharply defined yield point. Hence **IS 10182** specifies the **0.2% offset method** as the standardized yield criterion.

**Step 1:** On the stress–strain curve, draw a line parallel to the initial linear (elastic) portion, but offset along the strain axis by **0.002 (i.e., 0.2%)**.

**Step 2:** The intersection of this offset line with the actual stress–strain curve defines the **0.2% Proof Stress**, denoted $\sigma_{0.2}$.

**Mathematically, if the elastic modulus is $E$ and the offset line equation is:**

$$\sigma = E\,(\varepsilon - 0.002)$$

**Step 3:** Intersect with the actual stress–strain curve (e.g., Ramberg-Osgood model):

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} + \alpha\left(\frac{\sigma}{\sigma_0}\right)^{n}$$

**Iterative solution** gives $\sigma_{0.2}$, typically read off the plotted graph in a lab setting.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)

**Step 1:** During the test, record the maximum load: $F_{max}$.

**Step 2:**

$$\sigma_{UTS} = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{4 F_{max}}{\pi \, d_0^{\,2}}$$

**Significance:** The UTS is the maximum engineering stress the material can sustain under uniaxial tension. Beyond this, **necking** begins and engineering stress decreases, although the material is still intact locally.

---

## 3.8 Comprehensive Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution Style)

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example for Lab Record / KTU Board-Style Question:**

**Problem:**
A cylindrical specimen of **brass** with initial diameter $d_0 = 20 \text{ mm}$ and gauge length $L_0 = 50 \text{ mm}$ is tested in a UTM. The following data was recorded:

| Load (kN) | Elongation (mm) |
|-----------|------------------|
| 5         | 0.012            |
| 10        | 0.024            |
| 25        | 0.061            |
| 50        | 0.125            |
| 75        | 0.250            |
| **90** (max) | **1.500**     |
| 75 (fracture) | 12.5 (final $L_f$ with $d_f = 14 \text{ mm}$) |

**Compute:**
1. Young's Modulus $E$
2. Ultimate Tensile Strength $\sigma_{UTS}$
3. Percentage Elongation % El
4. Percentage Reduction in Area % RA
5. Yield Strength (0.2% offset)

---

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial area:**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi \, d_0^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (20)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 400}{4} = 100\pi \approx 314.16 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Young's Modulus (using linear elastic data point: 25 kN, 0.061 mm):**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{25\,000}{314.16} = 79.58 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{0.061}{50} = 0.00122$$

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{79.58}{0.00122} = 65\,230 \text{ MPa} \approx 65.23 \text{ GPa}$$

> *(Note: For brass, expected E ≈ 100 GPa; the linear region check should use the first 2–3 points, where data is most reliable.)*

**Step 3 — Ultimate Tensile Strength:**

$$\sigma_{UTS} = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{90\,000}{314.16} = 286.48 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Elongation:**

$$\%\,El = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{62.5 - 50}{50} \times 100 = 25\,\%$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Reduction in Area:**

$$A_f = \frac{\pi\, d_f^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 14^2}{4} = 49\pi \approx 153.94 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\%\,RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{314.16 - 153.94}{314.16} \times 100 = 51.0\,\%$$

**Step 6 — Yield Strength (0.2% offset):**
- Plot the stress-strain curve from the table.
- Draw a line from $\varepsilon = 0.002$ parallel to the initial elastic slope.
- Read $\sigma$ at intersection → typically around **~ 250 MPa** for brass specimens of this grade.

---

## 3.9 Analytical Matrix — Stress vs. True Stress Distinction

| Concept | Engineering Stress $\sigma$ | True Stress $\sigma_T$ |
|---------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Definition | $F / A_0$ | $F / A_i$ (instantaneous area) |
| Validity region | Entire test | Plastic region only (post-necking) |
| Beyond UTS | **Decreases** | **Continues to increase** |
| Formula | $\sigma = F/A_0$ | $\sigma_T = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$ |
| Use case | Design calculations | Metal forming, fracture mechanics |

**Derivation of relationship:** Using plastic incompressibility $A_i L_i = A_0 L_0$:

$$\sigma_T = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot \frac{A_0}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot \frac{L_i}{L_0} = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a UTM

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **internal functional architecture** of an electromechanical UTM used for tensile testing of non-ferrous materials. It traces the signal/force flow from operator input to data output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Console] --> B[Servo Controller Unit]
    B --> C[Servo Motor Drive]
    C --> D[Ball Screw Gearbox Assembly]
    D --> E[Crosshead Moving Platform]
    E --> F[Upper Grip Chuck]
    G[Specimen Mounted in Grips] --> F
    G --> H[Lower Grip Chuck]
    H --> I[Fixed Reaction Base]
    E --> J[Load Cell Force Transducer]
    J --> K[Signal Amplifier Circuit]
    E --> L[Extensometer LVDT]
    L --> M[Strain Signal Conditioner]
    K --> N[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Data Acquisition System DAQ]
    O --> P[Real Time Plot Load vs Extension]
    O --> Q[Post Processor Calculator]
    Q --> R[Stress Strain Curve Output]
    Q --> S[Mechanical Properties Module]
    S --> S1[Ultimate Tensile Strength]
    S --> S2[Yield Proof Stress 0.2 percent]
    S --> S3[Percentage Elongation]
    S --> S4[Young Modulus Value]
    S --> S5[Percentage Reduction in Area]
    R --> T[Display Monitor Lab Report]
    S --> T
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Tensile Test Sequence on UTM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Prepare Specimen Per IS 10182] --> P2[Step 2 Measure d0 and L0]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Compute Initial Area A0]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Mount Specimen in UTM Grips]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Attach Extensometer to Gauge Length]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Set Crosshead Speed and Load Range]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Start Test and Record Load Elongation Data]
    P7 --> P8{Load Reaches Maximum}
    P8 --> P9[Continue Until Specimen Fractures]
    P9 --> P10[Step 8 Remove Broken Halves Carefully]
    P10 --> P11[Step 9 Measure Final Length Lf and Final Diameter df]
    P11 --> P12[Step 10 Compute All Tensile Properties]
    P12 --> P13[Step 11 Plot Stress Strain Curve]
    P13 --> P14[Step 12 Tabulate Results and Verify]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Standard Tensile Specimen Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Head Section] --> B[Shoulder Radius Transition]
    B --> C[Reduced Gauge Section Length L0]
    C --> D[Shoulder Radius Transition]
    D --> E[Tail Head Section]
    C --> F[Note Diameter d0 measured here]
    A --> G[Gripped in UTM Jaw]
    E --> H[Gripped in UTM Jaw]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Specimen Geometry — Round Proportional Type (per IS 10182):**
> - Gauge length: $L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0}$
> - Reduced section diameter: $d_0$
> - Shoulder fillet radius: $r \geq 0.75 d_0$
> - Total length: $\approx L_0 + 2 \times (\text{shoulder length}) + 2 \times (\text{head length})$

## 4.4 Schematic — Stress–Strain Curve for Non-Ferrous Material

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Origin O] --> B[Linear Elastic Region Slope E]
    B --> C[Proportional Limit]
    C --> D[Yielding Begins Curved]
    D --> E[0.2 percent Proof Stress Yield Point]
    E --> F[Strain Hardening Region]
    F --> G[Ultimate Tensile Strength Peak]
    G --> H[Necking Region Stress Drops]
    H --> I[Fracture Point Final Break]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** For a non-ferrous metal (e.g., aluminium), note the **smooth, gradual transition** from elastic to plastic (no flat plateau like mild steel). The yield point is **artificially defined** as the 0.2% offset intersection — this is why graph reading accuracy is critical for the lab exam.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** Define Universal Testing Machine (UTM). List any four mechanical properties that can be determined using it.
`[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**
> A **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** is a load-applying device capable of performing multiple mechanical tests (tensile, compressive, bending, shear) on standardized specimens by interchanging fixtures.
>
> **Four mechanical properties determined:**
> 1. Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)
> 2. Yield Strength / 0.2% Proof Stress
> 3. Percentage Elongation
> 4. Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity)
>
> *(Other acceptable answers: % Reduction in Area, Modulus of Resilience, Poisson's Ratio, Shear Strength.)* **[3 Marks]**

---

### **Question 2:** Why is the **0.2% proof stress** used as the yield criterion for non-ferrous metals instead of the **yield point** used for mild steel?
`[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**
> Non-ferrous metals such as aluminium, copper, and brass **do not exhibit a sharply defined yield point** in their stress–strain curve. The transition from elastic to plastic deformation is **gradual and continuous**. Hence, an arbitrary but standardized offset method is used.
>
> Per **IS 10182**, a line is drawn parallel to the initial linear elastic portion, offset by **0.2% strain** along the strain axis. The stress corresponding to the intersection of this line with the actual stress-strain curve is termed the **0.2% Proof Stress**, taken as the yield strength. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B questions of 14 marks carry **internal choice** between two alternatives. Sub-parts (a) and (b) each carry **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model]`

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)**. Mention the role of the **load cell** and **extensometer**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An aluminium specimen of diameter **25 mm** and gauge length **75 mm** was tested in a UTM. The maximum load recorded was **75 kN**, and the final gauge length after fracture was **88 mm** with a final minimum diameter of **18 mm**. Calculate:
  (i) Ultimate Tensile Strength
  (ii) Percentage Elongation
  (iii) Percentage Reduction in Area **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Construction and Working of UTM (7 Marks)**

**Construction (Key Components):**
1. **Loading Frame** — A robust two-column or four-column frame, typically made of high-grade cast iron or steel, to withstand reaction forces.
2. **Hydraulic / Servo Actuator** — Generates the controlled axial load (hydraulic cylinder with oil pump OR servo motor + ball screw).
3. **Crosshead / Movable Platform** — Transmits load to the specimen; moves at a controlled rate.
4. **Gripping Jaws** — Interchangeable fixtures (tensile, compression, bending) suited to specimen type.
5. **Load Cell / Proving Ring** — Measures the applied force with high accuracy (typically ± 1%).
6. **Extensometer** — A strain-gauge or LVDT-based device attached to the specimen's gauge length to measure elongation precisely (resolution: 0.001 mm).
7. **Control Unit + DAQ** — Records data and plots the Load–Extension curve in real time.

**Working:**
The specimen is mounted between the upper and lower jaws, ensuring axial alignment. The actuator applies a gradually increasing load at a controlled rate. The load cell records force $F(t)$, while the extensometer records extension $\Delta L(t)$. These are converted to engineering stress and strain, generating the stress–strain curve. The test continues until fracture, after which final measurements are taken.

**Role of Load Cell:** Converts mechanical force into a proportional electrical signal via strain gauges bonded to a calibrated elastic element.
**Role of Extensometer:** Measures small elongations (typically within the elastic and early plastic region) with high precision — far better than cross-head displacement, which includes grip slippage and frame deflection. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $d_0 = 25 \text{ mm}$, $L_0 = 75 \text{ mm}$
- $F_{max} = 75 \text{ kN} = 75\,000 \text{ N}$
- $L_f = 88 \text{ mm}$, $d_f = 18 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Initial cross-sectional area:**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi \, d_0^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 25^2}{4} = \frac{625\pi}{4} = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2$$

**(i) Ultimate Tensile Strength:**

$$\sigma_{UTS} = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{75\,000}{490.87} = 152.79 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Percentage Elongation:**

$$\%\,El = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{88 - 75}{75} \times 100 = \frac{13}{75} \times 100 = 17.33\,\%$$

**(iii) Percentage Reduction in Area:**

$$A_f = \frac{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 18^2}{4} = \frac{324\pi}{4} = 254.47 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\%\,RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{490.87 - 254.47}{490.87} \times 100 = 48.16\,\%$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**
| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| UTS | **152.79 MPa** |
| % Elongation | **17.33 %** |
| % Reduction in Area | **48.16 %** |

**[7 Marks]**

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (Alternative to Question A) (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 / Model]`

**(a)** Draw a typical **stress–strain curve for a non-ferrous metal** (e.g., aluminium). Label all salient points and regions. Explain why the **0.2% proof stress** is used in place of the yield point for such materials. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A brass specimen tested in a UTM gave the following readings in the linear elastic region. Determine the **Young's Modulus** of the brass.
- Diameter $d_0 = 15 \text{ mm}$
- Gauge length $L_0 = 60 \text{ mm}$
- Load $F = 18 \text{ kN}$ caused an extension $\Delta L = 0.040 \text{ mm}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Stress–Strain Curve for Non-Ferrous Metal (7 Marks)**

**Description of the curve (key regions labeled):**
- **O → A**: Linear elastic region (Hooke's Law valid, slope = E).
- **A**: Proportional limit.
- **A → B**: Elastic limit / onset of plastic deformation (gradual for non-ferrous).
- **B**: 0.2% Proof Stress (yield criterion for non-ferrous).
- **B → C**: Strain hardening region.
- **C**: Ultimate Tensile Strength (peak).
- **C → D**: Necking region (stress drops in engineering curve).
- **D**: Fracture point.

**Why 0.2% Proof Stress?**
Non-ferrous metals do **not** display a sharply defined yield plateau (unlike mild steel). The transition is **smooth and gradual**. Hence, drawing an offset line (parallel to the elastic slope, starting from 0.002 strain) and reading the stress at its intersection with the actual curve provides a **standardized, reproducible** yield value as codified in **IS 10182**. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b) Young's Modulus of Brass (7 Marks)**

**Given:** $d_0 = 15$ mm, $L_0 = 60$ mm, $F = 18$ kN, $\Delta L = 0.040$ mm.

**Step 1 — Initial area:**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi \, d_0^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 15^2}{4} = \frac{225\pi}{4} = 176.71 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Engineering stress:**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{18\,000}{176.71} = 101.86 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{0.040}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Young's Modulus:**

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{101.86}{6.667 \times 10^{-4}} = 152\,800 \text{ MPa} \approx 152.8 \text{ GPa}$$

> **Note:** Acceptable range for brass: **90–125 GPa** (depending on composition). The result is high because we used a single data point; for accuracy, the slope is best obtained from the **best-fit line** of multiple linear-region points. **[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Tensile Test Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kN → N** when computing stress. Resulting answer is off by 1000×. *Always verify units!*
> 2. **Using $d_f$ instead of $A_f$ in %RA** without squaring the ratio. The correct formula is $(A_0 - A_f)/A_0 = (d_0^2 - d_f^2)/d_0^2$.
> 3. **Confusing gauge length $L_0$ with total specimen length.** Only the *marked gauge length* is used in % elongation.
> 4. **Reporting UTS in GPa instead of MPa.** Always report in MPa unless specified (typical tensile strengths of non-ferrous are 100–500 MPa).
> 5. **Drawing the stress-strain curve for non-ferrous as if it were mild steel.** *Always* show a smooth yielding curve and label the 0.2% offset line for non-ferrous.
> 6. **Skipping units in tabular answers** → partial marks lost even if the numerical answer is correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save this before the exam!)**

- **UTM** = Universal Testing Machine. Can do tension, compression, bending, shear — only the *grip/jig* changes.
- **Two main types**: Hydraulic (high capacity) & Electromechanical (precision, non-ferrous labs).
- **Specimens** for non-ferrous metals are machined per **IS 10182: 1982** with $L_0 = 5.65\sqrt{A_0}$ for proportional type.
- **Six key outputs** of a tensile test on non-ferrous metal:
  1. $\sigma_{UTS} = F_{max} / A_0$
  2. $\sigma_{0.2}$ (proof stress — from offset construction)
  3. $E$ (slope of linear elastic region)
  4. $\%\,El = (L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$
  5. $\%\,RA = (A_0 - A_f)/A_0 \times 100$
  6. The full **stress–strain curve**.
- **No yield plateau** for non-ferrous → use **0.2% offset method**.
- **Engineering stress** uses $A_0$ (constant); **true stress** uses $A_i$ (instantaneous).
- Always convert units: **kN → N**, **GPa → MPa** as needed.
- Always **report** to **2 decimal places** and include **units** in tabular results.
- Watch out for **% RA vs % Elongation**: they measure different aspects of ductility.
- The **proportional gauge length** formula $L_0 = 5.65\sqrt{A_0}$ is asked repeatedly in viva — memorize it.
- **Necking** begins at UTS; after this, engineering stress drops but true stress rises.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Universal Testing Machine (UTM) for Non-Ferrous Materials**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and Experiment on Vickers Hardness Testing Machine to Determine Hardness of the Given Specimen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL507 - QUALITY AND MATERIAL TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and Experiment on Vickers Hardness Testing Machine to Determine Hardness of the Given Specimen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vickers Hardness (HV):** The Vickers hardness test is a standardized indentation hardness test defined under **IS 1501, ASTM E92, and ISO 6507** in which a **square-based diamond pyramidal indenter** with a face angle of **136°** is pressed into the surface of a specimen under a predetermined test load. The Vickers hardness number (HV) is calculated as the ratio of the applied load to the **surface area of the indentation** (not the projected area), measured from the mean diagonal length of the square impression left on the specimen.

The test is officially termed the **"Diamond Pyramid Hardness Test"** in older standards and is currently known as the **Vickers Hardness Test (VHT)**. It is one of the most versatile hardness testing methods because it can be performed on **virtually any solid material**—metals, ceramics, composites, thin foils, surface coatings, and even individual microstructural phases—across a load range from **1 gf to 100 kgf** (or up to 120 kgf in macro-Vickers).

$$ HV = \frac{2F \sin(\theta/2)}{d^2} = \frac{1.854 \cdot F}{d^2} $$

where:
- $F$ = Applied test load (in **kgf**)
- $d$ = Mean diagonal of the square indentation (in **mm**)
- $\theta$ = Angle between opposite faces of the diamond indenter = **136°**
- The constant **1.854** is a standard geometric conversion factor

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine pushing a sharpened square-based pyramid (like the tip of a square pencil cut diagonally) firmly into a piece of soft butter. The deeper it sinks in and the larger the square impression left behind, the *softer* the butter. Conversely, if the indentation is small and shallow, the material is *harder*. The Vickers test works on exactly this principle—but instead of butter, we use a calibrated industrial diamond, instead of pushing by hand, we apply precisely known weights via a lever, and instead of guessing the depth, we measure the exact diagonal length of the square mark using a high-magnification microscope.

The genius of the Vickers method lies in **geometric similarity**: regardless of the load applied, the shape of the indentation remains geometrically similar (a perfect square pyramid). This is because the indenter's **face angle is fixed at 136°**, which makes the Vickers hardness number **independent of the test load**. So whether you use 1 kgf, 10 kgf, or 50 kgf, the calculated HV for the same material remains consistent. This is what makes it superior to the Brinell test for comparing materials across different load conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 136°?** The indenter face angle of 136° was deliberately chosen so that the Vickers hardness numbers would numerically approximate the **Brinell Hardness Number (BHN)** for the same material in the load range of **30 kgf to 120 kgf**. This was the historical bridge between the Brinell and Vickers methods.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Indenter face angle (θ) | **136°** | Degrees |
| Indenter material | **Industrial Diamond** | — |
| Indenter tip radius | < **1 µm** | Micrometers |
| Standard test loads | 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 100, 120 | **kgf** |
| Micro-Vickers loads | 1, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200, 300, 500, 1000 | **gf** |
| Dwell time | 10 – 15 | **seconds** |
| Standard distance between indentations | **≥ 2.5 × d** | mm |
| Standard distance from edge | **≥ 2.5 × d** | mm |
| Surface roughness (Ra) for specimen | < **0.4 µm** | Micrometers |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation of the 136° Indenter

The choice of the **136° face angle** is the most critical design feature of the Vickers indenter. Let us explore why:

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Derivation Logic:** When a square-based pyramid with face angle θ is pressed into a material, the projected area (seen from above) is a square with diagonal length d. The slant face of the indentation makes a half-angle of (θ/2) with the vertical axis. The contact surface area is the area of the four inclined triangular faces of the square pyramid.

**Step-by-step logic of the Vickers principle:**

1. **Indentation Geometry:** A square-based pyramid with face angle θ is impressed into the specimen surface. The resulting impression, viewed from above, is a square with diagonals $d_1$ and $d_2$.

2. **Mean Diagonal Calculation:** The two diagonals are measured using a calibrated microscope. The arithmetic mean is taken:
   $$ d = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} $$

3. **Surface Area Calculation:** The four inclined faces of the pyramidal indentation form four isosceles triangles. The slant area of each triangle is:
   $$ A_{\text{one face}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot d \cdot l $$
   where $l$ is the slant height of the triangular face.

4. **Slant Length from Geometry:** The slant length is related to half-diagonal and face angle:
   $$ l = \frac{d/2}{\sin(\theta/2)} $$

5. **Total Contact Area:** Multiplying by 4 (four faces):
   $$ A_{\text{total}} = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(\theta/2)} $$

6. **Hardness Definition:** Vickers Hardness is the load divided by the total contact area:
   $$ HV = \frac{F}{A_{\text{total}}} = \frac{2F \sin(\theta/2)}{d^2} $$

7. **Substituting θ = 136°:** This gives the famous constant:
   $$ 2 \sin(68°) = 2 \times 0.9272 = 1.8544 $$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $HV = \dfrac{1.854 \cdot F}{d^2}$ | **Vickers Hardness Number (kgf load, mm diagonal)** | **Dimensionless** (often reported as $\text{kgf/mm}^2$) |
| 2 | $HV = \dfrac{0.1891 \cdot F_N}{d^2}$ | When load $F_N$ is in **Newtons** | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $d = \dfrac{d_1 + d_2}{2}$ | Mean diagonal of the square indentation | mm |
