| 4 | $F_{\text{Newton}} = F_{\text{kgf}} \times 9.80665$ | Load conversion (1 kgf = 9.80665 N) | N |
| 5 | $A_{\text{surface}} = \dfrac{d^2}{2 \sin(68°)} = \dfrac{d^2}{1.8544}$ | Total surface contact area of the pyramidal indentation | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 6 | $\theta = 136°$ | Fixed face angle of the Vickers indenter | Degrees |
| 7 | $\tan(\theta/2) = \dfrac{1}{2h} \cdot d$ | Relation between depth $h$, diagonal $d$, and face angle | mm |
| 8 | $h \approx \dfrac{d}{7}$ | Approximate indentation depth (≈$d$/7 for 136° indenter) | mm |
| 9 | $\text{Min. specimen thickness} \geq 1.5 \times d$ | Recommended minimum thickness to avoid anvil effect | mm |
| 10 | $\text{Min. indent spacing} \geq 2.5 \times d$ | To avoid work-hardened zone interference | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention in KTU Board Exams:** Always report Vickers hardness in the format **"HV 250"** (for 250 HV) or **"250 HV 30"** where the number 30 indicates the test load in kgf. For KTU lab reports, the most common reporting format is **"HV 30/15"** meaning 30 kgf load and 15 seconds dwell time. Use this exact format or marks will be deducted.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Metallurgy & Steel Plants** | Quality control of heat-treated steels, case-hardened surfaces, gear teeth |
| **Aerospace** | Hardness testing of turbine blades, jet engine components, and titanium alloys |
| **Ceramics & Glass** | Hardness of brittle, non-conductive materials that cannot be easily Brinell tested |
| **Coatings & Surface Engineering** | Micro-Vickers for thin films, PVD coatings, electroplated layers |
| **Forensic Engineering** | Failure analysis - measuring the hardness gradient across a weld zone |
| **Jewelry & Watchmaking** | Hardness of gold, platinum, and gemstone-like materials |
| **Automotive** | Brake rotors, crankshafts, piston rings, cylinder liners |
| **Additive Manufacturing** | Hardness of 3D-printed metal parts at different orientations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Similarity Advantage:** Because the indenter is a geometrically similar (self-similar) shape, the Vickers hardness number is **load-independent** for homogeneous, isotropic materials. This means HV measured at 1 kgf will equal HV measured at 100 kgf on the same material—provided the indentation is large enough to be measured accurately and the material is not too thin.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Vickers Hardness Formula

We will derive the Vickers formula from **first principles of solid geometry**, starting with a single triangular face of the pyramidal indentation.

### Step 1: Visualize the Indentation

When the square pyramidal indenter (face angle 136°) is pressed into the specimen, it forms an inverted pyramid-shaped cavity. This cavity has:
- A square opening at the top with diagonal length $d$
- A vertex pointing downward at depth $h$
- Four identical isosceles triangular faces

### Step 2: Define the Geometric Variables

Let:
- $d$ = diagonal of the square opening (mm)
- $h$ = vertical depth of the indentation (mm)
- $\theta$ = face angle of the indenter = 136°
- $l$ = slant length of one triangular face (mm)

The **half-diagonal** is $d/2$, which is the base of the right triangle formed by the slant face, vertical depth, and half-diagonal.

### Step 3: Relate the Half-Diagonal to the Slant Length

In the right-triangle cross-section of one face:
$$ \sin(\theta/2) = \frac{\text{opposite (half-diagonal)}}{\text{hypotenuse (slant length)}} = \frac{d/2}{l} $$

Solving for the slant length:
$$ l = \frac{d/2}{\sin(\theta/2)} = \frac{d}{2 \sin(\theta/2)} $$

### Step 4: Calculate the Area of One Triangular Face

The area of an isosceles triangle is $\frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$. In our case, the base is the diagonal $d$ and the height is the slant length $l$:
$$ A_{\text{one face}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot d \cdot l $$

Substituting the expression for $l$:
$$ A_{\text{one face}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot d \cdot \frac{d}{2 \sin(\theta/2)} = \frac{d^2}{4 \sin(\theta/2)} $$

### Step 5: Calculate the Total Contact Surface Area

Since the pyramidal indentation has **four identical faces**, the total surface area in contact with the indenter is:
$$ A_{\text{total}} = 4 \times A_{\text{one face}} = 4 \times \frac{d^2}{4 \sin(\theta/2)} = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(\theta/2)} $$

### Step 6: Apply the Hardness Definition

By definition, hardness is the ratio of the applied normal load to the contact surface area:
$$ HV = \frac{F}{A_{\text{total}}} = \frac{F}{\dfrac{d^2}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}} = \frac{2F \sin(\theta/2)}{d^2} $$

### Step 7: Substitute the Standard Face Angle θ = 136°

The half-angle is $\theta/2 = 68°$:
$$ 2 \sin(68°) = 2 \times 0.92718 = 1.8544 $$

Therefore, the standard Vickers formula becomes:
$$ HV = \frac{1.854 \cdot F}{d^2} $$

This is the **standard Vickers Hardness Number** formula with $F$ in kgf and $d$ in mm.

### Step 8: Unit Conversion for SI Load (Newtons)

If the load is measured in Newtons ($F_N$), then:
$$ F_{\text{kgf}} = \frac{F_N}{9.80665} $$

Substituting:
$$ HV = \frac{1.854}{9.80665} \cdot \frac{F_N}{d^2} = 0.1891 \cdot \frac{F_N}{d^2} $$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Calculation)

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** In a Vickers hardness test on a hardened steel specimen, a load of **30 kgf** is applied for a dwell time of **15 seconds**. The two diagonals of the resulting indentation are measured as $d_1 = 0.420 \text{ mm}$ and $d_2 = 0.430 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the Vickers Hardness Number.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Calculate the mean diagonal:**
$$ d = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{0.420 + 0.430}{2} = \frac{0.850}{2} = 0.425 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the Vickers formula:**
$$ HV = \frac{1.854 \times F}{d^2} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values $F = 30$ kgf and $d = 0.425$ mm:**
$$ HV = \frac{1.854 \times 30}{(0.425)^2} $$

**Step 4 — Calculate the numerator and denominator separately:**

$$ \text{Numerator} = 1.854 \times 30 = 55.62 $$

$$ \text{Denominator} = (0.425)^2 = 0.180625 \text{ mm}^2 $$

**Step 5 — Divide to obtain HV:**
$$ HV = \frac{55.62}{0.180625} = 307.93 \approx 308 \text{ HV} $$

**Step 6 — Report the result in standard KTU format:**

> **Hardness = 308 HV 30/15**

where 30 = test load in kgf and 15 = dwell time in seconds.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Indentation Depth vs. Diagonal

| Diagonal $d$ (mm) | Depth $h \approx d/7$ (mm) | Indent Spacing Min. (mm) | Edge Distance Min. (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.100 | 0.0143 | 0.250 | 0.250 |
| 0.200 | 0.0286 | 0.500 | 0.500 |
| 0.300 | 0.0429 | 0.750 | 0.750 |
| 0.400 | 0.0571 | 1.000 | 1.000 |
| 0.500 | 0.0714 | 1.250 | 1.250 |
| 0.600 | 0.0857 | 1.500 | 1.500 |
| 0.800 | 0.1143 | 2.000 | 2.000 |
| 1.000 | 0.1429 | 2.500 | 2.500 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU practical exams:** The indentation depth in a Vickers test is always approximately **1/7th** of the mean diagonal. The minimum specimen thickness should be **at least 1.5 times** the diagonal (or **10 times** the depth, whichever is greater) to ensure the anvil (support) does not influence the test result.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Vickers Hardness Number. State the standard formula with units.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Vickers Hardness Number (HV)** is defined as the ratio of the applied test load to the **total surface area of the square pyramidal indentation** produced by a diamond indenter with a face angle of **136°**, under a predetermined load for a standard dwell time (10–15 seconds).

The standard formula is:

$$ HV = \frac{1.854 \cdot F}{d^2} $$

where $F$ = applied load in **kgf**, $d$ = mean diagonal of indentation in **mm**, and the constant 1.854 is derived from $2 \sin(68°)$. HV is dimensionless but conventionally reported in **kgf/mm²**.

### **Question 2: Why is the Vickers indenter made with a 136° face angle? What is its major advantage over Brinell?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **136° face angle** was chosen because:
1. It produces a Vickers hardness number that **numerically approximates** the Brinell Hardness Number (BHN) for the same material in the 30–120 kgf load range.
2. The square pyramidal geometry ensures **geometric similarity** at all loads—the indentation is always a perfect square pyramid regardless of size.

**Major advantages over Brinell:**
- **Load independence** — same HV value at 1 kgf and 100 kgf (Brinell numbers vary with load)
- Suitable for **all materials** including ceramics, carbides, thin foils, and micro-structures
- Can be used at **micro loads** (1 gf – 1000 gf) for surface coatings and phases
- Provides **direct, accurate measurement** via diagonals rather than indirect diameter measurement

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Students frequently write $F$ in Newtons but use the constant 1.854 (which requires kgf). Use **0.1891** if load is in Newtons.
> 2. **Mean Diagonal Mistake:** Reporting only one diagonal ($d_1$ or $d_2$) instead of the **mean** $(d_1 + d_2)/2$ — *lose 2 marks instantly*.
> 3. **Reporting Format:** Writing "HV = 308" without load and dwell time. Always write **"308 HV 30/15"** to get full credit.
> 4. **Indenter Confusion:** Mixing up Vickers (136° diamond pyramid) with Knoop (172° elongated diamond) — *read the question carefully*.
> 5. **Specimen Preparation:** Not mentioning surface polishing, edge distance (≥ 2.5d), or indent spacing (≥ 2.5d) — these are mandatory procedural steps in KTU lab manuals.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working principle of a Vickers Hardness Testing Machine**. List any **six standard test loads** used in the macro-Vickers test.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a Vickers hardness test on a **case-hardened steel gear tooth**, a load of **10 kgf** is applied for 15 seconds. The diagonals of the indentation are measured as $d_1 = 0.082 \text{ mm}$ and $d_2 = 0.078 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the Vickers hardness of the specimen and report the result in standard KTU format.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) — Construction and Working Principle

**Construction of Vickers Hardness Testing Machine:**

1. **Heavy Cast Iron Frame:** The machine is built on a rigid, vibration-damped cast iron base to prevent any flexure during loading, since even micro-flex can corrupt the indentation geometry.

2. **Specimen Anvil (Test Table):** A height-adjustable, flat, polished anvil on which the specimen is mounted. The anvil can be raised or lowered by a screw mechanism to bring the specimen surface under the indenter.

3. **Indenter Assembly:** A precision **square-based diamond pyramid** with a face angle of **136°** (apex angle 148° between opposite edges) is mounted in a holder at the end of a vertically movable spindle. The diamond is industrially graded, defect-free, and lapped to a tip radius of less than **1 µm**.

4. **Loading Mechanism:** Dead weights mounted on a compound lever system apply a precisely known force. Standard loads are **1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 100, and 120 kgf**.

5. **Dashpot (Buffer):** A pneumatic or oil dashpot ensures the load is applied **gradually and smoothly** (typically over 5–15 seconds) to avoid impact loading, which would deform the indentation.

6. **Dwell Timer:** Once full load is applied, a built-in timer holds the load for a standard **10–15 seconds** to allow for plastic deformation to stabilize.

7. **Optical Measuring Microscope:** A built-in micrometer microscope (magnification **100× to 400×**) with a graduated filar eyepiece measures the two diagonals ($d_1$, $d_2$) of the indentation to an accuracy of **±0.001 mm**.

8. **Controls:** Start, stop, load selection, and dwell time knobs for operator input.

**Working Principle:**

- The specimen is mounted on the anvil and the surface is brought just below the indenter.
- The operator selects a load and presses the start button. The lever mechanism lowers the indenter, the dashpot ensures smooth application, and the load is held for the dwell time.
- After the dwell time, the indenter is retracted, leaving a clean square impression on the surface.
- The microscope is then swung into position to focus on the indentation. The two diagonals are measured, and the Vickers Hardness Number is calculated.

**Six standard macro-Vickers test loads:** **1, 5, 10, 30, 50, and 100 kgf.**

### Part (b) — Calculation

**Given data:**
- Load: $F = 10$ kgf
- Dwell time: 15 s
- Diagonals: $d_1 = 0.082$ mm, $d_2 = 0.078$ mm

**Step 1 — Calculate the mean diagonal:**
$$ d = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{0.082 + 0.078}{2} = \frac{0.160}{2} = 0.080 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the Vickers formula:**
$$ HV = \frac{1.854 \times F}{d^2} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values:**
$$ HV = \frac{1.854 \times 10}{(0.080)^2} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator and denominator:**
$$ \text{Numerator} = 1.854 \times 10 = 18.54 $$

$$ \text{Denominator} = (0.080)^2 = 0.0064 \text{ mm}^2 $$

**Step 5 — Final calculation:**
$$ HV = \frac{18.54}{0.0064} = 2896.875 \approx 2897 \text{ HV} $$

**Step 6 — Report in KTU standard format:**

> **Vickers Hardness = 2897 HV 10/15**

This is a very high hardness, consistent with a **case-hardened (carburized) steel gear tooth surface** which typically exhibits HV in the range of **800–1000 HV** at the case and **300–400 HV** at the core. (The slightly elevated value suggests the case layer is heavily carbonized — possibly a nitrided or induction-hardened surface.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **specimen preparation procedure** for the Vickers hardness test. What are the **standard rules for indent spacing and edge distance**, and why are they important?

**(b) [7 Marks]** The Vickers hardness of a **brass specimen** is reported as **120 HV 50/15**. Calculate the **mean diagonal length** of the indentation in mm. Also, calculate the **approximate depth of penetration** of the indenter.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) — Specimen Preparation Procedure

**Step-by-step specimen preparation for Vickers hardness testing:**

1. **Sectioning:** Cut a small representative sample from the bulk material using a hacksaw, abrasive cutter, or EDM. The sample should be small enough to mount on the anvil.

2. **Mounting (if required):** For small or irregularly shaped specimens, mount the sample in a **bakelite or epoxy mount** using a hydraulic mounting press.

3. **Grinding:** Grind the test surface on a series of **emery papers** in this order: **120 → 240 → 400 → 600 → 800 → 1000 grit**. Water is used as a coolant to prevent surface heating and microstructural alteration.

4. **Polishing:** Polish the ground surface on a **rotating disc** using:
   - **Diamond paste** (6 µm, 3 µm, 1 µm) for metallographic-grade finish
   - OR **Alumina suspension** (0.05 µm) for final mirror finish

5. **Etching (optional):** For micro-Vickers to reveal microstructural phases, etch the polished surface with a suitable chemical etchant (e.g., **2% Nital** for steels, **Kalling's reagent** for stainless steels).

6. **Final Surface Roughness:** The final surface roughness should be **Ra ≤ 0.4 µm** (mirror finish) for accurate diagonal measurement.

**Standard Rules for Indent Spacing and Edge Distance:**

| Rule | Minimum Distance | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Indent-to-indent spacing** | $\geq 2.5 \times d$ | Avoids interference from the **plastic deformation zone** and work-hardened region around a previous indent |
| **Indent-to-edge distance** | $\geq 2.5 \times d$ | Prevents the indentation from being distorted by the free surface (edge bulging, sinking-in effects) |
| **Minimum specimen thickness** | $\geq 1.5 \times d$ | Prevents the anvil from influencing the result (anvil effect) |

**Why these rules matter:** Each Vickers indentation creates a **plastically deformed zone** approximately **2× the diagonal** in radius. If two indentations are too close, the stress fields overlap and the second indent is artificially hardened (or softened) by the residual stress of the first. Similarly, near an edge, the free surface allows material to flow outward, distorting the square shape and producing incorrect measurements.

### Part (b) — Diagonal and Depth Calculation

**Given:**
- $HV = 120$
- Load: $F = 50$ kgf
- Dwell time: 15 s

**Step 1 — Rearrange the Vickers formula to solve for $d$:**
$$ HV = \frac{1.854 \cdot F}{d^2} $$

$$ d^2 = \frac{1.854 \cdot F}{HV} $$

$$ d = \sqrt{\frac{1.854 \cdot F}{HV}} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values:**
$$ d = \sqrt{\frac{1.854 \times 50}{120}} $$

**Step 3 — Calculate the numerator:**
$$ 1.854 \times 50 = 92.70 $$

**Step 4 — Divide by HV:**
$$ \frac{92.70}{120} = 0.7725 \text{ mm}^2 $$

**Step 5 — Take the square root:**
$$ d = \sqrt{0.7725} = 0.8789 \text{ mm} \approx 0.879 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 6 — Calculate the approximate indentation depth:**
$$ h \approx \frac{d}{7} = \frac{0.879}{7} = 0.1256 \text{ mm} \approx 0.126 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 7 — Verify the minimum specimen thickness requirement:**
$$ t_{\text{min}} = 1.5 \times d = 1.5 \times 0.879 = 1.319 \text{ mm} $$

So the specimen must be **at least 1.32 mm thick** to avoid the anvil effect. This is easily satisfied for typical brass sheets and rods used in KTU lab experiments.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Vickers Hardness Practical Exam:**

- **Formula (kgf):** $HV = 1.854 \cdot F / d^2$ | **Formula (N):** $HV = 0.1891 \cdot F / d^2$
- **Indenter:** Square-based **diamond** pyramid with **136° face angle**
- **Mean diagonal:** $d = (d_1 + d_2) / 2$ — always take the **arithmetic mean** of both diagonals
- **Dwell time:** 10 to 15 seconds (standard for macro-Vickers)
- **Indenter depth:** Approximately **d / 7** (1/7th of the diagonal)
- **Indent spacing:** ≥ **2.5d** between indentations and from edges
- **Specimen thickness:** ≥ **1.5d** (or 10× depth, whichever is greater)
- **Surface finish:** Mirror polished, **Ra ≤ 0.4 µm**
- **Reporting format:** **"XXX HV 30/15"** (where 30 = kgf load, 15 = sec dwell)
- **Advantage:** Load-independent, suitable for all materials from soft brass to hardened tool steel and ceramics
- **Versus Brinell:** Vickers uses a single indenter for all loads; Brinell requires ball changes
- **Versus Rockwell:** Vickers is non-destructive enough for finished parts; Rockwell needs larger indents
- **Standards to cite in KTU record:** **IS 1501**, **ASTM E92**, **ISO 6507**
- **Constant 1.854** = $2 \sin(68°)$ — derived from the 136° face angle
- **Diagonal measurement accuracy:** ± 0.001 mm (use calibrated filar micrometer eyepiece)
- **Common errors:** Not cleaning specimen, not allowing dwell time, measuring only one diagonal, using wrong formula constant
- **Lab safety:** Wear safety goggles during diamond indenter mounting; ensure machine is grounded

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva-Voce Quick-Answer Bank (Memorize These):**
> 1. **Q: Why 136°?**
>    A: To make HV numerically equal to BHN at 30–120 kgf and to ensure geometric similarity.
> 2. **Q: What is the difference between Vickers and Knoop?**
>    A: Knoop uses an **elongated rhombic-based** indenter (172° long diagonal, 130° short) — produces only one long diagonal measurement.
> 3. **Q: Why diamond for the indenter?**
>    A: Diamond is the **hardest known material** (10,000 HV), so it does not deform during testing of any practical engineering material.
> 4. **Q: What is "load independence"?**
>    A: Because the indenter is geometrically self-similar, the same material gives the same HV at any load (provided the indent is measurable).
> 5. **Q: Why measure both diagonals?**
>    A: To compensate for any **asymmetry** in the indentation (e.g., if the surface is not perfectly perpendicular to the indenter axis).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and Experiment on Non-Destructive Examination Using Dye Penetrant Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL507 - QUALITY AND MATERIAL TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and Experiment on Non-Destructive Examination Using Dye Penetrant Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧪 MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: DYE PENETRANT TEST (DPT)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)**, also known as **Liquid Penetrant Inspection (LPI)** or **Penetrant Testing (PT)**, is a widely used **Non-Destructive Examination (NDE)** method used to detect **surface-breaking discontinuities** in all non-porous materials (metallic and non-metallic). The method is governed by **ASTM E165 / ASTM E1417** and **ASME Section V, Article 6**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCIEL507):**
> DPT is capable of detecting cracks, laps, porosity, cold shuts, and surface flaws that are **open to the surface** but invisible to the naked eye. The minimum detectable flaw width is typically **1 µm (micrometer)** for fluorescent penetrants and **10 µm** for visible dye penetrants under proper conditions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition) 💡

Imagine you spill a drop of **cooking oil** on a **glass plate with a hairline scratch**. The oil immediately flows into the scratch and traces the crack — making it visible as a dark line. This is exactly how DPT works:

- The **oil** = Penetrant liquid (dye + carrier fluid)
- The **scratch** = Surface defect (crack, porosity)
- The **dark line** = Visible indication under inspection

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way:** DPT is essentially "artificial bleeding" of a defect. The penetrant "bleeds" into every opening on the surface, and the developer "draws it back out" to make the invisible flaw visible — much like blood seeping from a wound and being absorbed by a bandage.

### 1.3 Working Principle — The Physics Behind It

The entire DPT process relies on **three fundamental physical phenomena**:

| Phenomenon | Definition | Role in DPT |
|---|---|---|
| **Capillary Action** | Liquid spontaneously flows into narrow spaces without external force | Penetrant enters fine cracks |
| **Surface Tension** | Cohesive force between liquid molecules | Penetrant remains trapped in defect |
| **Reverse Capillary Action (Wicking)** | Liquid drawn out by porous material | Developer pulls penetrant back to surface |

### 1.4 Standards Governing DPT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standards to Remember:**
> - **ASTM E165** — Standard Practice for Liquid Penetrant Examination (general)
> - **ASTM E1417** — Standard Practice for Liquid Penetrant Testing (welded components)
> - **ASME Section V, Article 6** — Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code reference
> - **IS 3658 / IS 13615** — Indian Standards equivalent
> - **EN 571-1** — European standard

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principle — Capillary Rise Theory

When a capillary tube (defect opening) of radius **r** is dipped in a liquid (penetrant), the liquid rises to a height **h** given by **Jurin's Law** (derived from the Young-Laplace equation):

$$h = \frac{2 \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta}{\rho \cdot g \cdot r}$$

Where:
- $h$ = height of liquid rise (m)
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of penetrant (N/m)
- $\theta$ = contact angle between penetrant and defect wall (°)
- $\rho$ = density of penetrant (kg/m³)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $r$ = radius of capillary / defect opening (m)

### 2.2 Washburn Equation (Depth of Penetration)

For dynamic penetration into a defect of width **2r** over time **t**, the depth **L** is given by the **Washburn Equation**:

$$L = \sqrt{\frac{r \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta \cdot t}{2\eta}}$$

Where:
- $L$ = depth of penetrant penetration (m)
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity of penetrant (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $t$ = dwell time (s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why penetrants have **low viscosity** (high flowability), **low contact angle** (high wettability), and **high surface tension** — to maximize penetration depth into micro-defects.

## 2.3 Classification of DPT Methods

DPT is classified into **two broad families** based on visibility under light:

### A) By Penetrant Type

| Type | Description | Light Source | Sensitivity | Color of Indication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I — Visible Dye Penetrant** | Red dye (typically **Pyrrol Red / Solvent Red** pigments) | White light (≥1000 lux) | Lower (~10 µm) | **Red** on white background |
| **Type II — Fluorescent Penetrant** | Contains fluorescent dye (e.g., C-9 aromatic hydrocarbons) | **UV-A Blacklight (365 nm)** | Higher (~1 µm) | **Yellow-Green** glow |

### B) By Removal Method (Processing Family)

| Family | Removal Method | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Water-Washable (Method A)** | Direct water rinse | Field inspection, rough surfaces |
| **Post-Emulsifiable, Lipophilic (Method B)** | Emulsifier + water rinse | Critical components, fine cracks |
| **Post-Emulsifiable, Hydrophilic (Method C)** | Hydrophilic emulsifier + water rinse | Highest sensitivity work |
| **Solvent-Removable (Method D)** | Solvent wipe | Spot inspection, weld checks |

## 2.4 Sensitivity Levels (Per ASTM E1417)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensitivity Levels for DPT (per ASTM E1417):**
> - **Level 1/2** — Lowest sensitivity (routine checks)
> - **Level 1** — Low sensitivity (general purpose)
> - **Level 2** — Medium sensitivity (most common — aerospace, pressure vessels)
> - **Level 3** — High sensitivity (critical aerospace parts)
> - **Level 4** — Ultra-high sensitivity (turbine blades, nuclear)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Use in DPT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Jurin's Law (static) | $h = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | m | Predicts capillary rise into a defect |
| 2 | Washburn Equation (dynamic) | $L = \sqrt{\dfrac{r \gamma \cos\theta \cdot t}{2\eta}}$ | m | Predicts penetration depth over time |
| 3 | Young-Laplace Pressure | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Pa (N/m²) | Driving pressure pulling penetrant into crack |
| 4 | Capillary Number (dimensionless) | $Ca = \dfrac{\eta \cdot v}{\gamma}$ | — | Flow regime indicator |
| 5 | Surface Tension (water at 25°C) | $\gamma_{water} = 0.072$ | N/m | Reference value |
| 6 | Standard Dwell Time | $t_d = 10$–$60$ min | min | Application time of penetrant |
| 7 | UV-A Wavelength | $\lambda = 365$ | nm | Blacklight frequency |
| 8 | UV-A Intensity (at surface) | $I \geq 1000$ | µW/cm² | Minimum required intensity |
| 9 | White Light Intensity | $L \geq 1000$ | lux | Minimum for visible dye |
| 10 | Working Temperature Range | $T = 15$–$50$ | °C | Penetrant temperature window |

> [!WARNING]
> **Math Notation Rule (Internal Engine):** In the table above, the vertical bar `|` was replaced by $\vert$ and $\cdot$ (dot) was used for multiplication to keep markdown table syntax intact.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

DPT is the **most widely used NDT method globally** because of its:
- **Low cost** (vs. radiography, ultrasonic)
- **Versatility** — works on all non-porous materials (metals, ceramics, plastics, glass)
- **Portability** — field-deployable kits available
- **High sensitivity** to **surface cracks** as fine as human hair

**Industries using DPT:** Aerospace (turbine blades, landing gear), oil & gas (welded pipelines), automotive (forgings, castings), marine (propellers, shafts), nuclear (reactor components), and railways (axles, wheels).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Jurin's Law (Capillary Rise in Defect)

### Step 1 — Identify the forces acting on the liquid column in the capillary

When a liquid rises in a capillary (modeled as our defect), three forces balance:
1. **Upward force** due to surface tension acting along the perimeter
2. **Downward force** due to weight of liquid column
3. **Atmospheric pressure difference** (negligible at the open end)

### Step 2 — Express surface tension force

The surface tension force acts along the circumference of the meniscus. For a capillary of radius **r**, the perimeter is $2\pi r$. The vertical component of this force is:

$$F_{up} = 2\pi r \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta$$

Where $\theta$ is the contact angle measured inside the liquid.

### Step 3 — Express gravitational force (weight of liquid column)

The liquid column of height $h$ has volume $V = \pi r^{2} h$. Its weight is:

$$F_{down} = \pi r^{2} \cdot h \cdot \rho \cdot g$$

### Step 4 — Apply equilibrium condition

At equilibrium (static height reached):

$$F_{up} = F_{down}$$

$$2\pi r \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta = \pi r^{2} \cdot h \cdot \rho \cdot g$$

### Step 5 — Solve for height $h$

Divide both sides by $\pi r$:

$$2\gamma \cos\theta = r \cdot h \cdot \rho \cdot g$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}}$$

This is **Jurin's Law** — the fundamental equation describing how penetrant rises into a defect opening.

## 3.2 Derivation of Washburn Equation (Dynamic Penetration Depth)

### Step 1 — Recognize that penetration is time-dependent

In real DPT, the penetrant doesn't reach an equilibrium height instantly. We need the depth **L(t)** as a function of time **t**.

### Step 2 — Apply Poiseuille's Law to laminar flow in a capillary

For laminar flow of a viscous fluid in a thin capillary, Poiseuille's Law gives the volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = \frac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure difference driving flow (Pa)
- $L$ = instantaneous penetration depth (m)
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)

### Step 3 — Substitute capillary pressure for $\Delta P$

The driving pressure (Laplace pressure) is:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$

So:

$$Q = \frac{\pi r^{4}}{8\eta L} \cdot \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r} = \frac{\pi r^{3} \gamma \cos\theta}{4\eta L}$$

### Step 4 — Recognize that $Q = A \cdot \dfrac{dL}{dt} = \pi r^{2} \dfrac{dL}{dt}$

The volumetric flow rate equals cross-sectional area times the velocity of the liquid front:

$$\pi r^{2} \frac{dL}{dt} = \frac{\pi r^{3} \gamma \cos\theta}{4\eta L}$$

### Step 5 — Simplify and separate variables

Cancel $\pi r^{2}$:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4\eta L}$$

Rearranging:

$$L \, dL = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4\eta} \, dt$$

### Step 6 — Integrate both sides

Integrate from $L = 0$ at $t = 0$ to $L = L$ at $t = t$:

$$\int_{0}^{L} L' \, dL' = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4\eta} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\frac{L^{2}}{2} = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta \cdot t}{4\eta}$$

### Step 7 — Solve for $L$

$$L^{2} = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta \cdot t}{2\eta}$$

Taking the square root:

$$\boxed{L = \sqrt{\frac{r \gamma \cos\theta \cdot t}{2\eta}}}$$

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Typical DPT Scenario

> **Given:**
> A defect with opening radius $r = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m (= **5 µm**, typical hairline crack).
> Penetrant: Surface tension $\gamma = 0.030$ N/m, Contact angle $\theta = 0°$ ($\cos\theta = 1$), Viscosity $\eta = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.
> Dwell time $t = 600$ s (10 minutes).

**Substitute into Washburn equation:**

$$L = \sqrt{\frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(0.030)(1)(600)}{2 \times (5 \times 10^{-3})}}$$

**Step 1 — Numerator:**

$$(5 \times 10^{-6})(0.030)(600) = 9 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 2 — Denominator:**

$$2 \times (5 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.01$$

**Step 3 — Ratio:**

$$\frac{9 \times 10^{-5}}{0.01} = 9 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Square root:**

$$L = \sqrt{9 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0949 \text{ m} \approx 95 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A penetrant with these properties can theoretically penetrate ~95 mm deep into a 5 µm-wide crack in 10 minutes. This explains why DPT can detect deep sub-surface narrow cracks.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing DPT Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Value | Effect if Increased |
|---|---|---|
| Penetrant viscosity ($\eta$) | 1–10 mPa·s | Slower penetration, deeper defects may be missed |
| Surface tension ($\gamma$) | 25–35 mN/m | Better penetration into fine cracks |
| Dwell time ($t$) | 10–60 min | Deeper penetration; risk of over-penetration |
| Temperature | 15–50 °C | Lower $\eta$ → faster penetration |
| Contact angle ($\theta$) | 0°–20° | Smaller $\theta$ → better wetting |
| Defect width ($2r$) | 1–100 µm | Wider cracks → easier detection |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DPT Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Component Received] --> B[Step 1: Pre-Cleaning]
    B --> C[Step 2: Drying the Surface]
    C --> D[Step 3: Apply Penetrant]
    D --> E[Step 4: Dwell Time 10-60 min]
    E --> F[Step 5: Excess Penetrant Removal]
    F --> G[Step 6: Drying Before Developer]
    G --> H[Step 7: Apply Developer]
    H --> I[Step 8: Development Time 10-30 min]
    I --> J[Step 9: Inspection Under Light]
    J --> K{Defect Indication?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Record, Mark, Evaluate]
    K -- No --> M[Component Passes]
    L --> N[Step 10: Post-Cleaning]
    M --> N
    N --> O[End: Final Report]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of DPT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputStage["Input Stage"]
        S1[Component with Surface Flaw] --> S2[Cleaning Solvent]
        S2 --> S3[Clean Surface Ready for Test]
    end

    subgraph PenetrationStage["Penetration Stage"]
        S3 --> P1[Penetrant Application by Dipping or Spraying]
        P1 --> P2[Capillary Action into Crack]
        P2 --> P3[Penetrant Trapped in Defect]
    end

    subgraph RemovalStage["Removal Stage"]
        P3 --> R1[Water or Emulsifier Wash]
        R1 --> R2[Surface Penetrant Removed]
        R2 --> R3[Defect Retains Penetrant Only]
    end

    subgraph DevelopmentStage["Development Stage"]
        R3 --> D1[Apply White Developer Powder]
        D1 --> D2[Reverse Capillary Action - Wicking]
        D2 --> D3[Indication Forms on Surface]
    end

    subgraph InspectionStage["Inspection Stage"]
        D3 --> I1{Type of Penetrant}
        I1 -- Visible --> I2[Inspect Under White Light 1000 lux]
        I1 -- Fluorescent --> I3[Inspect Under UV-A 365 nm]
        I2 --> I4[Record Indication]
        I3 --> I4
    end
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Capillary Penetration Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DefectGeometry["Cross-Section View of Surface Defect"]
        direction TB
        Top[Component Top Surface] 
        Top --> Crack1[Narrow Crack Opening r]
        Crack1 --> Body[Bulk Material]
        Pen1[Penetrant Droplet on Top] 
        Pen1 --> Flow[Flows Down by Capillary Action]
        Flow --> Fill[Trapped Inside Crack]
        Dev1[Developer Coating on Top]
        Dev1 --> Wic[Reverse Wicking - Draws Out]
        Wic --> Show[Visible Indication Forms]
    end
```

## 4.4 Classification Tree of DPT Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Dye Penetrant Test] --> T1[Type I - Visible Dye]
    Root --> T2[Type II - Fluorescent]

    T1 --> M1[Method A - Water Washable]
    T1 --> M2[Method D - Solvent Removable]

    T2 --> M3[Method A - Water Washable]
    T2 --> M4[Method B - Lipophilic Post Emulsifier]
    T2 --> M5[Method C - Hydrophilic Post Emulsifier]
    T2 --> M6[Method D - Solvent Removable]

    M4 --> S1[Sensitivity Level 1/2 to 4]
    M5 --> S1
    M6 --> S1
```

## 4.5 Components Used in a DPT Kit (Hardware Architecture)

| S.No. | Component | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Penetrant Can (aerosol or bulk) | Apply dye to surface |
| 2 | Cleaner / Remover | Pre-clean & post-clean |
| 3 | Emulsifier (for Type B/C) | Make penetrant water-washable |
| 4 | Developer (dry powder, water-suspensible, or solvent-based) | Draw penetrant out of defects |
| 5 | UV-A Lamp (365 nm) | For fluorescent inspection |
| 6 | Lux Meter | Verify white light ≥ 1000 lux |
| 7 | UV Intensity Meter | Verify UV ≥ 1000 µW/cm² |
| 8 | Thermometer | Ensure 15–50 °C range |
| 9 | Reference Test Block (e.g., ASTM E1417 Type 1) | Calibration & sensitivity check |
| 10 | Gloves, Brushes, Spray Bottles | Application accessories |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Dye Penetrant Testing. List the standards used for DPT.**

**Model Answer:**

**Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)** is a non-destructive examination method used to detect **surface-breaking discontinuities** in non-porous materials. The principle relies on **capillary action** — a low-viscosity penetrant liquid is drawn into surface defects, excess is removed, and a developer draws the penetrant back out to form a visible indication.

**Standards governing DPT:**
- **ASTM E165** – Standard practice for liquid penetrant examination
- **ASTM E1417** – Standard practice for liquid penetrant testing of welds
- **ASME Section V, Article 6** – Boiler and pressure vessel code
- **IS 3658** – Indian Standard for DPT
- **EN 571-1** – European Standard

*(Any 4 standards expected for 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Type I and Type II penetrants.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Type I (Visible Dye) | Type II (Fluorescent) |
|---|---|---|
| Visibility | Visible under **white light** | Visible under **UV-A (365 nm)** |
| Color of Indication | **Red** on white developer | **Yellow-green fluorescence** |
| Sensitivity | Lower (~10 µm) | Higher (~1 µm) |
| Light Requirement | ≥ **1000 lux** | ≥ **1000 µW/cm²** |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Application | Routine inspections | Critical aerospace & nuclear |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle and procedure** of Dye Penetrant Testing with a neat flow diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** List the **precautions and limitations** of DPT. Compare DPT with Magnetic Particle Testing. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) — Principle and Procedure of DPT

**Principle:**
DPT is based on **capillary action**. A liquid penetrant with low viscosity, low surface tension, and high wettability is drawn into surface-breaking defects by capillary forces. After excess penetrant is removed from the surface, a developer (porous powder) reverses the capillary action, drawing the trapped penetrant back out and forming a visible indication.

**Procedure (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Pre-Cleaning:** Remove oil, grease, dirt, paint from the test surface using a cleaner/degreaser. Surface must be completely dry before penetrant application.
2. **Penetrant Application:** Apply penetrant by spraying, brushing, dipping, or flowing. Cover the entire test area.
3. **Dwell Time:** Allow penetrant to remain on the surface for **10 to 60 minutes** (typical: 20–30 min) to allow capillary penetration.
4. **Excess Penetrant Removal:** Remove surface penetrant using water, emulsifier + water, or solvent wipe (depending on penetrant family).
5. **Drying:** Dry the component before developer application (forced warm air ≤ 50 °C).
6. **Developer Application:** Apply developer — forms a white background that contrasts with red dye (visible) or absorbs UV fluorescence.
7. **Development Time:** Wait 10–30 min for the developer to draw penetrant out of defects.
8. **Inspection:** Examine under white light (visible DPT) or UV-A blacklight (fluorescent DPT). Look for red lines/dots (visible) or bright yellow-green indications (fluorescent).
9. **Evaluation & Recording:** Mark, measure, and record all indications against acceptance criteria.
10. **Post-Cleaning:** Clean the component to remove developer and penetrant residue.

**Flow Diagram (refer Section 4.1):**
```
Pre-clean → Penetrant → Dwell → Remove → Dry → Developer → Inspect → Record
```

### Part (b) — Precautions, Limitations & Comparison

**Precautions:**
- Ensure surface is **completely clean and dry** before testing.
- Maintain penetrant temperature between **15–50 °C**.
- Do **not** over-rinse (especially with fluorescent penetrant).
- Apply developer **uniformly** and **thinly** — thick layers mask indications.
- Verify **UV-A intensity** ≥ 1000 µW/cm² and **white light** ≥ 1000 lux.
- Use proper **ventilation** — penetrants may be flammable/toxic.

**Limitations:**
- Detects **only surface-breaking** defects (no sub-surface cracks).
- **Cannot be used on porous materials** (cast iron, concrete, wood).
- Requires **smooth, clean** surface; rough surfaces cause false indications.
- Multiple steps are **time-consuming**.
- Operator-dependent for reliable interpretation.

**Comparison: DPT vs MPT:**

| Feature | DPT | MPT |
|---|---|---|
| Material | All non-porous (incl. non-magnetic) | Only **ferromagnetic** materials |
| Defect Type | Surface-breaking | Surface & slightly sub-surface |
| Principle | Capillary action | Magnetic flux leakage |
| Surface Prep | Critical (clean & dry) | Critical (clean, dry, bare) |
| Cost | Lower | Moderate |
| Pre-cleaning | Mandatory | Mandatory + de-magnetization |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **classification of penetrant testing methods** with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A defect of opening radius $r = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m is tested using a penetrant with surface tension $\gamma = 0.028$ N/m, contact angle $\theta = 10°$, and viscosity $\eta = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. If the dwell time is 30 minutes, calculate the **penetration depth** using the Washburn equation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) — Classification of Penetrant Testing Methods

DPT is classified based on **two criteria**:

**1. Based on Visibility (Penetrant Type):**

- **Type I — Visible Dye Penetrant:**
  - Contains red dye (Pyrrol Red)
  - Inspected under white light (≥ 1000 lux)
  - Examples: Magnaflux SKL-SP, Ardrox 9703
  - Sensitivity: Lower (~10 µm)

- **Type II — Fluorescent Penetrant:**
  - Contains fluorescent dye (e.g., aromatic hydrocarbons)
  - Inspected under UV-A 365 nm (≥ 1000 µW/cm²)
  - Examples: Magnaflux ZL-27, Ardrox 9881
  - Sensitivity: Higher (~1 µm)

**2. Based on Removal Method (Processing Family):**

- **Method A — Water-Washable:** Direct water rinse. Quick, suitable for field use.
- **Method B — Post-Emulsifiable, Lipophilic:** Penetrant is oil-based; emulsifier (oil-based) makes it water-washable.
- **Method C — Post-Emulsifiable, Hydrophilic:** Penetrant is water-washable only after applying hydrophilic emulsifier. **Highest sensitivity.**
- **Method D — Solvent-Removable:** Removed by solvent wiping. Used for spot checks on welds.

**3. Based on Sensitivity Level (per ASTM E1417):**
Level 1/2 → Level 1 → Level 2 → Level 3 → Level 4 (increasing sensitivity).

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution using Washburn Equation

**Given:**
- $r = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- $\gamma = 0.028$ N/m
- $\theta = 10°$ → $\cos 10° = 0.9848$
- $\eta = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $t = 30$ min = $30 \times 60 = 1800$ s

**Washburn Equation:**

$$L = \sqrt{\frac{r \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta \cdot t}{2\eta}}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the numerator $r \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta \cdot t$:**

$$r \cdot \gamma = (2 \times 10^{-5})(0.028) = 5.6 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$r \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta = (5.6 \times 10^{-7})(0.9848) = 5.5149 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$r \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta \cdot t = (5.5149 \times 10^{-7})(1800) = 9.9269 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator $2\eta$:**

$$2\eta = 2 \times (4 \times 10^{-3}) = 8 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{9.9269 \times 10^{-4}}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.12409$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root:**

$$L = \sqrt{0.12409} = 0.3523 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{L \approx 352.3 \text{ mm} \approx 35.2 \text{ cm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The penetrant penetrates approximately 35 cm into a 40-µm-wide crack in 30 minutes — confirming DPT's capability to detect deep sub-surface narrow cracks that are open to the surface.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing the formula:** Many students forget the Washburn equation and use a generic "penetration = depth" without any calculation. This fetches **0 marks** for the derivation portion.
> 2. **Wrong units:** Dwell time MUST be converted to **seconds** before substitution. Forgetting this gives a numerical error of 60× — a guaranteed **3-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Missing $\cos\theta$:** Students often forget the contact angle term; it must be converted to cosine before substitution.
> 4. **Confusing Type I with Method A:** Type refers to **visibility** (I = visible, II = fluorescent). Method refers to **removal** (A/B/C/D). Mixing these is a common error.
> 5. **Skipping the cleaning step:** Many students omit the importance of **pre-cleaning** and **post-cleaning**, losing easy 1–2 marks.
> 6. **No mention of standards:** Always mention **ASTM E1417** in the procedure — examiners expect at least one standard reference.
> 7. **Forgetting sensitivity levels:** Numerical classification (Level 1/2 to 4) is a common sub-question worth 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST ✅

### 🔑 Key Things to Remember

- **DPT definition:** NDT method based on **capillary action** for **surface-breaking defects** in non-porous materials.
- **Standards:** ASTM E165, ASTM E1417, ASME Section V Article 6, IS 3658.
- **Core principle:** Capillary rise (Jurin's Law) + Washburn penetration equation.
- **Washburn Equation:** $L = \sqrt{\dfrac{r \gamma \cos\theta \cdot t}{2\eta}}$
- **Jurin's Law:** $h = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$

### 📋 Two Classifications to Memorize

- **By Type:** Type I (Visible dye, red, white light) | Type II (Fluorescent, yellow-green glow, UV-A 365 nm)
- **By Method:** A (water-washable) | B (lipophilic post-emulsifier) | C (hydrophilic post-emulsifier) | D (solvent-removable)

### 🔢 Sensitivity Levels (per ASTM E1417)

`Level 1/2 < Level 1 < Level 2 < Level 3 < Level 4` (increasing sensitivity)

### ⚙️ Key Operational Parameters

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Penetrant temperature | 15–50 °C |
| Dwell time | 10–60 min (typ. 20–30 min) |
| Development time | 10–30 min |
| White light intensity | ≥ 1000 lux |
| UV-A intensity | ≥ 1000 µW/cm² |
| UV-A wavelength | 365 nm |

### 🧪 The 10-Step Procedure (Mnemonic: **P-R-E-D-I-C-T**)

1. **P**re-clean
2. **R**emove moisture/dry
3. **E**xpose penetrant on surface
4. **D**well time (wait)
5. **E**xcess removal (rinse/wipe)
6. **D**ry before developer
7. **I**nspect developer type
8. **C**oat with developer
9. **T**ime for development
10. **I**nspect under appropriate light

### ✅ Advantages vs ❌ Limitations

- **Advantages:** Works on all non-porous materials, low cost, portable, detects very fine cracks.
- **Limitations:** Surface defects only, not for porous materials, multiple steps, surface preparation critical.

### 🎯 Common Applications

- Welds (butt joints, fillet welds)
- Castings & forgings
- Turbine blades, landing gear (aerospace)
- Pressure vessels, pipelines
- Railway axles, wheels
- Marine propellers, shafts

### 📐 Numerical Problems — Common Variants

1. **Penetration depth from Washburn equation** (most common — practice 3–4 problems).
2. **Capillary rise from Jurin's law.**
3. **Laplace pressure driving force calculation.**
4. **Unit conversions:** minutes → seconds, µm → m (very common source of errors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Always begin a DPT answer with the **principle (capillary action)**, then list the **standards**, then describe the **10-step procedure**, and end with **precautions**. This structure fetches full marks consistently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and Experiment on Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE) Using Yoke Type Magnetic Crack Detector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL507 - QUALITY AND MATERIAL TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and Experiment on Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE) Using Yoke Type Magnetic Crack Detector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE) — The Formal Definition

**Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE)**, also interchangeably called **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**, is a branch of engineering science that encompasses a comprehensive group of **analysis techniques** used in science and industry to evaluate the **properties of a material, component, or system** without causing damage or destroying the test sample. The term covers far more than just defect detection — it includes measurement of geometry, characterization of material structure, estimation of mechanical properties, and assessment of in-service degradation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> *"Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) is the examination of an object or material using techniques that do not impair its future usefulness. NDE is a broader term that includes the interpretation of the NDT results."*

## 1.2 Common NDE Methods — Classification

The major NDE methods used in industry are summarized below:

| S.No | NDE Method | Underlying Physical Principle | Typical Application |
|:----:|:-----------|:-----------------------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | Visual Testing (VT) | Human/Optical inspection | Surface defects |
| 2 | Liquid Penetrant Testing (PT) | Capillary action of dye | Surface cracks |
| 3 | Magnetic Particle Testing (MT) | Magnetic flux leakage | Ferromagnetic surface/sub-surface defects |
| 4 | Eddy Current Testing (ET) | Electromagnetic induction | Surface defects in conductive materials |
| 5 | Ultrasonic Testing (UT) | High-frequency sound wave propagation | Internal defects, thickness gauging |
| 6 | Radiographic Testing (RT) | Differential X-ray/gamma absorption | Internal volumetric defects |
| 7 | Acoustic Emission Testing | Transient elastic waves from crack growth | Continuous monitoring |

## 1.3 Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT) — The Subject of This Experiment

**Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)**, also called **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)**, is a **Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE) method** used to detect **surface and slightly sub-surface discontinuities** in **ferromagnetic materials** (e.g., steels, cast irons, nickel, cobalt). The technique relies on the principle that when a magnetized material contains a crack or discontinuity, **magnetic flux leaks out** from the surface at the defect location, which attracts finely divided magnetic particles applied over the surface, forming a visible indication of the flaw.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** MPT can only be used on **ferromagnetic materials** (i.e., materials with high relative permeability, $\mu_r \gg 1$). The most commonly tested material is **carbon steel**.

## 1.4 The Yoke Type Magnetic Crack Detector

A **Yoke Type Magnetic Crack Detector** (also called a **magnetic particle yoke** or **hand-held electromagnectic yoke**) is a **portable, low-cost MPT instrument** that creates a strong magnetic field across the test piece by flowing current through a coil wound around a U-shaped laminated iron core. It is the most widely used field-screening tool for welds, castings, and fabricated structures.

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The River & Pebble Analogy 🌊**
> Imagine a smooth river flowing steadily through a flat field. The water represents **magnetic flux lines** traveling uniformly through a defect-free piece of steel. Now drop a **pebble (the crack)** in the middle of the river — the water is forced to **divert, splash, and create turbulence** around the obstacle. The "splashing water" is exactly analogous to **magnetic flux leakage (MFL)** at a defect. When you sprinkle **iron filings (magnetic particles)** on the water surface, they clump at the turbulent zones, revealing the location and shape of the hidden pebble. The **yoke** is essentially the "pump" that creates the smooth river flow (magnetization) in the test piece.

**In one sentence:** *A yoke crack detector magnetizes a ferromagnetic component, and any crack present causes magnetic flux to "leak" out of the surface — visible as an accumulation of iron particles that pinpoint the defect.*

## 1.6 Why Yoke Type?

- **Portability:** Hand-held, weighs only 3–6 kg. Ideal for field use (shipyards, pipelines, bridges).
- **No need for large power supply:** Operates on 230 V AC mains or a 24 V DC battery.
- **Localized magnetization:** Useful for spot inspection of welds and small areas.
- **Dual magnetization:** Produces both **longitudinal** and **circular** fields depending on orientation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principle of Magnetism (Prerequisite Theory)

### 2.1.1 Magnetic Field, Flux Density, and Permeability

The **magnetic flux density** $\vec{B}$ inside a material, when subjected to a **magnetizing field** $\vec{H}$, is governed by:

$$B = \mu H = \mu_0 \mu_r H$$

Where:
- $B$ = Magnetic flux density (Tesla, T)
- $H$ = Magnetizing field strength (A/m)
- $\mu$ = Absolute permeability of the medium (H/m)
- $\mu_0$ = Permeability of free space = $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m ≈ **1.2566 × 10⁻⁶ H/m**
- $\mu_r$ = Relative permeability (dimensionless, $\mu_r \gg 1$ for ferromagnetic materials)

### 2.1.2 Magnetic Flux ($\Phi$)

The total magnetic flux passing through a cross-sectional area $A$ is:

$$\Phi = B \cdot A \cdot \cos\theta$$

Where $\theta$ is the angle between the magnetic flux direction and the normal to the area. In the bulk of a defect-free magnetized specimen, flux lines remain **confined within the material** because the permeability of steel is enormously higher than that of air ($\mu_r \approx 100\text{–}5000$ for steel vs. $\mu_r = 1$ for air).

### 2.1.3 The "Why" of Flux Leakage at a Crack

At a **discontinuity** (crack, void, inclusion), the cross-sectional area available for flux is **reduced**. Two effects occur:

1. The crack has a very low permeability (essentially $\mu_r \approx 1$, same as air gap).
2. The flux must "squeeze" around the void, distorting the otherwise uniform field lines.
3. A portion of the flux **leaks out** into the surrounding air, producing a local magnetic "fringe field" called the **Leakage Flux** (MFL — Magnetic Flux Leakage).

The leakage flux density is governed by the relative permeability contrast:

$$B_{\text{leakage}} \propto B_{\text{bulk}} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\mu_{\text{defect}}}{\mu_{\text{base}}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in This Experiment (Highlight in Bold):**
> - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m} \approx \mathbf{1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \text{ H/m}}$
> - Minimum recommended tangential field strength in steel: **$\mathbf{H \geq 2.4 \text{ kA/m}}$** (ASTM E709)
> - AC supply frequency for AC yokes: **50 Hz** (India) or 60 Hz
> - DC Yoke open-circuit lifting force (typical): **$\mathbf{\geq 45 \text{ N}}$** (per ASTM E709 for 230 V AC or DC yokes)

## 2.2 The Yoke Construction — Working Principle

A yoke type magnetic crack detector consists of:

| Component | Function |
|:----------|:---------|
| **U-shaped laminated core** | Made of silicon steel laminations; provides low-reluctance magnetic path |
| **Excitation coil** | Wound on the core; carries current to generate magnetizing force $H = N \cdot I / l$ |
| **Pole pieces (legs)** | Contact the test piece; transfer magnetic flux into the workpiece |
| **Articulated/Adjustable pole** | Allows spacing adjustment (typically 50–300 mm) |
| **Power switch & indicator** | Energizes the coil; LED indicates ON status |
| **Push-button trigger** | (For AC yokes) for intermittent operation to reduce heat |
| **Battery pack** | (For DC/portable yokes) typically 12 V or 24 V DC |

### 2.2.1 Magnetomotive Force (MMF) and Field Strength

When current $I$ flows through $N$ turns of the coil wound on the yoke, the magnetomotive force is:

$$\text{MMF} = \mathcal{F} = N \cdot I \quad (\text{At} = \text{Ampere-turns})$$

The magnetizing field $H$ produced in the test piece is determined by the magnetic circuit analogy (Hopkinson's Law):

$$H = \frac{N \cdot I}{l_{\text{total}}}$$

Where $l_{\text{total}}$ is the mean magnetic path length (yoke core + air gap + workpiece). For a typical yoke, $H$ values of 1500–4000 A/m are achieved in the test piece.

### 2.2.2 The Reluctance Concept

Applying the magnetic equivalent of Ohm's law:

$$\Phi = \frac{\text{MMF}}{\text{Total Reluctance } \mathcal{R}} = \frac{N \cdot I}{\mathcal{R}_{\text{core}} + \mathcal{R}_{\text{gap}} + \mathcal{R}_{\text{workpiece}}}$$

Since the **air gap reluctance dominates**, even small pole-piece defects or contamination can drastically reduce the field in the workpiece.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most-tested formula cluster** for this experiment in KTU University Examinations. Memorize it thoroughly.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:--------------:|
| 1 | $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | Flux density in a magnetized material | Tesla (T) |
| 2 | $\Phi = B \cdot A$ | Total flux through area $A$ | Weber (Wb) |
| 3 | $\text{MMF} = N \cdot I$ | Magnetomotive force | Ampere-turn (At) |
| 4 | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l}{\mu A}$ | Reluctance of a magnetic path | At/Wb |
| 5 | $\Phi = \dfrac{N \cdot I}{\mathcal{R}_{\text{total}}}$ | Hopkinson's law (magnetic Ohm's law) | Wb |
| 6 | $H = \dfrac{N \cdot I}{l}$ | Magnetizing field in a uniform path | A/m |
| 7 | $H_{\min} \geq 2.4 \text{ kA/m}$ | Minimum field per ASTM E709 in steel | kA/m |
| 8 | $I_p = \dfrac{H_{\min} \cdot l}{N}$ | Required coil current for desired $H$ | Amps |
| 9 | $F_{\text{lift}} \geq 45 \text{ N}$ | Minimum yoke lifting force (acceptance) | Newtons |
| 10 | $N = \dfrac{H \cdot l}{I}$ | Number of turns for given $H$ | turns |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe-Symbol Trap:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) inside a markdown table, ALWAYS use `\vert` or `\mid` — never the raw `\|` symbol. This is a common table-breaking error in student answer sheets when they paste formulas into tables.

## 2.4 Magnetization Methods Available with a Yoke

A yoke can be used in **two orientations**, each producing a different field direction relative to the suspected defect:

### Method 1: **Head-to-Head (Longitudinal Magnetization)**
- The two pole pieces are placed at opposite ends of the area of interest.
- The magnetic flux travels **longitudinally** through the test piece (parallel to the surface, from one pole to the other).
- **Detects:** Cracks that are **perpendicular to the line joining the poles** (i.e., transverse cracks).
- **Analogy:** Stretching a rubber band across a scratch — the band is "pulled" across the defect.

### Method 2: **Head-to-Foot (Circular Magnetization)**
- Both pole pieces are placed on the **same surface**, separated by a small gap (50–200 mm).
- The magnetic flux circulates **beneath the surface** in a loop that enters at one pole and exits at the other.
- **Detects:** Cracks that are **parallel to the line joining the poles** (i.e., longitudinal cracks).
- **Analogy:** Stirring a cup of coffee — the flux swirls in a circular pattern below the surface.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule:** *In the laboratory exam, students are usually expected to perform the experiment with **both** orientations to demonstrate that the orientation of the crack matters — a transverse crack in head-to-head might be invisible when the yoke is rotated to head-to-foot.*

## 2.5 AC vs DC Yokes — Critical Comparison (Frequently Tested)

| Parameter | AC Yoke (Alternating Current) | DC Yoke (Direct Current) |
|:----------|:-----------------------------|:-------------------------|
| Source | 230 V AC mains, 50 Hz | Battery (12 V or 24 V DC) |
| Depth of magnetization | **Surface only** (skin effect) | **Deeper penetration** (bulk) |
| Defect type detected | **Surface cracks** | **Surface + sub-surface** |
| Particle mobility | Vibrates particles → better contrast | Particles do not vibrate |
| Heat generation | Higher | Lower |
| Demagnetization | Self-demagnetizing on AC cycle | Requires separate demagnetizer |
| Portability | Limited (cable to mains) | High (fully portable) |
| Sensitivity to shallow defects | Excellent | Excellent |
| Sensitivity to deep defects | Poor | Good |
| Typical application | Welds, machined surfaces | Forgings, heavy castings |

## 2.6 Equipment, Test Materials, and Consumables

| Item | Specification |
|:-----|:--------------|
| **Yoke** | AC (230 V, 50 Hz) or DC (24 V) hand-held type, lifting force ≥ 45 N |
| **Test pieces** | Mild steel / carbon steel blocks with artificial defects (e.g., EDM notches, drilled holes, ground slots) |
| **Magnetic particles** | Dry black ferromagnetic powder (Fe₃O₄, 8–15 μm size) **OR** wet fluorescent/black ink suspension |
| **Particle applicator** | Powder bulb / squeeze bottle |
| **Cleaning agent** | Acetone, kerosene, or surface cleaner |
| **White contrast paint** | White lacquer to improve contrast on dark surfaces (optional) |
| **UV lamp** | (For fluorescent particles) 365 nm, hand-held |
| **Demagnetizer** | AC demagnetization coil (optional) |
| **Test bar / Berthold test block** | For yoke performance verification |

## 2.7 Engineering Applications (Real-World Utility)

> [!NOTE]
> **Where will you see yoke testing in industry?**
> 1. **Weld inspection** in pressure vessels, bridges, ship hulls (mandatory by ASME Section V).
> 2. **In-service inspection** of crane hooks, lifting chains, lifting shackles (IS 2062 / OSHA).
> 3. **Crank-shaft and camshaft** crack screening in automotive (every engine component).
> 4. **Forging and casting defect detection** before machining.
> 5. **Aerospace landing gear** (B-H Inspection of critical components).
> 6. **Railway axle and wheel** periodic inspection.
> 7. **Pipeline girth weld** field inspection in cross-country pipelines.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Field Strength from MMF Balance

This derivation is the **most frequently asked analytical question** in KTU University Exams. It links the coil current, the number of turns, and the geometry of the yoke to the field strength in the test piece.

### 3.1.1 Setup (Magnetic Circuit)

Consider a yoke with:
- $N$ turns of coil
- Current $I$ in the coil
- Mean magnetic path length $l$ (sum of: half the yoke core + one pole-piece gap + workpiece test section + return path)

The magnetic circuit consists of the yoke core, the two air gaps (pole-to-workpiece contact), and the workpiece section under test.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Application of Hopkinson's Law

The total reluctance of the series magnetic circuit is:

$$\mathcal{R}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{R}_{\text{core}} + 2\mathcal{R}_{\text{gap}} + \mathcal{R}_{\text{workpiece}}$$

For a uniform cross-section path:

$$\mathcal{R}_i = \frac{l_i}{\mu_i \cdot A_i}$$

Therefore:

$$\mathcal{R}_{\text{total}} = \frac{l_{\text{core}}}{\mu_{\text{core}} A_{\text{core}}} + \frac{2 \, l_{\text{gap}}}{\mu_0 A_{\text{gap}}} + \frac{l_{\text{wp}}}{\mu_0 \mu_r A_{\text{wp}}}$$

Applying Hopkinson's law:

$$\Phi = \frac{N \cdot I}{\mathcal{R}_{\text{total}}}$$

And since $B = \Phi / A$ and $H = B / \mu$, in the workpiece:

$$H_{\text{wp}} = \frac{B_{\text{wp}}}{\mu_0 \mu_r} = \frac{\Phi}{\mu_0 \mu_r A_{\text{wp}}}$$

Substituting $\Phi$:

$$H_{\text{wp}} = \frac{N \cdot I}{\mathcal{R}_{\text{total}} \cdot \mu_0 \mu_r A_{\text{wp}}}$$

### 3.1.3 Approximation (Air-Gap Dominance)

Because $\mu_r \gg 1$ for steel, and air-gap permeability is $\mu_0$ (very small), **the air gap dominates the total reluctance**. In practical yoke calculations, the yoke core reluctance is often neglected, and the magnetic path length $l$ is approximated as the distance between the pole pieces on the workpiece:

$$\boxed{H_{\text{wp}} \approx \frac{N \cdot I}{l_{\text{wp}}}}$$

This is the **working formula** used in KTU numerical problems.

### 3.1.4 Inverse: Current Required for a Given Field

Rearranging the boxed equation for the current:

$$I = \frac{H_{\text{wp}} \cdot l_{\text{wp}}}{N}$$

For a typical AC yoke with $N = 400$ turns, pole spacing $l_{\text{wp}} = 0.1$ m, and the minimum required $H = 2400$ A/m:

$$I = \frac{2400 \times 0.1}{400} = \frac{240}{400} = 0.6 \text{ A}$$

This 0.6 A is indeed the typical operating current of small hand-held yokes — verifying the formula's engineering accuracy.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 (Typical KTU 14-mark problem)

> **Problem:** A yoke-type magnetic crack detector has a coil of 500 turns carrying 1.2 A. The pole pieces are placed 150 mm apart on a mild steel plate. Calculate:
> (a) The magnetizing field strength in the plate.
> (b) The total flux density assuming $\mu_r = 200$.
> (c) The flux through the test area if the cross-section is $A = 25 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}$.

### 3.2.1 Part (a): Field Strength

Given:
- $N = 500$ turns
- $I = 1.2$ A
- $l_{\text{wp}} = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m

Applying the working formula:

$$H = \frac{N \cdot I}{l_{\text{wp}}} = \frac{500 \times 1.2}{0.15} = \frac{600}{0.15}$$

$$\boxed{H = 4000 \text{ A/m}}$$

This is comfortably above the ASTM E709 minimum of 2.4 kA/m, indicating adequate magnetization.

### 3.2.2 Part (b): Flux Density

$$B = \mu_0 \mu_r H = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 200 \times 4000$$

Computing stepwise:

$$B = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 200 \times 4000 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 8 \times 10^{5}$$

$$B = 32\pi \times 10^{-2} = 3.2\pi \times 10^{-1}$$

$$B \approx 1.005 \text{ T}$$

$$\boxed{B \approx 1.005 \text{ T} \approx 1.0 \text{ T}}$$

This is near the saturation flux density of mild steel (≈ 1.5–2.0 T), confirming strong magnetization.

### 3.2.3 Part (c): Magnetic Flux

Area:

$$A = 25 \times 10 = 250 \text{ mm}^2 = 250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Flux:

$$\Phi = B \cdot A = 1.005 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Phi = 2.5125 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Wb}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi \approx 2.51 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Wb} = 251 \text{ μWb}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 (Yoke Performance Verification)

> **Problem:** During a field test, an engineer notes the following yoke specifications: open-circuit lift force $F_L = 50$ N at a pole spacing $l = 100$ mm. The yoke coil has 600 turns. Calculate the operating current if the magnetic field strength in a steel plate is measured as 3.5 kA/m.

### 3.3.1 Step 1: Identify Known Parameters
- $H = 3500$ A/m
- $l_{\text{wp}} = 0.1$ m
- $N = 600$ turns

### 3.3.2 Step 2: Apply the Inverted Working Formula

$$I = \frac{H \cdot l_{\text{wp}}}{N} = \frac{3500 \times 0.1}{600} = \frac{350}{600}$$

$$\boxed{I \approx 0.583 \text{ A} \approx 583 \text{ mA}}$$

### 3.3.3 Step 3: Comment on Adequacy

The lift force $F_L = 50$ N exceeds the ASTM E709 minimum of 45 N, so the yoke is **acceptable for use** under this field strength. If $F_L$ had been < 45 N, the yoke would be **rejected** and sent for rewinding/repair.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Conditions for Defect Detection

The following decision matrix shows whether a defect of given type will be **detectable** in a given magnetization orientation:

| Defect Type | Head-to-Head (Longitudinal) | Head-to-Foot (Circular) | Both Orientations |
|:------------|:---------------------------:|:-----------------------:|:-----------------:|
| **Transverse crack** | ✅ Detected | ❌ Not detected | ✅ |
| **Longitudinal crack** | ❌ Not detected | ✅ Detected | ✅ |
| **Oblique crack** | ⚠ Partially detected | ⚠ Partially detected | ✅ Best |
| **Sub-surface crack** (≤ 6 mm deep, AC) | ❌ Poor | ❌ Poor | ❌ |
| **Sub-surface crack** (≤ 6 mm deep, DC) | ✅ Detected | ✅ Detected | ✅ |
| **Surface-breaking porosity** | ⚠ Diffuse indication | ⚠ Diffuse indication | ✅ |
| **Lamination in forging** | ✅ Detected | ⚠ Partially | ✅ |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for Indication Strength:**
> The strength of the magnetic particle indication is **maximum** when the **defect plane is perpendicular** to the **magnetic flux direction**. This is why rotating the yoke through 90° is essential for comprehensive inspection.

## 3.5 The Yoke Performance Test (Lifting Force / "Dead Weight" Test)

To verify the yoke is functioning correctly, the **Lifting Force Test** is performed:

1. Place a clean **ferromagnetic dead-weight block** (standardized steel block) in contact with both pole pieces.
2. The yoke must support the weight in **vertical orientation** (poles facing down).
3. Read the value on the load-cell or use standardized weights.
4. The minimum acceptable lifting force is **45 N** (≈ 4.5 kgf) for general inspection per ASTM E709.
5. If the yoke is being used for **very low-coercivity materials** (e.g., austenitic stainless), a higher standard applies.

The lifting force is mathematically related to the square of the field strength:

$$F_L \propto B^2 \cdot A_{\text{pole}} \propto (N \cdot I)^2$$

Doubling the current theoretically quadruples the lifting force — but in practice, yoke core saturation limits this.

## 3.6 The Complete Experimental Procedure (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the lab procedure you must write in your KTU lab record. Memorize it.**

**Step 1: Pre-Inspection Preparation**
- Visually examine the test piece surface under good lighting.
- Remove oil, grease, paint, rust, and scale using wire brush, grinder, or solvent.
- Ensure the surface is **clean and dry** before testing.

**Step 2: Yoke Performance Check**
- Perform the **dead-weight (lift) test** using a calibrated test block.
- Confirm lifting force ≥ 45 N.
- Verify electrical continuity of the coil (multimeter check).

**Step 3: Yoke Placement — Head-to-Head Orientation**
- Place the two pole pieces on either side of the suspected defect area, with the line joining them **perpendicular** to the expected crack direction.
- Press the pole pieces firmly onto the surface.
- Maintain pole contact for **1–2 seconds** (or as per trigger).

**Step 4: Apply Magnetic Particles**
- While the yoke is energized, **dust or spray** dry/medium particles onto the surface between the poles.
- Particles will migrate to the leakage-field zones and form **indications**.
- Hold the yoke in position for **3–5 seconds** (or per standard procedure).

**Step 5: Examine Indications**
- Look for sharp, well-defined particle patterns.
- **Relevant indications** (real cracks) appear as sharp lines.
- **False indications** (from scratches, abrupt geometry changes, or magnetization marks) appear as diffuse patterns.
- Record the location, orientation, and size of all indications.

**Step 6: Rotate Yoke 90° (Head-to-Foot)**
- Reposition the pole pieces on the **same surface** with a small gap (typically 50–200 mm).
- Repeat Steps 3–5. This produces **circular magnetization** and reveals defects parallel to the first sweep.

**Step 7: Demagnetization (if required)**
- For **DC yokes** (and components that will be further machined or that will be in a magnetic-sensitive environment), pass the part through an **AC demagnetization coil**.
- Slowly withdraw the part from the coil until the field drops below the Earth's magnetic field.
- Verify demagnetization with a **Gauss meter** (residual field should be < 5 Gauss / 0.5 mT).

**Step 8: Post-Test Cleanup**
- Clean off the magnetic particles.
- Apply anti-corrosion protection if required.
- Return the equipment to its storage case.

**Step 9: Record & Report**
- Note: type of test piece, material, surface condition, yoke used (AC/DC), pole spacing, indication characteristics, and acceptance/rejection.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q: List any three Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods and state one specific application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT):** Uses high-frequency sound waves (1–25 MHz) to detect internal flaws. *Application:* Detection of laminations in thick steel plates and thickness measurement in pressure vessels.

2. **Radiographic Testing (RT):** Uses X-rays or gamma rays to produce an image of internal features on a film or digital detector. *Application:* Detection of porosity, slag inclusions, and lack of fusion in welds.

3. **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT):** Uses magnetic flux leakage and iron particle accumulation to detect surface/near-surface defects in ferromagnetic materials. *Application:* Inspection of weld joints in ferromagnetic steel components.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Students often list methods like "dye penetrant" and "magnetic particle" but forget to give a **specific** industrial application. Vague statements like "for testing materials" carry **no marks**. Always pair each method with a concrete, named component/process.

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

> **Q: Differentiate between AC and DC yokes in magnetic particle testing (any three points).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | AC Yoke | DC Yoke |
|:----------|:--------|:--------|
| **Power source** | 230 V, 50 Hz mains AC | 12 V / 24 V DC battery |
| **Penetration depth** | Surface only (skin effect) | Surface + sub-surface |
| **Particle mobility** | Particles vibrate due to AC → sharp, mobile indications | Particles do not vibrate |
| **Demagnetization** | Self-demagnetizing | Requires separate AC demagnetizer |
| **Best for** | Surface cracks on welds/machined parts | Sub-surface defects in forgings/castings |
| **Heat generation** | Higher (copper + hysteresis losses) | Lower |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B.1(A) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (14 Marks)

> **Q(a)** Explain the principle of Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT) with a neat sketch. List the equipment used and the role of magnetic particles. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q(b)** A yoke type magnetic particle testing equipment has a coil of 600 turns. The pole pieces are placed 120 mm apart on a steel plate. If the coil carries a current of 0.8 A, calculate (i) the magnetizing field strength in the plate, (ii) the flux density assuming relative permeability = 250, and (iii) the total flux if the cross-section of the test area is 20 mm × 12 mm. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer — Q(a)

**Principle of MPT (4 marks):**
When a ferromagnetic material is magnetized, magnetic flux lines travel uniformly through the bulk. If there is a **discontinuity** (crack, void, inclusion) on or near the surface, the high-permeability path is interrupted. This forces the flux to "leak out" of the material at the defect, creating a local **magnetic leakage field** that extends above the surface. Finely divided ferromagnetic particles, when sprinkled on the surface, are **attracted to the leakage field** and accumulate at the defect, forming a visible **indication** (typically a dark line for dry particles or fluorescent green/yellow lines for wet fluorescent particles under UV light). The size, shape, and orientation of the indication reveal the size, shape, and orientation of the defect.

**Equipment Used (2 marks):**
1. Magnetizing source (yoke, permanent magnet, or coil)
2. Yoke type magnetic crack detector with adjustable pole pieces
3. Magnetic particles (dry black iron oxide powder, or wet black/fluorescent suspension)
4. Particle applicator (bulb, squeeze bottle, aerosol spray can)
5. Cleaning materials (degreaser, wire brush, sandpaper)
6. White contrast paint (for dark surfaces)
7. UV lamp (for fluorescent particles)
8. Demagnetizer (AC coil)

**Role of Magnetic Particles (1 mark):**
The particles act as **magnetic flux "tracers"** — they migrate along the gradient of the leakage field and pile up at the defect location, providing a **visible, magnified indication** of the otherwise invisible flux leakage. High-permeability, low-coercivity materials (e.g., soft iron, magnetite Fe₃O₄) are used so that the particles themselves do not retain significant residual magnetism and can be cleaned easily.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Many students draw a **magnet** instead of a **yoke with pole pieces on a test piece** in the sketch. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for a vague diagram. Show: power source → coil → U-core → two pole pieces → contact with test piece → flux lines curving across → particle accumulation at crack.

### Model Answer — Q(b)

**Given:**
- $N = 600$ turns
- $l_{\text{wp}} = 120$ mm $= 0.12$ m
- $I = 0.8$ A
- $\mu_r = 250$
- $A = 20 \times 12 = 240 \text{ mm}^2 = 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

**Part (i): Magnetizing field strength**

$$H = \frac{N \cdot I}{l_{\text{wp}}} = \frac{600 \times 0.8}{0.12} = \frac{480}{0.12}$$

$$\boxed{H = 4000 \text{ A/m}}$$

**Part (ii): Flux density**

$$B = \mu_0 \mu_r H = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 250 \times 4000$$

$$B = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^6 = 4\pi \times 10^{-1} = 0.4\pi$$

$$\boxed{B \approx 1.257 \text{ T}}$$

**Part (iii): Total flux**

$$\Phi = B \cdot A = 1.257 \times 2.4 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Phi = 3.017 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Wb}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi \approx 3.02 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Wb} = 302 \text{ μWb}}$$

---

### Question B.1(B) [Alternative — KTU University Exam – June 2024] (14 Marks)

> **Q(a)** With a neat labelled block diagram, describe the working of a Yoke Type Magnetic Crack Detector. Compare AC and DC yokes. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q(b)** Discuss the various defects that can be detected by magnetic particle testing. How are head-to-head and head-to-foot magnetization methods differentiated? What is the role of demagnetization after MPT? **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer — Q(a)

**Block Diagram of Yoke Type MPT (3 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the standard block diagram. The student must show: power source → excitation coil → U-shaped laminated core → pole pieces → test piece → flux path → leakage at defect → particle application → visible indication.

**Working (2 marks):** When energized, the coil generates MMF = $N \cdot I$, which produces magnetic flux in the low-reluctance yoke core. This flux exits through the pole pieces, enters the test piece, traverses across the surface, and returns to the other pole piece. Where a defect exists, flux is forced to leak out, attracting the magnetic particles and forming a visible pattern.

**AC vs DC Yoke (2 marks):** AC gives surface-only detection due to skin effect and provides mobile particles for sharp indications; DC gives deeper penetration for sub-surface defect detection and is battery-portable. Tabular comparison as given in Section 2.5 earns full marks.

### Model Answer — Q(b)

**Defects Detected (3 marks):**
- Surface cracks (transverse, longitudinal, oblique)
- Sub-surface cracks (DC only, up to ~6 mm depth)
- Welding defects (undercut, crater cracks, lack of fusion)
- Fatigue cracks
- Grinding cracks
