- Quench cracks
- Forging laps and bursts
- Inclusions near the surface

**Head-to-Head vs Head-to-Foot (2 marks):** As described in Section 2.4 of these notes. Head-to-head gives longitudinal magnetization (detects transverse cracks); head-to-foot gives circular magnetization (detects longitudinal cracks). A complete inspection requires **both**.

**Role of Demagnetization (2 marks):**
After MPT, the test piece may retain **residual magnetism**, which can:
1. Attract iron filings and debris in service (e.g., brake rotors, bearings).
2. Interfere with downstream machining (chips stick to the tool).
3. Affect sensitive instruments (compasses, magnetometers).
4. Cause welding arc blow during subsequent welding.

A demagnetization coil fed with AC is used; the part is slowly withdrawn from the coil, leaving residual field < 5 Gauss (verified by Gauss meter).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in This Topic (KTU Lab Exam + University ESE):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "yoke" with "permanent magnet"** in the diagram — examiners strictly expect a **coil + laminated core + pole pieces** (2 marks lost).
> 2. **Using the wrong orientation:** placing the yoke parallel to a transverse crack makes it invisible. Examiners check this on the **practical answer sheet sketch**.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention particle size** (8–15 μm for dry powder; 1–10 μm for wet fluorescent) — a 1-mark penalty.
> 4. **Skipping the surface preparation step** — a clean surface is mandatory per ASTM E709. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **Not stating the minimum lift force value** (45 N) when describing yoke verification — losing 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing the role of "white contrast paint":** it is applied **before** magnetization, not after, to enhance visibility of dark indications on dark surfaces. Reversing the order loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Numerical problem unit errors:** mixing mm and m without conversion. Always convert **l** to **meters** before substituting.
> 8. **Wrong formula for $\mu_0$:** students often write $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ correctly, but forget that in $\text{T·m/A}$ — though the unit is not always demanded, it is a sign of rigorous understanding.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Use before the exam):**
>
> - ✅ **NDE = Non-Destructive Evaluation; NDT = Non-Destructive Testing** (broader interpretation is NDE).
> - ✅ **MPT works ONLY on ferromagnetic materials** (steel, cast iron, nickel, cobalt).
> - ✅ **Five primary NDT methods:** VT, PT, MT, ET, UT, RT (remember all 6).
> - ✅ **Yoke = portable AC or DC electromagnet** with U-core and two pole pieces.
> - ✅ **MMF = $N \cdot I$** (Ampere-turns).
> - ✅ **Field strength in test piece: $H = N \cdot I / l$** (this is the working formula).
> - ✅ **Flux density: $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$** (in Tesla).
> - ✅ **$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m** = permeability of free space.
> - ✅ **ASTM E709 minimum $H$ in steel = 2.4 kA/m.**
> - ✅ **Yoke lift force minimum = 45 N (acceptance criterion).**
> - ✅ **Head-to-Head magnetization** → longitudinal field → detects **transverse** cracks.
> - ✅ **Head-to-Foot magnetization** → circular field → detects **longitudinal** cracks.
> - ✅ **AC yoke** = surface defects only; **DC yoke** = surface + sub-surface.
> - ✅ **Particle size:** Dry = 8–15 μm; Wet fluorescent = 1–10 μm.
> - ✅ **Demagnetization** is mandatory for DC yokes and for parts going to service.
> - ✅ **Flux leakage occurs** because air/defect has $\mu_r \approx 1 \ll \mu_r$ of steel.
> - ✅ **Indication is maximum** when **defect plane ⊥ flux direction**.
> - ✅ **White contrast paint** is applied **before** magnetization (if used).
> - ✅ **Clean surface** is mandatory before MPT.
> - ✅ **MFL = Magnetic Flux Leakage** — the core physics behind MPT.
> - ✅ **Yoke must be pressed firmly** on the surface to minimize air-gap reluctance.
> - ✅ **Holding time after energizing** = 1–2 s; particle observation time = 3–5 s.
> - ✅ **ASTM E709** = Standard Practice for Magnetic Particle Testing.
> - ✅ **Demagnetization verification:** residual field < 5 Gauss (0.5 mT).
> - ✅ **Application domains:** Welds, crane hooks, railway axles, aerospace forgings, pipeline girth welds, engine crankshafts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and Experiment on Portable Magnetic Inspection Unit (Model Radent-2000)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIEL507 - QUALITY AND MATERIAL TESTING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Study and Experiment on Portable Magnetic Inspection Unit (Model Radent-2000) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI / MT)** is a **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** method used to detect surface and slightly sub-surface discontinuities in ferromagnetic materials. It works on the principle of **magnetic flux leakage (MFL)** at defect locations when the specimen is magnetized and fine ferromagnetic particles (dry powder or wet suspension) are applied. A **Portable Magnetic Inspection Unit (Model Radent-2000)** is a compact, battery/AC-powered, hand-held electromagnetic yoke used in the field to perform **localized magnetization** of ferromagnetic components for magnetic particle inspection. The Radent-2000 typically delivers **Peak Ampere-Turns** in the range of **2500–3500 AT peak** with an adjustable **lift-off** (pole contact gap) of up to approximately **100 mm**, conforming to standards such as **ASTM E709**, **ASTM E125**, and **ASME Section V Article 7**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a smooth garden hose (the magnetized component) carrying water (magnetic flux lines). If you puncture a small hole (a crack) on the hose, water leaks out. Magnetic particle inspection is essentially sprinkling iron filings on the hose — wherever there is a leak (crack), the filings clump together and reveal the defect. **In simpler terms:** - The yoke creates an invisible "magnetic river" inside the metal. - A crack acts like a rock in the river, forcing the lines to break out of the surface. - Iron dust sticks to these break-out points, painting the crack so we can see it. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Radent-2000) | Unit | |-----------|--------|----------------------------|------| | Peak Magnetomotive Force | $F_m$ | **2500 – 3500** | Ampere-turns (AT) | | Operating Voltage | $V$ | **230 V AC / 24 V DC** | V | | Pole Spacing (Lift-off) | $L$ | **50 – 100** | mm | | Magnetizing Current | $I$ | **8 – 12** | A (peak) | | Magnetizing Frequency | $f$ | **50 / 60** | Hz | | Field Strength (H) at pole face | $H$ | **40 – 60** | A/cm | | Lifting Power (Dead-weight test) | $W$ | **≥ 4.5 kg** | kgf | > [!IMPORTANT] > **ASTM E125 Reference Block** and **AISI Ketos Ring** (Type I or II) are used to **verify the sensitivity** of the Radent-2000 system before every inspection. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Ferromagnetic Specimen Selection:** Only materials with high relative permeability ($\mu_r \gg 1$) can be magnetized — e.g., **carbon steel, low-alloy steel, wrought iron**. Austenitic stainless steel and aluminium are **non-magnetic** and **cannot** be inspected by MPI. 2. **Magnetization via Yoke:** The Radent-2000 uses a **horseshoe (U-shaped) electromagnet yoke** energized by an AC or DC coil wrapped around its central limb. 3. **Establishment of Flux Lines:** When energized, magnetic flux travels from the **North pole**, through the **specimen**, to the **South pole**, completing the magnetic circuit. 4. **Flux Leakage at Defects:** Discontinuities (cracks, laps, porosity) have very low permeability (essentially air). Flux lines are forced out of the metal surface at these points — this is **flux leakage**. 5. **Particle Accumulation:** Iron particles applied to the surface are attracted to the leakage field, forming a **visible indication** roughly perpendicular to the defect. 6. **Interpretation:** The trained inspector analyzes indication color, shape, location, and direction to characterize the flaw. ### 2.2 Modes of Magnetization in Radent-2000 The Radent-2000 can perform two types of magnetization: > [!NOTE] > **a) Circular Magnetization** — By passing current directly through the component using prods/cables (not used in the portable yoke configuration but supported by some models). > > **b) Longitudinal (Yoke) Magnetization** — Flux travels *along* the length of the component between the two pole pieces. This is the **primary mode** of the Radent-2000 portable yoke. ### 2.3 AC vs DC Yoke — Critical Difference | Feature | AC Yoke (50 Hz) | DC Yoke (HWDC) | |---------|-----------------|----------------| | Penetration depth | Surface only (~1–2 mm) | Sub-surface (~3–6 mm) | | Particle mobility | High — vibrates particles | Low | | Indications | Sharp, bright | Slightly fuzzy | | Use case | Surface cracks | Sub-surface defects | | Typical application | Weld inspection | Critical aerospace parts | > [!TIP] > The Radent-2000 portable unit typically operates on **AC (mains or battery-inverter)** — therefore it detects primarily **surface-breaking defects**. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use | |---|----------|---------|-------|-----------------| | 1 | Magnetomotive Force (MMF) | $F_m = N \cdot I$ | AT (Ampere-turns) | Total driving force of the coil | | 2 | Magnetic Field Strength | $H = \dfrac{F_m}{L} = \dfrac{N \cdot I}{L}$ | A/m | Strength of field inside the part | | 3 | Magnetic Flux Density | $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | Tesla (T) | Field strength inside the material | | 4 | Flux (Total) | $\Phi = B \cdot A$ | Weber (Wb) | Total magnetic lines | | 5 | Reluctance | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$ | A-t/Wb | Opposition to flux (like resistance) | | 6 | Ohm's Law (Magnetic Circuit) | $F_m = \Phi \cdot \mathcal{R}$ | — | Fundamental magnetic circuit law | | 7 | Lift-off Field (approx.) | $H_{\text{lift}} \approx \dfrac{N \cdot I}{2 \pi d}$ | A/m | Effective field at distance $d$ | | 8 | Peak Ampere-Turns Rule | $\text{Peak AT} \ge 4 \times (\text{lift-off in mm})$ | AT | Empirical Radent-2000 setting rule | | 9 | Lift-off Mass Test | $W_{\min} \ge 4.5 \text{ kg}$ | kgf | Sensitivity verification | | 10 | Demagnetization Field | $H_d = -N_d \cdot M$ | A/m | Internal field opposing magnetization | Where: - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T·m/A}$ — permeability of free space - $\mu_r$ — relative permeability of the specimen - $N$ — number of turns in the yoke coil - $I$ — coil current (A) - $L$ — magnetic path length (m) - $A$ — cross-sectional area of flux path ($\text{m}^2$) - $d$ — lift-off distance (m) - $N_d$ — demagnetization factor (geometry dependent) - $M$ — magnetization (A/m) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse *magnetic field strength* $H$ (A/m) with *magnetic flux density* $B$ (T). They are related by permeability, **not equal**. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **In-service weld inspection** of bridges, pipelines, pressure vessels. - **Pre-service QA** of forgings, castings, and machined components. - **Aerospace:** Landing gear, turbine shafts. - **Automotive:** Crankshafts, axle housings. - **Power plants:** Boiler tubes, turbine blades. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Magnetic Circuit Analogy The magnetic circuit obeys an equation structurally identical to Ohm's law. **Step 1 — Definition of MMF** The magnetomotive force generated by a coil of $N$ turns carrying current $I$ is defined as the line integral of magnetic field strength along the closed magnetic path: $$F_m = \oint H \, dl$$ **Step 2 — Uniform Path Assumption** For a uniform magnetic path of length $L$ with constant $H$: $$F_m = H \cdot L$$ **Step 3 — Substitute Definition of H** By definition: $$H = \frac{F_m}{L} = \frac{N \cdot I}{L}$$ **Step 4 — Introduce Reluctance** Reluctance is defined analogously to electrical resistance: $$\mathcal{R} = \frac{L}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$$ **Step 5 — Combine to Get Magnetic Ohm's Law** Starting from the relation $F_m = H \cdot L$ and substituting $H = \dfrac{B}{\mu_0 \mu_r}$ along with $B = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$: $$F_m = \frac{B \cdot L}{\mu_0 \mu_r} = \frac{\Phi}{A} \cdot \frac{L}{\mu_0 \mu_r}$$ **Step 6 — Final Form** $$F_m = \Phi \cdot \mathcal{R}$$ This is **Hopkinson's Law** — the magnetic counterpart of Ohm's law $V = IR$. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Lift-off Ampere-Turn Rule For the Radent-2000, the empirical rule relating peak AT to lift-off is derived as follows. **Step 1 — Pole Face Field Strength** The field strength $H$ at the surface of a pole face carrying current is given by: $$H = \frac{N \cdot I}{2 \pi r}$$ where $r$ is the effective pole radius (m). **Step 2 — Empirical Replacement** For practical yoke use, the lift-off distance $d$ (in mm) replaces $2\pi r$ via empirical correlation: $$\text{Peak AT} \approx k \cdot d$$ **Step 3 — Empirical Constant from Standards** ASTM E709 and Radent-2000 manual specify the empirical multiplier $k = 4$: $$\text{Peak AT} \ge 4 \cdot d_{\text{mm}}$$ **Step 4 — Example Application** If lift-off $d = 75 \text{ mm}$: $$\text{Peak AT}_{\min} = 4 \times 75 = 300 \text{ AT}$$ ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard Pattern) > **Problem:** A Radent-2000 yoke is used to inspect a steel plate (relative permeability $\mu_r = 500$) of cross-section $25 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}$ and pole spacing $60 \text{ mm}$. The yoke coil has $N = 300$ turns carrying peak current $I = 10 \text{ A}$. Calculate: (a) MMF, (b) H-field, (c) B-field, (d) Total flux $\Phi$, (e) Reluctance. **Given:** - $N = 300$ turns - $I = 10$ A (peak) - $L = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$ - $A = 25 \times 10 = 250 \text{ mm}^2 = 250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$ - $\mu_r = 500$ - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T·m/A}$ **Solution:** **(a) Magnetomotive Force:** $$F_m = N \cdot I = 300 \times 10 = 3000 \text{ AT}$$ **(b) Magnetic Field Strength:** $$H = \frac{F_m}{L} = \frac{3000}{0.06} = 50{,}000 \text{ A/m} = 500 \text{ A/cm}$$ **(c) Magnetic Flux Density:** $$B = \mu_0 \mu_r H = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 500 \times 50{,}000$$ $$B = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 500 \times 5 \times 10^{4}$$ $$B = 4\pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{0} = 10\pi \approx 31.42 \text{ T}$$ **Note:** This value exceeds the saturation flux density of carbon steel (~1.6–2.0 T), confirming the steel is **magnetically saturated** at this field strength. For practical MPI, we use $H \approx 40\text{–}60 \text{ A/cm}$ which is well above the **knee of the B-H curve** to ensure defect detection. **(d) Total Magnetic Flux (using actual saturation B = 1.7 T):** $$\Phi = B \cdot A = 1.7 \times 250 \times 10^{-6} = 4.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Wb} = 425 \text{ μWb}$$ **(e) Reluctance of the Magnetic Path:** $$\mathcal{R} = \frac{L}{\mu_0 \mu_r A} = \frac{0.06}{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 500 \times 250 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\mathcal{R} = \frac{0.06}{1.57 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.82 \times 10^{5} \text{ A-t/Wb}$$ **Verification using Hopkinson's Law:** $$F_m = \Phi \cdot \mathcal{R} = 4.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.82 \times 10^{5} \approx 162 \text{ AT}$$ This is lower than the applied 3000 AT because the rest of the MMF is "dropped" across the air gap between the yoke poles and the specimen — which is exactly why **lift-off must be minimized**. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — AC vs DC Yoke Performance | Parameter | AC Yoke | DC (HWDC) Yoke | |-----------|---------|----------------| | Penetration | ~1–2 mm surface | ~3–6 mm sub-surface | | Particle type | Dry or fluorescent wet | Wet fluorescent preferred | | Indications | Crisp, well-defined | Broader, less defined | | Demagnetization needed | Yes (residual magnetism) | Yes (more critical) | | Battery life | Longer | Shorter | | Use case | Field weld QA | Critical aerospace, military | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Radent-2000 ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Power Supply Module
230V AC Mains or 24V DC Battery] --> B[Rectifier and Filter Unit
AC to DC Conversion] B --> C[Current Limiting Circuit
Protection and Regulation] C --> D[Energizing Coil
N turns wound on Central Limb] D --> E[U Shaped Ferromagnetic Yoke Core
Silicon Steel Laminations] E --> F[Pole Pieces
North and South Faces] F --> G[Test Specimen
Placed Between Poles] G --> H[Magnetic Flux Path
Through Specimen] H --> I[Flux Leakage Zone
At Defect Location] I --> J[Iron Particle Accumulation
Forms Visible Indication] J --> K[Operator Visual Inspection
and Interpretation] L[ASTM E125 Test Block] --> M[Sensitivity Verification
Before Each Use] F --> L K --> N[Demagnetization Step
AC Coil at Decaying Field] N --> O[Final Inspection Report
Accept or Reject] ``` ### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram of MPI Experiment using Radent-2000 ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1 Pre Cleaning
Remove Oil, Paint, Dirt] --> S2[Step 2 Sensitivity Check
ASTM E125 Block] S2 --> S3{Block Indications
Visible?} S3 -- No --> S3A[Reject Equipment
Call Maintenance] S3A --> S1 S3 -- Yes --> S4[Step 3 Position Yoke
Pole Spacing 50 to 100 mm] S4 --> S5[Step 4 Energize Yoke
Apply Iron Particles Simultaneously] S5 --> S6[Step 5 Observe Indications
Hold for 1 to 2 seconds] S6 --> S7{Indication
Found?} S7 -- No --> S7A[Move Yoke in 25 mm Overlaps
Scan Entire Area] S7A --> S6 S7 -- Yes --> S8[Step 6 Record Indication
Type, Location, Size] S8 --> S9[Step 7 Demagnetize
If Required by Code] S9 --> S10[Step 8 Post Cleaning
Remove Residual Particles] S10 --> S11[Step 9 Generate Report
Accept or Reject Decision] ``` ### 4.3 Magnetic Flux Path Diagram (Schematic Concept) ```mermaid flowchart LR N[N Pole Face
of Yoke] -- Flux Line --> S[Specimen Top Surface] S --> D[Defect Crack
Flux Leaks Out] D --> S2[Particles Cluster
on Leakage Field] D -- Continues Through Defect --> S3[Specimen Bottom Surface] S3 --> SP[S Pole Face
of Yoke] SP -- Through Yoke Core --> N ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** Define *magnetic flux leakage*. Why is it the fundamental principle behind MPI? **Model Answer:** Magnetic flux leakage (MFL) is the phenomenon where magnetic flux lines, originally confined within a ferromagnetic material, exit and re-enter the material at discontinuities due to the abrupt change in permeability (from $\mu_r \gg 1$ in steel to $\mu_r \approx 1$ in air/cracks). In MPI, the specimen is magnetized using the Radent-2000 yoke. Where a defect exists, the flux is forced out of the surface, creating a localized leakage field. Ferromagnetic particles applied to the surface are attracted to this leakage field, forming a visible indication that reveals the location, orientation, and approximate size of the defect. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** List the three main components of a portable magnetic inspection unit (Model Radent-2000). **Model Answer:** The three main components of the Radent-2000 portable magnetic inspection unit are: 1. **U-shaped ferromagnetic yoke core** — typically laminated silicon steel, providing the flux path. 2. **Energizing coil** — wound around the central limb, typically 300 turns of insulated copper, carrying 8–12 A peak current. 3. **Pole pieces (N and S)** — contacting the test specimen at a controlled lift-off distance (50–100 mm). Auxiliary items include: power cable, on/off switch with current regulator, sensitivity verification block, and demagnetizer. **(3 marks)** --- ### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of the Radent-2000 portable magnetic inspection unit. List any **four advantages** of using this portable unit over a fixed bench MPI setup. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Radent-2000 yoke has 350 turns carrying 9 A peak current. The pole spacing is 75 mm. Calculate (i) Magnetomotive Force, (ii) Magnetic field strength, (iii) Minimum required peak AT as per empirical rule, and (iv) comment on whether the yoke meets the sensitivity requirement. Take $\mu_r = 600$ for the specimen. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer to Part (a):** **Construction of Radent-2000:** - **U-shaped laminated core** made of high-permeability silicon steel, providing a low-reluctance flux path. - **Central energizing coil** wound with insulated copper wire (typically 300–350 turns). - **Pole pieces** at the open ends of the U, designed for contact with the test surface. - **Current regulator** on the handle to control peak AT output. - **Power input port** for 230 V AC or 24 V DC battery. **Working Principle:** When energized, current in the coil produces MMF ($F_m = N \cdot I$). This drives magnetic flux from the N pole through the specimen to the S pole. Any surface or sub-surface discontinuity (crack, lap, porosity) presents a region of high reluctance, forcing flux to leak out. Iron particles accumulate at leakage zones forming visible indications. **Four Advantages of Portable Unit:** 1. Field-usable on large structures (bridges, ship hulls, pipelines). 2. No need for bath immersion — uses dry powder or aerosol spray. 3. Quick setup; localized magnetization reduces inspection time. 4. Lower cost than fixed bench units; battery operation enables remote site work. **(7 marks — 4 for construction+working, 3 for advantages)** **Model Answer to Part (b):** **Given:** $N = 350$, $I = 9$ A (peak), $L = 75$ mm = 0.075 m, $\mu_r = 600$. **(i) MMF:** $$F_m = N \cdot I = 350 \times 9 = 3150 \text{ AT}$$ **(ii) Magnetic field strength:** $$H = \frac{F_m}{L} = \frac{3150}{0.075} = 42{,}000 \text{ A/m} = 420 \text{ A/cm}$$ **(iii) Empirical Rule:** $$\text{Peak AT}_{\min} = 4 \times d_{\text{mm}} = 4 \times 75 = 300 \text{ AT}$$ **(iv) Sensitivity Check:** Since available $F_m = 3150 \text{ AT} \gg 300 \text{ AT}_{\min}$, the yoke **comfortably meets** the sensitivity requirement with a safety margin of $\frac{3150-300}{300} \times 100\% = 950\%$ above the minimum threshold. The corresponding flux density is $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 600 \times 42000 \approx 31.7$ T (theoretical) — but in practice the material saturates at $B_{\text{sat}} \approx 1.7$ T, confirming effective magnetization. **(7 marks — 2 each for parts (i) and (ii), 1 for (iii), 2 for (iv))** --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]` **(a)** Explain the procedure to conduct magnetic particle inspection using the Radent-2000 on a welded mild steel plate. Mention the pre-inspection, inspection, and post-inspection steps in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare AC and DC (HWDC) modes of yoke magnetization. Which mode is preferred for detecting sub-surface defects, and why? Explain with reference to skin depth. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer to Part (a):** **Pre-Inspection Steps:** 1. Verify equipment sensitivity using **ASTM E125 reference block** or **Ketos ring**. Block indications must be sharp. 2. Clean the weld surface — remove slag, oil, paint, rust using wire brush and solvent. 3. Check ambient lighting and ensure UV-A lamp (365 nm) for fluorescent inspection is functional. 4. Confirm lift-off distance and select appropriate peak AT setting (≥ 4 × lift-off in mm). **Inspection Steps:** 1. Position the yoke with pole pieces perpendicular to the expected crack direction for maximum flux crossing the defect. 2. Energize the yoke (current on). 3. Apply iron particles (dry powder dusting or wet suspension spray) **simultaneously** with energization — keep energized for 1–2 seconds after particle application. 4. Observe indication formation under proper lighting. 5. Move yoke in overlapping steps (~25 mm overlap) to scan the entire weld zone. 6. Record all indications — note type (linear/round), location, length, and orientation. **Post-Inspection Steps:** 1. Demagnetize the component (if residual magnetism is unacceptable per code). 2. Clean residual particles from the surface. 3. Document findings — accept/reject as per acceptance standard (e.g., ASME Sec. VIII, AWS D1.1). 4. Sign and archive the inspection report. **(7 marks — 2 for pre, 3 for inspection, 2 for post)** **Model Answer to Part (b):** **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | AC Yoke | DC (HWDC) Yoke | |--------|---------|----------------| | Source | Mains AC (50/60 Hz) | Rectified and filtered DC | | Skin effect | Strong — flux confined near surface | Negligible | | Penetration | 1–2 mm only | 3–6 mm | | Particle mobility | High (vibration) | Low | | Indications | Sharp, bright | Broader, less crisp | | Battery drain | Less | More | | Demagnetization | Slightly easier | More difficult | **Preferred Mode for Sub-surface Defects: DC (HWDC) Yoke.** **Reason — Skin Depth Concept:** The skin depth $\delta$ is the depth at which the magnetic field amplitude falls to $\dfrac{1}{e}$ (~37%) of its surface value: $$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho}{\omega \mu_0 \mu_r}}$$ For AC at 50 Hz in steel ($\mu_r \approx 500$): $$\delta \approx 1\text{–}2 \text{ mm}$$ For DC ($\omega \to 0$), skin depth tends to infinity — flux penetrates uniformly through the cross-section. Hence DC magnetisation reaches defects located 3–6 mm below the surface. **Conclusion:** For sub-surface defects, **DC magnetization is mandatory**; for surface-breaking cracks in welds, AC suffices and gives sharper indications. **(7 marks — 3 for comparison, 4 for explanation and skin depth derivation)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing $H$ and $B$** — $H$ is in A/m, $B$ is in Tesla. Never equate them directly. > 2. **Forgetting to mention sensitivity verification** before every inspection — a guaranteed 1-mark loss if missed. > 3. **Using AC peak instead of RMS** in formulas — examiners expect peak AT for yoke calculations. > 4. **Not specifying lift-off distance** in numerical problems — the empirical rule (4 × lift-off mm) requires explicit lift-off value. > 5. **Stating "MPI works on all metals"** — WRONG. It works only on **ferromagnetic** materials. Austenitic SS, aluminium, copper cannot be MPI tested. > 6. **Skipping demagnetization step** — many components (bearings, instruments) require post-inspection demagnetization; ignoring this loses marks. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **MPI = Magnetic Particle Inspection / Magnetic Testing (MT)** — surface and near-surface NDT method for **ferromagnetic** materials only. - **Radent-2000** is a **portable electromagnetic yoke** delivering **2500–3500 peak AT** with **50–100 mm lift-off**. - **Two fundamental laws:** Hopkinson's Law ($F_m = \Phi \cdot \mathcal{R}$) and Ampere's Circuital Law ($F_m = N \cdot I$). - **Three key equations:** $F_m = N \cdot I$, $H = F_m/L$, $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$. - **Defect indication** requires field **perpendicular** to the crack direction (orient yoke correctly). - **Empirical AT rule:** $\text{Peak AT} \ge 4 \times (\text{lift-off in mm})$. - **Sensitivity verification:** ASTM E125 block or Ketos ring **before every test** — non-negotiable per ASTM E709. - **AC mode** = surface defects (skin depth 1–2 mm); **DC mode** = sub-surface defects (penetration 3–6 mm). - **Demagnetization** required if component will be in service near instruments or bearings. - **Indications must be evaluated** for: type (linear/round), relevance (true/false), and acceptance per code. - **Limitations:** ferromagnetic materials only; surface must be cleaned; operator-dependent; gives no depth information. - **Standards to remember:** ASTM E709 (MT), ASTM E125 (test block), ASME Sec. V Art. 7, ISO 9934, BS 6072. - **Particle types:** Dry powder (grey/black/red), Wet fluorescent (green under UV-A 365 nm). - **Report contents:** Procedure, equipment ID, sensitivity verification result, surface condition, indications found, acceptance criteria, operator signature, date.

topics.title: Study and Experiment on Bench Type Wet Horizontal Magnetic Particle Machine (Model Radent-B 2000) and Black Light
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL507 - QUALITY AND MATERIAL TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and Experiment on Bench Type Wet Horizontal Magnetic Particle Machine (Model Radent-B 2000) and Black Light

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Bench Type Wet Horizontal Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT) Machine** is a stationary, laboratory-scale non-destructive testing (NDT) apparatus designed to detect surface and slightly sub-surface discontinuities in ferromagnetic components by inducing a magnetic flux through the test piece and observing the leakage field pattern formed by suspended fluorescent magnetic particles. The Model **Radent-B 2000** is a commercial wet-method MPT bench that combines **continuous or residual magnetization modes** with **fluorescent particle indication** under **Black Light (UV-A radiation, 365 nm)** inspection.

According to **ASTM E709** and **ASTM E1444**, wet horizontal MPT utilizes finely divided ferromagnetic particles (typical particle size **1–10 µm**) suspended in a liquid carrier (petroleum distillate or water with conditioning agents), sprayed onto the magnetized workpiece, and viewed under ultraviolet-A illumination for high-contrast defect visibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment belongs to the Electromagnetic NDT domain. Students must clearly distinguish between *wet* (particle suspension in liquid) and *dry* (powder cloud) methods, and between *bench type* (stationary lab equipment) and *portable* (field-deployable yokes/prods) MPT systems.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine sprinkling **iron filings** on a sheet of paper placed over a bar magnet. The filings mysteriously cluster along invisible lines — these are the *magnetic field lines*. Now, suppose a tiny invisible **crack** lies hidden inside a metal block. The crack acts like a roadblock for those field lines; they bend and "leak" out of the surface at the defect location. Wet MPT works on the very same idea — millions of microscopic magnetic particles are sprayed over a magnetized component and obediently march to the **flux leakage sites**, outlining even hairline cracks that the naked eye cannot detect.

The **Black Light (UV-A)** is essentially a "magic flashlight": the wet particles are coated with a fluorescent dye. Under normal daylight they look dull, but under UV-A they glow with a brilliant yellow-green hue (peak emission near **555 nm**, the peak sensitivity of the human eye), transforming invisible cracks into bright glowing rivers on a dark purple background.

### 1.3 Why the Radent-B 2000 and Why "Horizontal"?

| Configuration | Description | Typical Use |
|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Horizontal Bench** | Workpiece lies flat between two contact pads | Long shafts, bars, axles, rods |
| Vertical Bench | Workpiece hangs vertically | Heavy castings, weldments |
| Portable Yoke | Hand-held AC or DC electromagnet | Field inspections, welds |

> [!NOTE]
> The "**2000**" in Radent-B 2000 denotes its **maximum magnetizing current of 2000 Amperes (AC or DC)**, classifying it as a medium-capacity industrial lab instrument used for components up to approximately **1000 mm length** with cross-sections suitable for adequate flux density penetration.

### 1.4 Definitions of Key Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Magnetic Flux Density (B)**: $\vec{B} = \mu_0(\vec{H} + \vec{M})$ ; required value **≥ 0.8 Tesla (8000 Gauss)** at the test surface per ASTM E709.
- **Magnetizing Force (H)**: $\vec{H} = \dfrac{NI}{L}$ (Ampere-turns per meter)
- **UV-A Wavelength**: **365 nm** (long-wave UV; safe band per IEC 62471)
- **Fluorescent Emission Peak of MPT Particles**: **555 ± 5 nm** (yellow-green)
- **Wet Particle Concentration in Carrier**: **0.1 – 0.4 %** by volume for fluorescent particles.
- **Ambient UV-A Irradiance at Inspection Surface**: **≥ 1000 µW/cm²** (minimum), ideally **3000–4000 µW/cm²**.
- **Demagnetization Residual Field**: must be reduced to **≤ 0.3 mT (3 Gauss)** for critical components.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The Radent-B 2000 performs wet fluorescent magnetic particle inspection through the following sequential logic:

1. **Pre-cleaning Stage**: The test piece is degreased and dried. Surface contaminants (oil, paint, scale) block flux leakage and prevent particle adherence — they are unacceptable per ASTM E709.

2. **Loading Stage**: The component is clamped between the **headstock** (fixed contact pad) and the **tailstock** (movable contact pad) on the horizontal bed. Pneumatic or manual pressure ensures **low-resistance contact**.

3. **Magnetization Stage**: Either **continuous** (current flows while particles are applied) or **residual** (current switched off, particles applied to remanent field) mode is selected. The Radent-B 2000 typically provides:
   - **AC magnetization** — for **surface defects only** (skin depth ≈ 1–3 mm in steel).
   - **DC / Half-wave DC magnetization** — for **sub-surface defects** up to ~6 mm deep.
   - **Three-phase full-wave DC** — for uniform deep penetration.

4. **Particle Application Stage**: The **agitation pump** keeps the fluorescent suspension homogenized. A **hand-held hose with nozzle** sprays a gentle, even film of particle-laden fluid over the workpiece. Gravity drains excess fluid back to the **reservoir tank**.

5. **Inspection under Black Light**: The operator dims ambient light and illuminates the workpiece with the **UV-A (365 nm) hand lamp** or built-in chamber lamp. Fluorescent indications appear as glowing lines or patterns.

6. **Demagnetization Stage**: The component is passed through a **decay-type demagnetizer** to reduce residual magnetism below acceptance limits (typically **≤ 3 Gauss** for austenitic welds and bearing surfaces).

7. **Interpretation and Reporting**: Indications are classified as **relevant (true defects)** or **non-relevant (false indications)** based on shape, location, and orientation relative to flux lines.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent markdown table breakage in absolute values and determinants.

| # | Quantity / Equation | Formula | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-------------------|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Magnetic Field Strength (Solenoid/Coil) | $H = \dfrac{N \cdot I}{L}$ | A-turns/m | Magnetizing force along coil axis |
| 2 | Magnetic Flux Density in Material | $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | Tesla (T) | Flux density inside workpiece |
| 3 | Required Magnetizing Current (Circular Magnetization) | $I = \dfrac{K \cdot D}{L_{eff}}$ | A | Direct formula for Radent-B 2000 current setting |
| 4 | Circular Magnetization Constant (Steel) | $K \approx 1200$–$2000$ | A/mm | Empirical constant per ASTM E709 |
| 5 | Required Ampere-Turns for Longitudinal Magnetization | $NI = 45000 \cdot L_{eff}$ (in inch-units) | A-turns | From ASTM E709 nomogram |
| 6 | Skin Depth in Steel (AC Magnetization) | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\omega \mu}}$ | mm | Effective defect detection depth for AC |
| 7 | Force on a Magnetic Particle in Leakage Field | $F = \dfrac{V \cdot \chi}{\mu_0} \cdot B \dfrac{dB}{dx}$ | Newton | Particle attraction to defect |
| 8 | Black Light Wavelength (UV-A) | $\lambda = 365$ nm | nm | Peak excitation wavelength for fluorescent MPT particles |
| 9 | Required UV-A Irradiance | $E_{UV} \geq 1000$ | µW/cm² | Minimum at inspection surface (ASTM E1444) |
| 10 | Acceptable Residual Magnetism (Demag) | $B_r \leq 0.3$ mT | mT | Post-inspection requirement |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Industry**: Inspection of landing gear shafts, turbine discs, and engine rotor components before assembly.
- **Automotive**: Crack detection in crankshafts, camshafts, steering knuckles.
- **Railways**: Axle and wheel-set inspection in depots (Radent-type benches are widely used).
- **Pressure Vessel Manufacturing**: Weld inspection in ferromagnetic steels (SA-516, SA-387).
- **Forging and Casting Foundries**: Quality control of as-forged / as-cast components.

The combination of **wet fluorescent particles + UV-A + AC/DC switchable magnetization** makes the Radent-B 2000 a versatile "all-in-one" machine for industry-grade laboratory training, precisely the kind of equipment KTU lab syllabi require students to be operationally familiar with.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Required Magnetizing Current for a Cylindrical Shaft on Radent-B 2000

**Problem Statement:** A low-alloy steel shaft of diameter $D = 60$ mm and effective length between contacts $L_{eff} = 400$ mm is to be magnetized by **head-to-tail current flow (longitudinal magnetization via prod/contact method)** on the Radent-B 2000. Calculate the required magnetizing current $I$ in DC mode to achieve a flux density $B \geq 0.8$ T at the surface.

**Step 1 — Recall the empirical relationship for circular magnetization:**

According to ASTM E709 Section 6.3, the magnetizing current required is given by:

$$I = \frac{K \cdot D}{L_{eff}}$$

where $K$ is the **magnetization constant** (empirically determined for the material and supply type).

**Step 2 — Select the correct value of K for low-alloy steel under DC magnetization:**

For low-alloy carbon steel under DC:

$$K_{DC} = 1600 \; \text{A/mm}$$

For AC magnetization (surface only):

$$K_{AC} = 1200 \; \text{A/mm}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the given numerical values:**

$$I = \frac{1600 \times 60}{400}$$

Here we explain the unit logic: $K$ is in A/mm, $D$ is in mm, $L_{eff}$ is unitless in the ratio, giving $I$ in Amperes.

$$I = \frac{96000}{400}$$

Performing the division:

$$I = 240 \; \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Radent-B 2000 capacity:**

Maximum output of the machine is **2000 A**. The required 240 A is well within range. The operator would set the current knob (typically a 10-tap selector) to the appropriate tap.

**Step 5 — Verification using Ampere's Law:**

Magnetic field strength:

$$H = \frac{I}{2\pi r}$$

where $r$ = radius of shaft = 30 mm = 0.030 m.

$$H = \frac{240}{2 \times \pi \times 0.030}$$

$$H = \frac{240}{0.1885}$$

$$H \approx 1273 \; \text{A/m}$$

Converting to flux density for steel (assume $\mu_r \approx 500$ for low-alloy steel near saturation):

$$B = \mu_0 \mu_r H = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 500 \times 1273$$

$$B \approx 8 \times 10^{-1} \; \text{T} = 0.8 \; \text{T}$$

The required value is achieved. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: Effective Defect Detection Depth for AC Magnetization

**Step 1 — Define skin depth:**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2\rho}{\omega \mu}}$$

For mild steel: $\rho \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \; \Omega\cdot\text{m}$, $\mu \approx \mu_0 \times 500 = 6.28 \times 10^{-4}$ H/m.

**Step 2 — At 50 Hz mains supply (India):**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.16 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-7}}{314.16 \times 6.28 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2.0 \times 10^{-7}}{1.973 \times 10^{-1}}}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{1.014 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\delta \approx 1.007 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m} = 1.0 \; \text{mm}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** AC magnetization detects surface defects only, up to **≈ 1 mm deep** under the surface. For sub-surface cracks, DC or half-wave DC magnetization is mandatory on the Radent-B 2000.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Relationships

| Parameter | Symbol | Increases With | Effect on Indication | KTU Lab Insight |
|-----------|--------|---------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Magnetizing Current | $I$ | Tap setting | Brighter, broader indications | Risk of burning at contacts if too high |
| Particle Concentration | $C$ | Reservoir top-up | Sharper indications up to a point | Excess causes masking (false positives) |
| UV-A Irradiance | $E_{UV}$ | Lamp power / proximity | Higher contrast | Lamp must be warmed up for ≥ 5 min |
| Ambient Light | $L_{amb}$ | Window/door opening | Reduced contrast | Inspection room must be darkened |
| Magnetization Mode | AC vs DC | Switch position | AC: surface ; DC: sub-surface | Use BOTH for comprehensive inspection |
| Contact Pressure | $F_c$ | Pneumatic regulator | Lower contact resistance | Insufficient pressure → arc burns → false indications |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Radent-B 2000

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Mains[Three Phase 415V Mains Supply] --> Contactor[Main Contactor and MCB Protection]
    Contactor --> Transformer[Step Down Isolation Transformer]
    Transformer --> Rectifier[Three Phase Full Wave Rectifier DC Option]
    Transformer --> ACSwitch[AC Tap Selector 0 to 2000 A]
    ACSwitch --> Headstock[Magnetizing Headstock Contact Pad]
    Rectifier --> Headstock
    Headstock --> Workpiece[Test Piece Clamped Horizontally]
    Workpiece --> Tailstock[Tailstock Movable Contact Pad]
    Tailstock --> ReturnBus[Return Current Bus Bar]
    ReturnBus --> Contactor
    AgitatorTank[Agitation Reservoir with Fluorescent Suspension] --> Pump[Recirculation Pump]
    Pump --> Hose[Hand Held Spray Hose and Nozzle]
    Hose --> Workpiece
    Workpiece --> DrainTray[Overflow Drain Tray]
    DrainTray --> AgitatorTank
    UVLamp[UV A 365 nm Hand Lamp or Chamber Lamp] -.illuminates.-> Workpiece
    DemagCoil[Decay Type Demagnetizer Coil] --> Workpiece
    Operator[Trained Operator Under Dim Ambient Light] -.inspects and records.-> Workpiece
```

### 4.2 Operational Workflow for a Wet Fluorescent MPT Cycle on Radent-B 2000

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Receive Workpiece and Inspection Procedure]) --> Clean[Pre Clean and Degrease the Surface]
    Clean --> Mount[Mount Between Headstock and Tailstock Contacts]
    Mount --> Mode{Select Magnetization Mode}
    Mode -->|Surface Defects| AC[Set AC Current Tap 1200 A per mm]
    Mode -->|Sub Surface Defects| DC[Set DC Current Tap 1600 A per mm]
    AC --> Spray[Spray Fluorescent Suspension Evenly]
    DC --> Spray
    Spray --> Inspect[Inspect Under UV A 365 nm in Darkened Booth]
    Inspect --> Mark[Mark Relevant Indications with Layout Dye or Chalk]
    Mark --> Demag[Pass Through Demagnetizer Coil]
    Demag --> Verify[Verify Residual Field Below 3 Gauss with Gauss Meter]
    Verify --> Report[Record Results on MPT Report Form]
    Report --> End([Submit Report to QC Engineer])
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Magnetic Flux Lines Around a Surface Crack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SaturatedBar[Magnetized Steel Bar Cross Section]
        Flux1[Flux Line Going In]:::flux
        Flux2[Flux Line Going In]:::flux
        Flux3[Flux Line Going In]:::flux
        Crack[[Surface Crack Discontinuity]]:::crack
        Leak1[Leakage Field Out of Surface]:::leak
        Leak2[Leakage Field Into Surface]:::leak
        Flux1 --> Leak1
        Leak1 --> Leak2
        Leak2 --> Flux3
        Crack -.blocks.-> Flux2
    end
    Particles[Fluorescent Particles Attracted to Leakage Zone]:::particle --> Leak1
    Particles --> Leak2
    UV[UV A 365 nm Radiation]:::uv --> Particles
    Glow[Visible Yellow Green Glowing Indication]:::glow --> UV
    classDef flux fill:#dbe9ff,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef crack fill:#ffe4e6,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef leak fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000
    classDef particle fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
    classDef uv fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#4c1d95,color:#000
    classDef glow fill:#fef08a,stroke:#713f12,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Photographic Layout Description of the Radent-B 2000

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Layout of the Equipment (front view from operator position):**
>
> - **Left Section:** Three-phase incoming isolator (415 V, 50 Hz) → step-down transformer.
> - **Center Section:** Bench-top horizontal bed, ~1200 mm long, with copper-clad contact pads at both ends. The workpiece rests on adjustable V-block supports.
> - **Right Section:** Control panel — rotary switch for **AC / Half-Wave DC / Full-Wave DC**, multi-tap selector for current (typically 500 A, 1000 A, 1500 A, 2000 A), ammeter, voltmeter, demag coil timer.
> - **Lower Section:** Built-in **stainless steel reservoir tank** (~30 L capacity) with magnetic-coupled agitator pump to prevent particle settling. Suction hose with pistol-grip nozzle.
> - **Optional:** Integrated UV-A chamber with retractable **365 nm lamp** and dark curtain enclosure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **Wet Method** of Magnetic Particle Testing. Why are fluorescent particles preferred when inspection is carried out under a Black Light?

**Model Answer:**
The **Wet Method** of Magnetic Particle Testing involves suspending finely divided ferromagnetic particles (typical size 1–10 µm) in a liquid carrier such as petroleum distillate or conditioned water. The suspension is sprayed onto the magnetized workpiece; the liquid drains off while the particles adhere to flux leakage sites around defects.
Fluorescent particles are preferred under Black Light (UV-A, 365 nm) because the fluorescent dye coating absorbs UV-A radiation and re-emits it as **visible yellow-green light (peak ~555 nm)**, which matches the peak sensitivity of the human eye. This produces **high-contrast glowing indications** even in partially lit conditions and dramatically improves the probability of detection (POD) of fine defects. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** State the function of the **Black Light (UV-A lamp)** in wet fluorescent MPT and specify the wavelength range used.

**Model Answer:**
The Black Light (UV-A lamp) provides ultraviolet radiation in the wavelength range **315 nm to 400 nm**, with the typical peak emission of MPT inspection lamps being **365 nm**. Its function is to **excite the fluorescent dye** coated on the wet magnetic particles, causing them to emit visible yellow-green light (peak emission ~555 nm) at defect locations. This creates high-contrast, easily visible indications of surface and near-surface discontinuities. The lamp must provide a minimum irradiance of **1000 µW/cm²** at the inspection surface per ASTM E1444. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labeled block diagram, explain the **construction and major components** of the **Radent-B 2000** Bench Type Wet Horizontal Magnetic Particle Testing Machine.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Radent-B 2000 is a stationary, medium-capacity (~2000 A max.) wet fluorescent magnetic particle testing bench designed for laboratory and shop-floor inspection of ferromagnetic components.

**Major Components and Their Functions (5 marks):**

1. **Three-Phase Supply and Isolation Panel:** 415 V, 50 Hz input with MCB, ELCB and contactor for operator safety.
2. **Step-Down Isolation Transformer:** Reduces mains voltage to low-voltage, high-current output suitable for magnetization.
3. **Current Selector and Rectifier Unit:** Provides selectable AC, Half-Wave DC, and Full-Wave DC outputs with a tap selector up to 2000 A.
4. **Horizontal Bed with Headstock and Tailstock:** Two copper-faced contact pads clamp the workpiece; tailstock is movable via hand wheel or pneumatic ram. V-blocks support cylindrical parts.
5. **Particle Suspension Reservoir and Agitation Pump:** 30–50 L stainless-steel tank with magnetic-coupled centrifugal pump and recirculation loop to keep particles uniformly suspended.
6. **Spray Hose with Nozzle:** Flexible hose with adjustable cone nozzle for fine mist application.
7. **Drain Tray and Filter:** Collects overflow and returns to tank via filtration.
8. **Control Panel:** Ammeter, voltmeter, mode selector switch, current tap selector, demag timer, indicator lamps.
9. **Demagnetizing Coil (Decay Type):** Built-in or external coil with decaying AC field for residual magnetism reduction below 3 Gauss.
10. **UV-A (365 nm) Inspection Lamp:** Hand-held or fixed-mount high-pressure mercury or LED UV-A lamp for fluorescent indication viewing.

**Working Principle (1 mark):**
The workpiece is clamped, magnetized by direct current flow or coil magnetization, sprayed with fluorescent suspension, and inspected under UV-A in a darkened area. Flux leakage due to defects attracts particles, producing glowing indications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **complete experimental procedure** to perform wet fluorescent MPT on a ferromagnetic shaft specimen using the Radent-B 2000, including safety precautions.

**Model Solution:**

**Aim:** To detect surface and sub-surface defects on a ferromagnetic shaft specimen using wet fluorescent MPT on the Radent-B 2000.

**Apparatus Required (1 mark):** Radent-B 2000 MPT machine, fluorescent particle suspension (e.g., Magnaflux 14A), UV-A (365 nm) lamp, demagnetizer, Gauss meter, pre-calibrated test piece with known defect, lint-free cloth, gloves, safety goggles (UV-blocking).

**Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Preparation:** Clean the specimen thoroughly with solvent; remove oil, paint, scale. Allow to dry.
2. **Pre-Inspection Check:** Verify particle suspension concentration (settling test) and UV-A lamp irradiance using a UV-A meter (≥ 1000 µW/cm²).
3. **Mounting:** Place the shaft horizontally between headstock and tailstock contact pads. Tighten with adequate pressure to ensure low-resistance contact.
4. **Mode Selection:** Choose AC for surface defects or DC for sub-surface defects.
5. **Current Setting:** Calculate $I = K D / L_{eff}$ and set the appropriate current tap.
6. **Magnetization and Particle Application:** Energize the circuit and simultaneously spray the fluorescent suspension evenly over the workpiece. Allow the liquid to drain.
7. **Inspection:** In a darkened booth, illuminate the workpiece with the UV-A lamp. Observe and mark any glowing indications.
8. **Demagnetization:** Pass the specimen through the demagnetizer several times.
9. **Verification:** Measure residual field with a Gauss meter; ensure it is below 3 Gauss.
10. **Reporting:** Record location, orientation, and size of indications; classify as relevant/non-relevant; attach photographs if required.

**Safety Precautions (1 mark):**
- Wear UV-A blocking goggles to prevent photokeratitis.
- Avoid skin contact with petroleum-based carriers; use nitrile gloves.
- Ensure the workpiece is firmly clamped to prevent arcing and burns.
- Never energize the circuit without a workpiece between contacts (risk of damage to machine).
- Keep the inspection area dim but ensure general safety illumination for movement.

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **role of Black Light (UV-A 365 nm)** in fluorescent magnetic particle inspection. Discuss the working of the human eye and the principle of fluorescence with relevant equations.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Black Light (UV-A, 365 nm) is a critical component in fluorescent MPT. It converts the invisible UV-A photons into visible yellow-green emission (≈555 nm) via fluorescence, dramatically improving defect detectability.

**Fluorescence Principle (3 marks):**
Fluorescence is the phenomenon in which a substance absorbs electromagnetic radiation at one wavelength (excitation) and almost instantaneously re-emits it at a longer wavelength (emission). The governing relation is the **Stokes Shift**:

$$\lambda_{emit} > \lambda_{excite}$$

For typical MPT fluorescent dyes:

$$\lambda_{excite} = 365 \text{ nm (UV-A)}$$
$$\lambda_{emit} \approx 555 \text{ nm (yellow-green)}$$

The quantum yield $\Phi$ of fluorescence:

$$\Phi = \frac{\text{Number of photons emitted}}{\text{Number of photons absorbed}}$$

High-quality MPT particles have $\Phi \approx 0.7$–$0.9$.

**Human Eye Sensitivity (2 marks):**
The photopic luminosity function $V(\lambda)$ of the human eye peaks at **555 nm** under daylight (photopic) vision. This is precisely why MPT particles are engineered to emit at this wavelength — they appear brilliantly bright to the operator. The scotopic (low-light) curve peaks near 507 nm, but fluorescent MPT uses photopic vision because the operator dark-adapts only partially.

$$V(555 \text{ nm}) \approx 1.0 \; (\text{maximum})$$
$$V(365 \text{ nm}) \approx 0 \; (\text{UV-A is invisible})$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The combination of high quantum yield, matched emission wavelength, and high visual sensitivity makes UV-A excited fluorescent MPT the most sensitive method among all conventional MPT variants.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A carbon steel shaft of diameter **80 mm** and length between contacts **500 mm** is to be magnetized for sub-surface defect detection on the Radent-B 2000. Calculate the required magnetizing current in **DC mode** and explain how you would verify the flux density.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Diameter $D = 80$ mm
- Effective length $L_{eff} = 500$ mm
- Mode = DC (for sub-surface defects)
- Material = Carbon steel

**Step 1 — Recall the formula (1 mark):**

$$I = \frac{K \cdot D}{L_{eff}}$$

**Step 2 — Select K for DC magnetization of carbon steel (1 mark):**

For carbon steel under DC magnetization:

$$K_{DC} = 1600 \; \text{A/mm}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (1 mark):**

$$I = \frac{1600 \times 80}{500}$$

$$I = \frac{128000}{500}$$

$$I = 256 \; \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Verify flux density at the surface (3 marks):**

The tangential magnetic field strength at the surface of a long cylinder carrying current $I$:

$$H = \frac{I}{2\pi r}$$

with $r = 40$ mm = 0.040 m:

$$H = \frac{256}{2 \times \pi \times 0.040}$$

$$H = \frac{256}{0.2513}$$

$$H \approx 1018.6 \; \text{A/m}$$

For carbon steel at the operating point near saturation, $\mu_r \approx 500$:

$$B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$$

$$B = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 500 \times 1018.6$$

$$B \approx 6.4 \times 10^{-1} \; \text{T} = 0.64 \; \text{T}$$

**Step 5 — Discussion and Correction (1 mark):**
Since 0.64 T is slightly below the ASTM E709 minimum of 0.8 T, the operator should increase the current tap by ~25 %:

$$I_{new} = 1.25 \times 256 \approx 320 \; \text{A}$$

This yields $B \approx 0.8$ T as required.

**Verification Method (1 mark):**
Use a calibrated **Hall-effect Gauss meter** at the shaft surface to confirm $B \geq 0.8$ T before proceeding with particle application.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in MPT Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing AC and DC skin depths:** Many students write that AC can detect deep defects — it cannot. AC skin depth in steel at 50 Hz is only **≈ 1 mm**.
> 2. **Wrong K-value:** Forgetting whether $K = 1200$ (AC) or $K = 1600$ (DC) and using the wrong unit conversions.
> 3. **Ignoring Demagnetization:** Always mention post-inspection demagnetization for critical components — many students skip this in viva.
> 4. **Mixing up wavelengths:** UV-A peak is **365 nm**, fluorescent emission peak is **555 nm** — these are *different* and must not be interchanged.
> 5. **Omitting Safety:** UV-A exposure causes **photokeratitis** (arc eye) and **erythema**; safety goggles must always be mentioned.
> 6. **Wet vs Dry Confusion:** Wet method uses suspension in liquid carrier; dry method uses powder cloud. Do not interchange.
> 7. **Forgetting "Continuous" vs "Residual" Mode:** In continuous mode the current flows during particle application; in residual mode it is switched off first. Examiner traps often test this distinction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Radent-B 2000** is a wet horizontal bench-type MPT machine rated at **2000 A** maximum.
- Uses **fluorescent particles in liquid carrier** sprayed onto the magnetized workpiece.
- Magnetization modes: **AC (surface defects, ≈ 1 mm depth)**, **Half-wave DC (≈ 3 mm)**, **Full-wave DC (≈ 6 mm)**.
- Critical formula: $I = \dfrac{K D}{L_{eff}}$ with $K = 1200$ (AC) or $K = 1600$ (DC) A/mm.
- Required flux density at surface: **≥ 0.8 T (ASTM E709)**.
- **Black Light (UV-A)** peak wavelength: **365 nm**; particle emission peak: **555 nm**.
- Minimum UV-A irradiance at surface: **≥ 1000 µW/cm²** (ASTM E1444).
- Eye response peaks at **555 nm**, matched to MPT fluorescent emission.
- Post-inspection **demagnetization** to **≤ 3 Gauss (0.3 mT)** residual field.
- Skin depth in steel for 50 Hz AC: **≈ 1 mm**.
- Particle concentration in suspension: **0.1 – 0.4 %** by volume.
- Indication shape clue: **Linear** indications (parallel to flux) → defect; **Round** indications → non-relevant.
- Safety essentials: **UV-blocking goggles, gloves, dim booth inspection, firm workpiece clamping**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and Experiment on Ultrasonic Flaw Detector (Model Einstein-II TFT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIEL507 - QUALITY AND MATERIAL TESTING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Study and Experiment on Ultrasonic Flaw Detector (Model Einstein-II TFT) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) **Ultrasonic Flaw Detection (UFD)** is a Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) method that uses high-frequency mechanical sound waves (typically **0.5 MHz to 25 MHz**) propagating through a test material to detect internal discontinuities, measure thickness, and characterize material properties without causing damage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition Snapshot:** > *Ultrasonic Flaw Detector is a pulse-echo based electro-acoustic instrument that transmits ultrasonic waves into a material through a piezoelectric transducer, receives the reflected/reflected echoes from internal flaws or boundaries, and displays the resulting signal on a screen (A-scan/B-scan) to determine the presence, location, and size of defects.* The **Einstein-II TFT (Thin Film Transistor) model** is a portable, microprocessor-controlled digital ultrasonic flaw detector featuring a high-resolution color TFT LCD display, advanced DAC (Distance Amplitude Correction) curves, TCG (Time Corrected Gain), DGS (Distance-Gain-Size) sizing, and built-in calibration routines compliant with international standards such as **EN 12668, ASTM E317, and ASME Section V Article 5**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine standing in a long, empty hallway and shouting "Hello!" You hear your voice bounce back as an echo from the far wall. Now imagine the wall has a small hole — the echo will sound *different* (weaker, or come back faster if the hole is near you). **Ultrasonic flaw detection works exactly the same way**, but instead of sound we use mechanical vibrations **15,000 times higher in frequency** than the human hearing limit (hence "ultra-sonic"). The "shout" is sent via a special crystal (transducer) that converts electrical pulses into mechanical vibrations. The "echo" tells us: - **How far** the reflector is (defect or back wall) → **Time of Flight** - **How big** the reflector is → **Echo Amplitude** - **What shape** the reflector has → **Echo Phase / Profile** > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Bats Sonar" Principle:** > Bats navigate caves by emitting high-frequency chirps and listening for returning echoes. The Einstein-II TFT mimics this biologically, but with a precision of **nanoseconds**, allowing flaw detection accuracy down to **0.1 mm** in metals. ## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics & Constants | Parameter | Standard Value | Application | |---|---|---| | **Transducer Frequency (f)** | **1 MHz to 10 MHz** | Common flaw detection range | | **Velocity in Steel (Longitudinal, cL)** | **5,920 m/s** | Standard calibration velocity | | **Velocity in Steel (Shear, cS)** | **3,255 m/s** | Angle beam testing | | **Velocity in Aluminium (cL)** | **6,320 m/s** | Aluminium alloy inspection | | **Velocity in Water (cL)** | **1,480 m/s** | Immersion testing couplant | | **Wavelength in Steel @ 2.25 MHz** | **2.63 mm** | λ = c/f | | **Near Field Length (N)** | **D²f / (4c)** | Transducer geometry | | **Angle Beam Standard Angles** | **45°, 60°, 70°** | EN 1714 / AWS D1.1 | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Pulse-Echo Method The Einstein-II TFT operates primarily on the **Pulse-Echo (PE) technique**, where a single transducer acts as both transmitter and receiver. The operational sequence is: 1. **Pulser Stage** generates a high-voltage spike (typically **–100 V to –400 V** with sub-microsecond rise time). 2. **Damping / Snubber Network** shapes the pulse (controls bandwidth and axial resolution). 3. **Piezoelectric Transducer** converts electrical pulse → mechanical ultrasonic wave via the *inverse piezoelectric effect*. 4. **Couplant Layer** (gel, oil, water, or glycerin) ensures acoustic impedance continuity between probe and test piece. 5. **Ultrasonic Wave Propagates** through the material at the longitudinal velocity of sound. 6. **Echoes Return** from: Initial Pulse (IP) → Flaw Echo (F) → Back-Wall Echo (B). 7. **Receiver Amplifier** (with TGC/DAC) processes the returning signal. 8. **TFT Display** shows the A-scan (Amplitude vs Time) for operator interpretation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why TFT Display?** > Traditional CRT oscilloscopes had slow refresh and persistence issues. The TFT (Thin Film Transistor) LCD of Einstein-II provides a real-time, flicker-free waveform, allowing the operator to view **rapidly sweeping echoes at refresh rates up to 60 Hz** — critical for handheld scanning. ## 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How' | Step | Physical Phenomenon | Engineering Justification | |---|---|---| | Generate pulse | High-voltage excitation of PZT crystal | Produces narrow-band acoustic pulse | | Couplant application | Acoustic impedance matching | Eliminates air gap; Z_air ≈ 0.0004 MRayl, Z_steel ≈ 46 MRayl | | Wave propagation | Elastic wave in isotropic solid | Speed depends on material modulus and density | | Reflection at defect | Acoustic impedance mismatch | ΔZ = Z2 − Z1 produces partial reflection (R = ((Z2−Z1)/(Z2+Z1))²) | | Time measurement | Time-of-Flight (ToF) | Allows depth = (c × ToF) / 2 calculation | | Amplitude measurement | Echo height in A-scan | Correlates with flaw reflectivity & size | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Expression | Units | Used For | |---|---|---|---| | **Sound Velocity (Longitudinal)** | $c_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$ | m/s | Material velocity | | **Sound Velocity (Shear)** | $c_S = \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{2\rho(1+\nu)}}$ | m/s | Transverse wave | | **Wavelength** | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | m | Resolution & near-field | | **Depth of Flaw** | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | m | Pulse-echo calculation | | **Near Field Length** | $N = \dfrac{D^2 \cdot f}{4 \cdot c}$ | m | Transducer geometry | | **Beam Divergence Angle** | $\sin(\alpha) = \dfrac{1.22 \cdot \lambda}{D}$ | rad | Beam spread | | **Acoustic Impedance** | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Rayl (kg/m²·s) | Reflection coefficient | | **Reflection Coefficient (Pressure)** | $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | Echo strength | | **Transmission Coefficient** | $T = \dfrac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ | dimensionless | Energy through interface | | **Time of Flight (Path Length)** | $s = c \cdot t$ | m | Distance calculation | | **Angle Beam Path (Leg)** | $x = c_S \cdot t \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | m | Actual depth in angled testing | | **DAC (Distance-Amplitude Correction)** | $A_{DAC} = A_0 + 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{x}{x_0}\right)$ | dB | Reference curve generation | | **Attenuation Correction** | $\alpha_{dB} = \dfrac{20}{2x}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{B_0}{B_x}\right)$ | dB/m | Material loss | > [!NOTE] > **The '2' in the depth formula** comes from the round-trip nature of pulse-echo: the wave travels DOWN to the flaw and BACK UP to the same transducer, covering twice the depth. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application | Defects Detected | |---|---|---| | **Aerospace** | Turbine discs, composite panels | Delamination, porosity, inclusions | | **Pressure Vessel / Boiler** | Welds, pipe bends | Cracks, lack of fusion, slag | | **Shipbuilding (Cochin Shipyard, Kerala)** | Hull plates, propeller shafts | Lamellar tearing, fatigue cracks | | **Railways (ICF, Chennai)** | Axles, wheel sets | Transverse defects, sub-surface flaws | | **Oil & Gas (Kochi Refinery)** | Storage tank shells, pipelines | Corrosion, erosion, weld defects | | **Power Plants** | Boiler tubes, rotors | Wall thinning, creep damage | | **Automotive (Foundries)** | Castings, forgings | Shrinkage, hot tears, porosity | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Depth of Flaw from Time-of-Flight The Einstein-II TFT measures the time interval between the **Initial Pulse (IP)** and the **Flaw Echo (F)**. We need to relate this time to the physical depth of the flaw. **Given:** - Longitudinal velocity in steel: $c_L = 5{,}920$ m/s - Measured time-of-flight: $t$ (seconds, displayed on screen) **Step 1 — Identify the wave path:** The ultrasonic wave travels from the transducer to the flaw and reflects back to the same transducer. Total path length: $$L_{total} = L_{down} + L_{up}$$ **Step 2 — Express the path in terms of depth:** Since the wave travels perpendicular to the surface (straight-beam probe) and the flaw is directly below at depth $d$: $$L_{down} = d, \quad L_{up} = d$$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$L_{total} = d + d = 2d$$ **Step 4 — Apply the fundamental kinematic relation** $L = c \cdot t$: $$2d = c_L \cdot t$$ **Step 5 — Solve for depth $d$:** $$d = \frac{c_L \cdot t}{2}$$ **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** For a flaw echo appearing at $t = 6.76$ µs in steel: $$d = \frac{5920 \times (6.76 \times 10^{-6})}{2}$$ $$d = \frac{0.0400}{2} = 0.0200 \text{ m} = 20.0 \text{ mm}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Near Field Length (Fresnel Zone) The near field is the cylindrical region immediately in front of the transducer where the wave field is complex (interference maxima and minima). **Starting with Huygens' principle:** Each point on the transducer face acts as a secondary source. The on-axis pressure $p(r)$ at distance $r$ is given by: $$p(r) = 2 p_0 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{\lambda}\left[\sqrt{D^2/4 + r^2} - r\right]\right)$$ **Step 1 — Set the argument to $\pi/2$** (first axial maximum, the boundary of near field): $$\frac{\pi}{\lambda}\left[\sqrt{D^2/4 + N^2} - N\right] = \frac{\pi}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Simplify:** $$\sqrt{\frac{D^2}{4} + N^2} - N = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$ **Step 3 — For $N \gg D$**, expand using the binomial approximation: $$\sqrt{\frac{D^2}{4} + N^2} \approx N\left(1 + \frac{D^2}{8N^2}\right) = N + \frac{D^2}{8N}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:** $$\frac{D^2}{8N} = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $N$:** $$N = \frac{D^2}{4\lambda} = \frac{D^2 f}{4c}$$ **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A 2.25 MHz, Ø20 mm probe in steel: $$N = \frac{(0.020)^2 \times (2.25 \times 10^6)}{4 \times 5920}$$ $$N = \frac{900}{23{,}680} = 0.038 \text{ m} = 38 \text{ mm}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Angle Beam Path Length (Snell's Law Application) For an angle beam probe, the wave enters the material at a refracted angle $\theta$ (measured from normal). **Snell's Law at the couplant–metal interface:** $$\frac{\sin\theta_i}{c_L^{couplant}} = \frac{\sin\theta}{c_L^{metal}}$$ Since couplant velocity is very small (water ≈ 1480 m/s) and metal is much higher, $\theta_i$ is large and $\theta$ is small. **Step 1 — Path length along the beam axis:** The sound travels at shear velocity $c_S$ along the refracted beam. **Step 2 — For a flaw at horizontal distance $x$ from probe index point:** The time-of-flight along the beam: $$t = \frac{x / \cos\theta}{c_S}$$ **Step 3 — Solving for path length:** $$x = c_S \cdot t \cdot \cos\theta$$ **Step 4 — Vertical depth (leg) and horizontal distance (skip):** $$d_{leg} = c_S \cdot t \cdot \cos\theta$$ $$d_{skip} = c_S \cdot t \cdot \sin\theta$$ **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A 60° angle beam probe in steel. Flaw echo appears at $t = 10$ µs: $$d_{skip} = 3255 \times (10 \times 10^{-6}) \times \sin(60°)$$ $$d_{skip} = 0.03255 \times 0.866 = 0.0282 \text{ m} = 28.2 \text{ mm}$$ $$d_{leg} = 3255 \times (10 \times 10^{-6}) \times \cos(60°)$$ $$d_{leg} = 0.03255 \times 0.500 = 0.0163 \text{ m} = 16.3 \text{ mm}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: DAC (Distance Amplitude Curve) Construction Due to beam divergence and attenuation, echoes from identical flaws at greater depths appear smaller. The DAC compensates for this by **boosting receiver gain** as a function of time. **Step 1 — Identify the reference flaw** (typically Ø2 mm flat-bottom hole in IIW V2 block at a known depth $x_0$). **Step 2 — Record the echo amplitude** $A_0$ (in % Full Screen Height, FSH). **Step 3 — For a flaw at a different depth $x$**, the geometric attenuation factor is $x_0 / x$ (inverse square law for amplitude). **Step 4 — The DAC-corrected amplitude** in dB is: $$\Delta G_{DAC} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{x}{x_0}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Three-point DAC curve** is built by: - Point 1: Reference at depth $x_0$ → 0 dB - Point 2: Half-amplitude distance (6 dB down) at $2x_0$ - Point 3: Quarter-amplitude distance (12 dB down) at $4x_0$ > [!NOTE] > The Einstein-II TFT can store **up to 3 DAC curves** simultaneously, allowing multi-amplitude evaluation levels (e.g., –6 dB, –10 dB, –14 dB rejection criteria) per **EN 1712 / ASME** acceptance standards. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of Einstein-II TFT Ultrasonic Flaw Detector ```mermaid flowchart TB PSU[Power Supply Unit
220V AC / Li-Ion Battery] CPU[Microprocessor Controller
ARM Cortex-M4] MEM[Memory Bank
Calibration Data + Datalogger] PULSER[Pulser Stage
100-400V spike] DAMP[Damping Network
50-1000 ohm] RECV[Receiver Amplifier
0-110 dB TGC] DAC[DAC TCG Generator
Time-Corrected Gain] DET[Detector Rectifier
RF to Envelope] ADC[ADC
10-bit 100 MSPS] DSP[Digital Signal Processor
Filtering Peak Hold] TFT[TFT LCD Display
640x480 Color] KEY[Keypad Encoder
Function Keys] PRB[Probe Connector
LEMO 00] USB[USB B Port
Data Transfer] PRT[Thermal Printer
Optional] PSU --> CPU CPU --> MEM CPU --> PULSER PULSER --> DAMP DAMP --> PRB PRB --> RECV RECV --> DET RECV --> DAC DAC --> RECV DET --> ADC ADC --> DSP DSP --> TFT KEY --> CPU CPU --> USB CPU --> PRT ``` ## 4.2 Pulse-Echo Signal Path (Signal Flow Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Pulser Pulse
-200V Spike] --> P2[PZT Transducer
Converts V to Acoustic] P2 --> P3[Couplant Layer
Impedance Match] P3 --> P4[Test Material
Wave Propagation] P4 --> P5{Reflector Type} P5 -->|Internal Flaw| P6[Flaw Echo F] P5 -->|Rear Surface| P7[Back-Wall Echo B] P5 -->|Initial Pulse| P8[Initial Pulse IP] P8 --> P9[Receiver Amplifier
TGC Correction] P6 --> P9 P7 --> P9 P9 --> P10[DAC Correction
Curve Application] P10 --> P11[Peak Detector
Envelope] P11 --> P12[TFT Display
A-scan Trace] ``` ## 4.3 A-Scan Display Interpretation (Key Echoes) ```mermaid flowchart LR S0[Screen Left
t = 0 us] --> IP[Initial Pulse IP
Piezoelectric Ring-down] IP -->|Surface Wave| SW[Surface Wave SW
c = 3255 m/s] IP -->|Dead Zone| DZ[Dead Zone
0.5-2 us] IP -->|Longitudinal| LE[Longitudinal Echo L] LE -->|First Backwall| B1[1st Backwall B1] B1 --> B2[2nd Backwall B2] B2 --> B3[3rd Backwall B3] LE -.->|Reflects earlier| F1[Flaw Echo F1
Mid-thickness defect] F1 -.-> F2[Flaw Echo F2
Smaller sub-flaw] S0 -->|Monitor Zero| S0 ``` ## 4.4 Calibration Block Reference Diagram (IIW V2 Block) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IIW["IIW V2 Calibration Block - EN 12223"] RING[Ring at 25mm
Diameter 50mm for shear] T50H[50mm Thickness Scale] T25H[25mm Thickness Scale] ARC[Curved Arc 100mm radius] T12H[12mm T Scale] HOLE[5mm Diameter Hole at 44mm depth] end ``` ## 4.5 Experimental Procedure Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB ST1[Step 1: Equipment Setup
Connect Probe + Cal Block] ST2[Step 2: Power ON + Self Test] ST3[Step 3: Select Probe Type
Straight 2.25 MHz] ST4[Step 4: Set Velocity
5920 m/s Steel] ST5[Step 5: Distance Calibration
Set Zero on 25mm] ST6[Step 6: Sensitivity Calibration
Set 80 percent FSH on 25mm] ST7[Step 7: DAC Curve Generation
3 points from V2 block] ST8[Step 8: Test Piece Scan
Apply couplant and scan] ST9[Step 9: Identify Flaws
Mark F echoes vs B echoes] ST10[Step 10: Measure and Record
Depth Amplitude Position] ST11[Step 11: Compare to DAC
Evaluate acceptance] ST12[Step 12: Generate Report
Save to USB] ST1 --> ST2 --> ST3 --> ST4 --> ST5 --> ST6 --> ST7 --> ST8 --> ST9 --> ST10 --> ST11 --> ST12 ``` ## 4.6 Couplant Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Choose Couplant] --> B{Test Temperature} B -->|< 50 degree C| C[Standard Gel or Glycerin] B -->|50-200 degree C| D[High-Temp Grease
Silicone Based] B -->|200-400 degree C| E[Special HT Couplant
Sodium Silicate] C --> F{Surface Roughness} D --> F E --> F F -->|Smooth| G[Light Oil or Water] F -->|Rough| H[Thick Paste or Paste Wax] F -->|Vertical Overhead| I[Gel Non-Drip Type] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **Define ultrasonic flaw detection. State the velocity of longitudinal ultrasonic waves in steel.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (2 marks):** Ultrasonic flaw detection is a non-destructive testing method that uses high-frequency mechanical waves (typically 1–10 MHz) propagated through a test material to detect internal discontinuities. A piezoelectric transducer generates the waves, and reflections from flaws are analyzed to determine their location, size, and orientation. - **Velocity in steel (1 mark):** $c_L = 5{,}920$ m/s ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **List any four controls provided on the front panel of an Ultrasonic Flaw Detector (Einstein-II TFT).** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Range Selector** — Sets horizontal time/distance scale (e.g., 25–1000 mm) 2. **Gain Control** — Adjusts receiver amplification (0–110 dB) 3. **Delay Control** — Shifts the start of the time base 4. **Reject (Suppression)** — Filters low-amplitude noise 5. **DAC/TCG Switch** — Activates distance-amplitude correction 6. **Probe Frequency Selector** — Selects matched transducer *(Any four with brief description — 1/2 mark each + 1 mark for accuracy)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional operation of an Ultrasonic Flaw Detector. Describe the role of each block. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** An ultrasonic flaw detector is a pulse-echo instrument that transmits ultrasonic pulses and analyzes returning echoes. The block diagram consists of: 1. **Pulser / Synchronizer (1 mark):** Generates a high-voltage spike (–100 to –400 V) at a fixed pulse repetition frequency (PRF) of typically 1–2 kHz. The synchronizer triggers the time base of the CRT/TFT display simultaneously, ensuring the displayed sweep starts when the pulse is emitted. 2. **Transmitter / Damping Network (1 mark):** The damping network (typically 50–1000 Ω) is placed across the piezoelectric crystal to control the pulse width. Lower damping → narrower pulse → better axial resolution, but lower sensitivity. 3. **Probe (Transducer) (1 mark):** A piezoelectric crystal (PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate) converts electrical energy to mechanical vibrations via the inverse piezoelectric effect. The Einstein-II uses dual-element or single-element probes of 1–10 MHz. 4. **Receiver / Amplifier (1 mark):** The returning echoes are amplified (0 to 110 dB). TGC (Time-Corrected Gain) compensates for material attenuation, increasing gain with depth. 5. **Detector and Display (1 mark):** The RF signal is rectified and filtered, then displayed on the TFT as an A-scan (Amplitude vs Time). 6. **Time Base / Horizontal Sweep (1 mark):** A linear ramp generator drives the horizontal deflection. The sweep speed is calibrated to material velocity (m/s) for direct depth reading. 7. **Calibration and Memory Unit (1 mark):** Stores calibration data (velocity, zero offset, DAC curves) and saves inspection reports. **(b)** An ultrasonic flaw detector operates at 4 MHz with a steel test piece. A flaw echo is observed at 8 µs and a back-wall echo at 24 µs. Calculate: (i) the depth of the flaw, and (ii) the thickness of the test piece. Take $c_L = 5{,}920$ m/s. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - Frequency $f = 4$ MHz - Time of flaw echo $t_f = 8$ µs $= 8 \times 10^{-6}$ s - Time of back-wall echo $t_b = 24$ µs $= 24 \times 10^{-6}$ s - Velocity $c_L = 5{,}920$ m/s **(i) Depth of flaw (3.5 marks):** The pulse travels down to the flaw and back (round trip). Hence: $$d_{flaw} = \frac{c_L \cdot t_f}{2}$$ $$d_{flaw} = \frac{5920 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$ $$d_{flaw} = \frac{0.04736}{2} = 0.02368 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{d_{flaw} = 23.68 \text{ mm} \approx 23.7 \text{ mm}}$$ **(ii) Thickness of test piece (3.5 marks):** $$T = \frac{c_L \cdot t_b}{2}$$ $$T = \frac{5920 \times 24 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$ $$T = \frac{0.14208}{2} = 0.07104 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{T = 71.04 \text{ mm} \approx 71 \text{ mm}}$$ --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]** **(a)** Explain the Pulse-Echo method of ultrasonic testing. Compare it with the Through-Transmission method. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Pulse-Echo Method (4 marks):** In the pulse-echo technique, a single probe acts as both transmitter and receiver. A short-duration high-voltage pulse excites the piezoelectric crystal, which emits an ultrasonic wave into the material. The wave travels through the test piece and reflects from discontinuities (flaws) and the back wall. The same probe receives the returning echoes. The time interval between the initial pulse and the flaw echo gives the depth of the flaw using $d = ct/2$. *Advantages:* Single-side access; can locate flaw depth directly; can detect both volumetric and planar defects. *Disadvantages:* Dead zone near surface; weaker signal from small flaws. **Through-Transmission Method (3 marks):** Two probes are used — one transmitter and one receiver, placed on opposite faces of the test piece. The presence of a flaw is indicated by a reduction or complete loss of the received signal (shadowing effect). | Aspect | Pulse-Echo | Through-Transmission | |---|---|---| | Probes required | 1 (same side) | 2 (opposite sides) | | Depth measurement | Yes (ToF) | No (only presence) | | Access required | Single side | Both sides | | Sensitivity to small flaws | High | Higher (no dead zone) | | Couplant issues | One side | Two sides | | Calibration | Easier | More complex | **(b)** A 2 MHz, Ø20 mm probe is used in steel.
