## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid Block Diagrams)

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Complete Acoustic Emission Test System

This Mermaid diagram shows the full signal path from mechanical event in the specimen to the operator display.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Specimen under load] --> A2[Crack growth / fiber break / dislocation]
    A2 --> A3[Elastic wave emitted]
    A3 --> A4[PZT AE Sensor 50-500 kHz]
    A4 --> A5[Preamplifier 20-60 dB]
    A5 --> A6[Bandpass Filter 20 kHz-1 MHz]
    A6 --> A7[Threshold Detector]
    A7 --> A8[ADC Sampling 5 MSPS]
    A8 --> A9[AE Processor]
    A9 --> A10[Display: Amplitude Counts Energy]
    A9 --> A11[Storage: Waveform and Hits]
    A9 --> A12[Source Location Triangulation]
    A9 --> A13[Alarm Output: FR and b-value]
```

**Reading the Diagram (left → right):**
1. The **specimen** is mechanically loaded.
2. **Damage** (crack, fiber break, dislocation glide) releases energy.
3. An **elastic wave** propagates through the material.
4. The **PZT sensor** converts the wave to a voltage.
5. The **preamplifier** boosts the weak signal (often microvolts).
6. The **bandpass filter** removes low-frequency noise (e.g., 50 Hz mains).
7. The **threshold detector** discriminates real AE from electronic noise.
8. The **ADC** digitizes the analog signal at high sampling rate.
9. The **AE processor** extracts all parameters.
10. Real-time display, data storage, source triangulation, and alarms are generated.

---

### 4.2 AE Test Procedure — Experimental Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Start of Experiment] --> B1[Couple AE sensor to specimen with high-vacuum grease]
    B1 --> B2[Connect sensor to preamplifier and AE system]
    B2 --> B3[Perform Pencil Lead Break Hsu-Nielsen test]
    B3 --> B4{Are all sensors detecting the break clearly?}
    B4 -- No --> B5[Re-couple sensor and adjust gain]
    B5 --> B3
    B4 -- Yes --> B6[Set threshold to 35 dB recommended]
    B6 --> B7[Mount specimen in UTM]
    B7 --> B8[Apply monotonic tensile load 0 to failure]
    B8 --> B9[Record AE hits amplitude counts energy]
    B9 --> B10[Identify Kaiser and Felicity thresholds]
    B10 --> B11[Plot Amplitude vs Time]
    B11 --> B12[Plot Cumulative Counts vs Stress]
    B12 --> B13[Calculate Felicity Ratio and b-value]
    B13 --> B14[Correlate AE with fracture point]
    B14 --> B15[End of Experiment - Report]
```

---

### 4.3 AE Waveform — Parameter Identification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Time axis] --> C1
    subgraph C1 ["AE Burst Waveform"]
        C2[Threshold voltage line]
        C3[First threshold crossing] --> C4[Rise time R]
        C4 --> C5[Peak Amplitude A in dB]
        C5 --> C6[Counts to Peak Cp]
        C6 --> C7[Decay envelope]
        C7 --> C8[Last threshold crossing]
        C3 --> C9[Total Duration D]
        C9 --> C8
        C3 --> C10[Total Counts N ring-down]
    end
```

**Reading the waveform diagram:**
- The horizontal line is the **threshold voltage V_T**.
- The burst begins at the **first threshold crossing**.
- The **rise time** is from first crossing to the peak.
- The **amplitude** is the height of the peak above threshold (in dB).
- The **duration** is the total time the signal stays above threshold.
- **Counts (N)** is the number of times the burst crosses the threshold.
- **Energy** is the area under the rectified envelope.

---

### 4.4 AE Source Mechanisms in Different Materials

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[AE Source Mechanisms] --> D1[Metals]
    D0 --> D2[Composites]
    D0 --> D3[Ceramics]
    D0 --> D4[Concrete]

    D1 --> D1a[Dislocation glide and twinning]
    D1 --> D1b[Microvoid nucleation and growth]
    D1 --> D1c[Ductile crack propagation]
    D1 --> D1d[Fatigue crack growth]

    D2 --> D2a[Fiber breakage]
    D2 --> D2b[Matrix cracking]
    D2 --> D2c[Fiber-matrix debonding]
    D2 --> D2d[Delamination]

    D3 --> D3a[Microcrack initiation]
    D3 --> D3b[Grain boundary sliding]
    D3 --> D3c[Phase transformation]

    D4 --> D4a[Aggregate fracture]
    D4 --> D4b[Mortar cracking]
    D4 --> D4c[Rebar slip]
    D4 --> D4d[Corrosion expansion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common 7-mark question asks, *"List five AE source mechanisms in fiber-reinforced composites."* Use the four sub-nodes under D2 above (and add **fiber pull-out** for the fifth) to score full marks.

---

### 4.5 Simplified Lab Setup — Schematic for the KTU Practical Record

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[UTM Hydraulic Cylinder] --> E2[Steel Coupon with extensometer]
    E2 --> E3[AE Sensor 1 - left]
    E2 --> E4[AE Sensor 2 - center]
    E2 --> E5[AE Sensor 3 - right]
    E3 --> E6[AE System Front End]
    E4 --> E6
    E5 --> E6
    E6 --> E7[Laptop with AEwin Software]
    E7 --> E8[Real-time display of hits]
    E7 --> E9[Data file in .CSV]
    E7 --> E10[Post-processing b-value Felicity]
```

**Practical Setup Notes for KTU Lab:**
- Use a **tensile dog-bone specimen** (mild steel or aluminium).
- Mount **two or three** AE sensors along the gauge length.
- Use **high-vacuum grease** as couplant; tape sensors with cellophane tape.
- Set **threshold to 35–40 dB** for steel, **45–50 dB** for composites.
- **Pre-load to 5% of expected UTS** to settle noise; then start AE recording.
- Apply tensile load at **0.5–2 mm/min** crosshead speed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU Board Exam — Part A: 3-Mark Conceptual Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define Acoustic Emission Testing. List any two differences between Acoustic Emission and Ultrasonic Testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Acoustic Emission (AE) is a non-destructive testing method in which transient elastic waves generated by the sudden release of energy within a material (such as crack growth, plastic deformation, or fiber failure) are detected and analyzed to assess the integrity of the component in real time.

| Acoustic Emission (AE) | Ultrasonic Testing (UT) |
|---|---|
| **Passive** technique — it *listens* to energy released by the material. | **Active** technique — it *sends* an ultrasonic pulse into the part. |
| Detects **active / growing** defects in real time. | Detects **existing** (dormant) defects via reflection. |
| Can monitor a **large area** with a few sensors. | Requires **scanning** point-by-point. |

**(1 mark)** Definition, **(1 mark)** table of two differences.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Explain the Kaiser Effect and the Felicity Effect in acoustic emission testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Kaiser Effect** is the phenomenon in which a material that has been previously loaded to a certain stress level does **not** produce significant acoustic emission until the previously applied stress level is **exceeded** during reloading. It reflects the "memory" of the material and is used to determine the previous maximum stress experienced by a structure. **(1.5 marks)**

The **Felicity Effect** is the inverse phenomenon observed in **damaged materials, composites, and fatigued metals**, where AE begins at a stress level **lower** than the previous maximum applied stress. The **Felicity Ratio** (σ_onset / σ_max_previous) becomes less than 1.0. A Felicity Ratio below 0.95 indicates active damage requiring engineering attention. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 KTU Board Exam — Part B: 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an Acoustic Emission Testing system. List the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A steel specimen is loaded in a Universal Testing Machine under tensile load. During the first loading cycle, significant AE was detected when stress reached **220 MPa**. The specimen was unloaded to 0 MPa. During the second loading cycle, AE was first detected at a stress of **190 MPa**. Calculate the **Felicity Ratio** and comment on the structural condition of the specimen. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Working of an AE System (7 marks)

The acoustic emission testing system consists of the following sequential blocks:

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **1. Specimen under load** | Provides the source of mechanical disturbance. |
| **2. AE Sensor (PZT)** | Converts elastic wave into electrical signal (resonant at 50–500 kHz). |
| **3. Couplant** | Grease/wax to transmit wave from specimen to sensor. |
| **4. Preamplifier (20–60 dB)** | Amplifies the microvolt-level sensor signal. |
| **5. Bandpass Filter (20 kHz–1 MHz)** | Removes low-frequency mechanical noise and high-frequency EMI. |
| **6. Threshold Detector** | Rejects noise; only signals above set threshold (dB) are recorded. |
| **7. ADC** | Samples the analog signal at ≥ 5 MSPS for digital storage. |
| **8. AE Processor** | Computes amplitude, counts, duration, energy, RMS, frequency. |
| **9. Display / Storage** | Real-time plots, hit list, waveform files. |
| **10. Source Locator** | Triangulates damage location from arrival time differences. |
| **11. Alarm / Output** | Triggers when Felicity Ratio drops or hits exceed limit. |

*(Full diagram should be drawn — refer to Section 4.1.)* **(7 marks = 1 mark diagram + 6 marks block-wise explanation)**

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Felicity Ratio Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Previous maximum stress: $\sigma_{max} = 220\ \text{MPa}$
- AE onset stress on reloading: $\sigma_{onset} = 190\ \text{MPa}$

**Formula:**

$$
FR = \frac{\sigma_{onset}}{\sigma_{max,previous}}
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
FR = \frac{190\ \text{MPa}}{220\ \text{MPa}}
$$

**Computation:**

$$
FR = 0.864
$$

**Interpretation Table:**

| Felicity Ratio Range | Structural Condition |
|---|---|
| FR ≥ 1.0 | Kaiser effect holds — material healthy |
| 0.95 ≤ FR < 1.0 | Minor damage |
| 0.85 ≤ FR < 0.95 | **Significant damage** ← our result |
| FR < 0.85 | Severe damage |

**Conclusion:** Since FR = **0.864** (which is < 0.95), the specimen has **significant internal damage** (likely from accumulated plastic strain, microcracking, or sub-surface defects). The Kaiser effect has been **violated**, indicating the structure is no longer fit for service without further inspection and possible repair. **(7 marks = 2 marks formula, 2 marks calculation, 3 marks interpretation)**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Define the following AE parameters with units: **Amplitude, Counts, Duration, Rise Time, Energy, RMS Voltage, Felicity Ratio**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 5 mm thick aluminium plate is monitored with two AE sensors placed 300 mm apart. The wave speed in aluminium is **5,100 m/s**. A pencil lead is broken at a point, and the two sensors record arrival times of **t₁ = 12 µs** and **t₂ = 42 µs**. Find the location of the source from sensor 1. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — AE Parameters (7 marks — 1 mark each)

| Parameter | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Amplitude** | Peak voltage of the largest excursion in the AE hit, expressed in dB. | **dB AE** |
| **Counts (N)** | Number of threshold crossings within the duration of the hit. | dimensionless |
| **Duration (D)** | Time between the first and last threshold crossing. | **µs** |
| **Rise Time (R)** | Time from first threshold crossing to peak amplitude. | **µs** |
| **Energy (MARSE)** | Area under the rectified signal envelope. | **eu** (energy units) |
| **RMS Voltage (V_rms)** | Square root of the mean of the squared voltage — continuous AE power. | **V** or **dB** |
| **Felicity Ratio (FR)** | Ratio of AE onset stress on reloading to the previous maximum stress. | dimensionless |

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Source Location (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Distance between sensors: $D = 300\ \text{mm} = 0.3\ \text{m}$
- Wave speed: $c = 5100\ \text{m/s} = 5.1\ \text{mm}/\mu s$
- Arrival times: $t_1 = 12\ \mu s$, $t_2 = 42\ \mu s$

**Step 1 — Calculate the arrival time difference:**

$$
\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = 42 - 12 = 30\ \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the linear source location formula:**

$$
x_1 = \frac{D - c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

(Note: for collinear sensors and source *between* them, the source is closer to sensor 1 since it arrived first.)

**Step 3 — Compute c · Δt:**

$$
c \cdot \Delta t = 5.1 \times 30 = 153\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute x₁:**

$$
x_1 = \frac{300 - 153}{2} = \frac{147}{2} = 73.5\ \text{mm}
$$

**Verification:** Distance from sensor 2: $x_2 = D - x_1 = 300 - 73.5 = 226.5\ \text{mm}$.
Time difference check: $\Delta t_{calc} = (x_2 - x_1)/c = (226.5 - 73.5)/5.1 = 153/5.1 = 30\ \mu s$. ✓

**Conclusion:** The source (pencil lead break) is located **73.5 mm from sensor 1** (and 226.5 mm from sensor 2). **(7 marks = 2 marks Δt, 3 marks formula and substitution, 2 marks final answer with verification)**

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS on Acoustic Emission questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Amplitude" with "Counts"** — Amplitude is the *peak voltage* (in dB); Counts is the *number of threshold crossings*. These are **different parameters**.
> 2. **Using dB in a logarithm** — You **cannot** take $\log_{10}$ of a dB value. Always convert dB → voltage ratio first.
> 3. **Forgetting units** — Felicity Ratio is dimensionless, but stress is in MPa, amplitude in dB AE, counts dimensionless, time in µs.
> 4. **Not drawing the threshold line** on waveform diagrams — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the Hsu-Nielsen source** — When asked to calibrate an AE system, always mention the **2H pencil lead break (Hsu-Nielsen source)**.
> 6. **Confusing "Active" and "Passive" NDT** — AE is **passive** (it listens); do not say "AE sends waves into the material."
> 7. **No diagram in 7-mark questions** — Always include a **labeled block diagram** in (a) part questions; 1 mark is reserved for it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **One-look Revision Block — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ **AE = Passive NDT** that **listens** to elastic waves from active damage in real time.
- ☐ **Frequency range:** 20 kHz – 1 MHz; sensors are **resonant PZT** (50, 150, 300, 500 kHz).
- ☐ **Standard cal source:** Hsu-Nielsen = **2H pencil lead break** (0.5 mm diameter).
- ☐ **Key parameters:** Amplitude (dB), Counts, Duration (µs), Rise Time (µs), Energy (eu), RMS, Frequency.
- ☐ **Threshold** is set typically at **35–40 dB** for steel, **45–50 dB** for composites.
- ☐ **Amplitude in dB:** $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V/V_{ref})$; V_ref = **1 µV**.
- ☐ **Kaiser Effect:** No AE until previous max stress is exceeded. Memory effect.
- ☐ **Felicity Effect:** AE starts *below* previous max stress in damaged material. FR < 1.
- ☐ **Felicity Ratio = σ_onset / σ_max_previous.** FR < 0.95 ⇒ significant damage.
- ☐ **b-value analysis:** b drops from ~0.10 to ~0.02 just before fracture (macro-event precursor).
- ☐ **Attenuation:** $\alpha = (1/x) \ln(A_1/A_2)$ — convert dB to linear ratio first.
- ☐ **Source location:** Use arrival-time differences and known wave speed c.
- ☐ **Lamb waves** dominate in thin plates; **Longitudinal + Shear** in thick sections.
- ☐ **AE detects active defects only** — not dormant ones (this is the *key advantage*).
- ☐ **Common uses:** Tank floor corrosion, bridge cable monitoring, FRP delamination, weld monitoring, leak detection.
- ☐ **KTU Standards to memorize:** **ASTM E1106**, **ASTM E2191**, **ASTM E569**, **ASME V-12**.
- ☐ **Lab safety:** Always use couplant; check sensor cables; ensure load fixture is aligned.

---

> **END OF MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 NOTES — PCIEL507: Quality and Material Testing Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and Experiment on Non-Destructive Evaluation Using Thermal Camera
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL507 - QUALITY AND MATERIAL TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and Experiment on Non-Destructive Evaluation Using Thermal Camera

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE) using a thermal camera is a contact-free, infrared-based diagnostic technique used to identify subsurface anomalies, delaminations, moisture ingress, voids, and thermal bridging in civil engineering materials and structures.

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Infrared Thermography (IRT)** is a non-destructive, non-contact, imaging-based testing method that converts the invisible infrared radiation (thermal energy) emitted by a material's surface into a visible, two-dimensional temperature map called a **thermogram**. The principle is rooted in the **Planck's Law of Blackbody Radiation**, where every object above absolute zero (0 K) emits electromagnetic radiation whose intensity and spectral distribution are functions of the object's surface temperature and emissivity.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for Civil Engineering labs, the experiment is defined as:

> "The use of a calibrated **thermal imaging camera (FLIR / Testo / IR camera)** to detect and record surface temperature variations in structural elements (concrete, masonry, RCC slabs, walls, composite laminates) caused by internal discontinuities such as **delamination, voids, honeycombing, rebars, moisture content, cracks, and thermal bridges** — without causing any damage to the structure."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are standing in a dark room with a wall ahead of you. You cannot see the wall surface, but you can *feel* warmth coming from one corner (because a heater is behind it) and coolness from another corner. Your skin acts like a "thermal sensor." A thermal camera does exactly this — but with extreme precision, converting invisible heat into a coloured picture where **hot regions appear red/white** and **cold regions appear blue/black**. Just as a doctor uses a medical forehead thermometer to detect a fever without touching the body, an engineer uses a thermal camera to detect "sick" zones inside a building without breaking it open.

**Real-World Picture:** When a building wall is exposed to sunlight, areas with **voids or delamination** underneath the plaster heat up faster than sound concrete because trapped air is a poor conductor. The wall surface above a defect therefore appears as a "hot spot" in the thermal image, betraying the hidden flaw.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant (σ):** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- **Wien's Displacement Constant (b):** $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m·K}$
- **Typical IR Camera Spectral Range:** **8 µm – 14 µm** (Long-Wave Infrared / LWIR)
- **Standard Emissivity (ε) of Concrete:** **0.93 – 0.96** (highly emissive, near-blackbody)
- **Standard Emissivity (ε) of Polished Steel:** **0.05 – 0.10** (highly reflective, low emitter)
- **Thermal Sensitivity (NETD):** **< 0.05 K** (modern cameras)
- **Recommended Test Standards:** **ASTM D4788-03**, **ASTM C1060**, **ISO 6781**, **BS 8706**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Emissivity (ε):** The ratio of thermal radiation emitted by a real surface to that emitted by a perfect blackbody at the same temperature. ε = 1 (blackbody), ε = 0 (perfect reflector). Always calibrate emissivity before scanning.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Physics of Infrared Thermography

The operational logic of thermal NDE rests on **three pillars**:

1. **All matter emits thermal radiation** as a function of its temperature (above 0 K).
2. **Defects alter the heat flow** within a material, producing surface temperature contrasts.
3. **A thermal camera detects these contrasts** and translates them into a colour-mapped image.

### 2.2 Modes of Infrared Thermography

- **Passive Thermography:** The natural thermal contrast (e.g., sun heating, ambient thermal gradients) is used. No external heat source is applied. **Common in building envelope inspections** for finding moisture, missing insulation, and air leakage.
- **Active Thermography:** An **external heat source** (halogen lamp, flash lamp, hot air blower, ultrasonic excitation) is applied to one side of the test object. The opposite side is monitored with the camera. Internal flaws disrupt heat diffusion and become visible.
  - **Pulsed Thermography:** A short (milliseconds) heat pulse is applied; transient cooling is observed.
  - **Lock-in (Modulated) Thermography:** Sinusoidally modulated heat input; phase images reveal deeper defects.
  - **Vibrothermography:** Ultrasonic excitation generates frictional heat at crack faces.

### 2.3 Heat Transfer Mechanisms Involved

The transport of heat through a structural member — and the disruption caused by defects — follows three classical modes:

- **Conduction** — through the solid matrix (concrete, steel). Governed by **Fourier's Law**.
- **Convection** — at the surface (air boundary layer). Governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**.
- **Radiation** — between surface and surroundings / camera sensor. Governed by the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**.

### 2.4 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

**Step 1 — Thermal Excitation:** A temperature gradient is established across the test object (by sun, by heating, by cooling).

**Step 2 — Heat Diffusion:** Heat flows through the material by conduction. Solid, dense regions conduct heat uniformly. Voids, cracks, and delaminations act as **thermal resistors**, trapping heat on the excited side.

**Step 3 — Surface Mapping:** The surface temperature distribution is captured by the IR camera. Sound areas show smooth, uniform temperature; defect areas show **anomalous hot/cold spots** (thermal contrast, $\Delta T$).

**Step 4 — Image Interpretation:** The thermogram is post-processed using software (e.g., FLIR Tools, ThermaCAM Researcher) to enhance contrast, remove reflections, and quantify the thermal anomalies.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** | $E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4}$ | $E$ = Radiant exitance (W/m²), $\varepsilon$ = Emissivity (0–1), $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴, $T$ = Absolute temperature (K) | Gives total IR energy emitted by a real surface — basis of thermal camera reading |
| 2 | **Wien's Displacement Law** | $\lambda_{max} \, T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | $\lambda_{max}$ = Peak emission wavelength (m), $T$ = Temperature (K) | Determines why LWIR (8–14 µm) cameras are used for room-temperature objects (~300 K) |
| 3 | **Planck's Spectral Distribution** | $E_{\lambda} = \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda kT}-1}$ | $h$ = Planck's constant, $c$ = Speed of light, $k$ = Boltzmann constant, $\lambda$ = Wavelength | Fundamental law describing spectral radiance of blackbody |
| 4 | **Newton's Law of Cooling** | $q = h \, (T_{s} - T_{\infty})$ | $q$ = Heat flux (W/m²), $h$ = Convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K), $T_s$ = Surface temperature, $T_{\infty}$ = Ambient | Models convective heat loss from a wall to surrounding air |
| 5 | **Fourier's Law (1-D)** | $q = -k \, \frac{dT}{dx}$ | $k$ = Thermal conductivity (W/mK), $\frac{dT}{dx}$ = Temperature gradient (K/m) | Models heat conduction through concrete / steel thickness |
| 6 | **Thermal Diffusivity** | $\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \, c_{p}}$ | $\alpha$ (m²/s), $k$ = Thermal conductivity, $\rho$ = Density (kg/m³), $c_p$ = Specific heat (J/kgK) | Characterizes how fast temperature changes propagate; defects reduce effective $\alpha$ |
| 7 | **Thermal Penetration Depth (pulsed)** | $\mu = \sqrt{2 \alpha \, t}$ | $\mu$ = Penetration depth (m), $\alpha$ = Diffusivity, $t$ = Time (s) | Defines maximum depth visible to active thermography for a given heating time |
| 8 | **Thermal Contrast (ΔT)** | $\Delta T = T_{defect} - T_{sound}$ | Both temperatures in °C or K | Key NDE indicator — quantitative measure of anomaly visibility |
| 9 | **Apparent vs True Temperature** | $T_{true} = \sqrt[4]{\frac{T_{app}^{4} - (1-\varepsilon)T_{refl}^{4}}{\varepsilon}}$ | $T_{app}$ = Apparent temperature measured, $T_{refl}$ = Reflected temperature | Required correction to convert radiometric reading to true surface temperature |
| 10 | **Emissivity Adjustment** | $\varepsilon_{mix} = \sum f_{i} \, \varepsilon_{i}$ | $f_i$ = Area fraction of i-th material, $\varepsilon_i$ = Emissivity of i-th material | Used for mixed (e.g., patched) surfaces in lab samples |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the Stefan-Boltzmann $T^4$ dependence and Wien's Law — they appear almost every year in Part A and Part B. The temperature appears to the **fourth power**, meaning a small change in $T$ produces a large change in emitted radiation — this is what makes thermal cameras extremely sensitive.

### 2.6 Real-World Civil Engineering Utility

- **Detection of delamination in bridge decks and parking structures** (ASTM D4788).
- **Locating rebars, post-tensioning ducts, and embedded conduits** in concrete before drilling/cutting.
- **Identifying moisture ingress, rising damp, and roof leaks** in building envelopes.
- **Energy audit of buildings** — finding missing insulation, thermal bridges, and air leakage paths.
- **Inspection of FRP-strengthened concrete members** for debonding.
- **Quality control of composite laminates and weld joints** in industrial settings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Why a Thermal Camera "Sees" Temperature (Stefan-Boltzmann)

The total energy emitted per unit area per unit time by a perfect blackbody is obtained by integrating Planck's spectral radiance over all wavelengths and over the full hemisphere (solid angle $2\pi$).

Starting from Planck's Law for spectral radiance per unit solid angle:

$$L_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{2 h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1}$$

Spectral exitance is obtained by multiplying by $\pi$ (Lambertian emission):

$$E_{\lambda} = \pi \cdot L_{\lambda} = \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1}$$

Integrating over all wavelengths from $0$ to $\infty$:

$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1} \; d\lambda$$

Apply the substitution $x = \frac{hc}{\lambda kT}$, so that $\lambda = \frac{hc}{xkT}$ and $d\lambda = -\frac{hc}{x^{2} kT} \, dx$:

$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{(hc/xkT)^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{x} - 1} \cdot \frac{hc}{x^{2} kT} \, dx$$

$$E = \frac{2 \pi k^{4} T^{4}}{h^{3} c^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx$$

The definite integral is a known result (Bose-Einstein integral):

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx = \frac{\pi^{4}}{15}$$

Substituting back:

$$E = \frac{2 \pi k^{4} T^{4}}{h^{3} c^{2}} \cdot \frac{\pi^{4}}{15} = \frac{2 \pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} \cdot T^{4}$$

Define the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**:

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}^{4}$$

For a **real (grey) body** of emissivity $\varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4}}$$

**Engineering meaning:** The camera's detector measures $E$, inverts this equation, and after correcting for $\varepsilon$ and $T_{refl}$, reports the surface temperature $T$. Any subsurface defect that perturbs $T$ is therefore directly observable.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermal Penetration Depth in Active Thermography

For a semi-infinite solid subjected to a step change in surface temperature, the transient temperature response is given by the **1-D heat conduction solution** (Carslaw & Jaeger):

$$T(x, t) - T_{i} = (T_{s} - T_{i}) \cdot \text{erfc}\!\left( \frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} \right)$$

where $\text{erfc}$ is the complementary error function, $T_i$ is the initial temperature, $T_s$ is the surface temperature, $x$ is the depth, $t$ is elapsed time, and $\alpha$ is the thermal diffusivity.

The **characteristic depth** at which the temperature disturbance has effectively penetrated is conventionally defined as the depth at which the temperature change is $\frac{1}{e}$ ($\approx 36.8\%$) of the surface change.

Setting the argument of $\text{erfc}$ equal to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \;\Rightarrow\; x = 2\sqrt{\alpha t} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2 \alpha t}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = \sqrt{2 \alpha t}}$$

**Example (KTU-style numerical):** For a concrete sample with $\alpha = 0.8 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$, find the maximum detectable depth after 60 seconds of heating.

$$\mu = \sqrt{2 \times (0.8 \times 10^{-6}) \times 60}$$

$$\mu = \sqrt{96 \times 10^{-6}} = \sqrt{0.000096}$$

$$\mu = 9.798 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m} \approx 9.8 \;\text{mm}$$

**Interpretation:** A defect deeper than ~10 mm will not produce a measurable surface thermal contrast for this heating duration. To inspect deeper, increase $t$ (longer heating) or use a more energetic source (flash lamp).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Thermal Contrast (ΔT) from a Subsurface Void

Consider a concrete slab of thickness $L$ with a thin air-filled void of thickness $d$ at depth $z$ from the heated surface. The thermal resistance of the void is much higher than that of sound concrete.

Treat the slab as a 1-D thermal circuit with two resistances in series — sound concrete (top) and concrete + void (bottom):

$$R_{sound} = \frac{L}{k_{c}}, \quad R_{defect} = \frac{(L - z)}{k_{c}} + \frac{d}{k_{air}}$$

Since $k_{air} \approx 0.026 \; \text{W/mK}$ and $k_{c} \approx 1.4 \; \text{W/mK}$:

$$\frac{d}{k_{air}} \gg \frac{d}{k_{c}}$$

The heat flux in the defective region is reduced because of the higher resistance. For a constant heat input $Q$ applied to the surface:

$$T_{defect, surface} - T_{back} = Q \cdot R_{defect}, \quad T_{sound, surface} - T_{back} = Q \cdot R_{sound}$$

The **thermal contrast** observed at the surface is:

$$\Delta T = T_{defect} - T_{sound} = Q (R_{defect} - R_{sound}) = Q \cdot \frac{d}{k_{air}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T = \frac{Q \cdot d}{k_{air}}}$$

**Example:** For $Q = 200 \; \text{W/m}^{2}$ applied to a concrete surface, with a 2 mm air void:

$$\Delta T = \frac{200 \times 0.002}{0.026} = 15.38 \; \text{K}$$

This 15.4 K contrast is **easily detectable** by a thermal camera with NETD of 0.05 K — the defect will appear as a clearly hot spot.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: True Temperature from Radiometric Reading

A thermal camera measures **apparent temperature** $T_{app}$, which combines the true emitted radiation with reflected ambient radiation:

$$E_{measured} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T_{true}^{4} + (1 - \varepsilon) \, \sigma \, T_{refl}^{4}$$

Dividing by $\sigma$ and isolating $T_{true}$:

$$T_{true}^{4} = \frac{T_{app}^{4} - (1 - \varepsilon) T_{refl}^{4}}{\varepsilon}$$

$$\boxed{T_{true} = \sqrt[4]{\frac{T_{app}^{4} - (1 - \varepsilon) T_{refl}^{4}}{\varepsilon}}}$$

**Example:** A concrete surface with $\varepsilon = 0.94$ reads $T_{app} = 303 \; \text{K}$ in a lab where the reflected ambient is $T_{refl} = 290 \; \text{K}$.

Numerator:

$$T_{app}^{4} = 303^{4} = 8.4325 \times 10^{9}$$

$$(1 - \varepsilon) T_{refl}^{4} = 0.06 \times 290^{4} = 0.06 \times 7.0728 \times 10^{9} = 4.244 \times 10^{8}$$

Numerator:

$$8.4325 \times 10^{9} - 4.244 \times 10^{8} = 8.008 \times 10^{9}$$

Division by $\varepsilon = 0.94$:

$$T_{true}^{4} = 8.519 \times 10^{9}$$

$$T_{true} = (8.519 \times 10^{9})^{0.25} = 304.1 \; \text{K} \approx 31.0 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$$

The correction is only ~1 K, but for metallic surfaces (ε ≈ 0.1), the correction is **critical and large**.

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Working Principle of an IR Thermal Camera

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Object Surface Emits IR Radiation] --> B[Optics Germanium Lens]
    B --> C[IR Detector Microbolometer Array 8-14 micrometer]
    C --> D[Signal Processor Amplifier and ADC]
    D --> E[Emissivity and Reflected Temp Correction]
    E --> F[False Color Palette Hot White to Cold Blue]
    F --> G[Digital Thermogram on Display]
    G --> H[Storage and Post Processing FLIR Tools]
    H --> I[Defect Identification and Thermal Contrast delta T]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Experimental Procedure for NDE Using Thermal Camera

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1 Prepare Concrete Specimen with Known Defects] --> P2[Step 2 Apply Emissivity Calibration using Contact Thermometer]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Set Test Parameters Ambient Temp Heating Time Distance]
    P3 --> P4{Passive or Active}
    P4 -- Passive --> P5a[Expose Specimen to Solar or Ambient Thermal Gradient]
    P4 -- Active --> P5b[Apply External Heat Source Halogen Lamp 500 W]
    P5a --> P6[Step 4 Position Camera 1 to 2 m from Surface Perpendicular]
    P5b --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Step 5 Capture Thermogram at 0 min 5 min 10 min 15 min]
    P7 --> P8[Step 6 Record Surface Temperature Distribution]
    P8 --> P9[Step 7 Analyze Image for Hot Cold Spots]
    P9 --> P10[Step 8 Compute Thermal Contrast delta T and Correlate with Defects]
    P10 --> P11[Step 9 Report Findings with Annotated Thermogram]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Typical Defect Signatures in Thermograms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Void Air Pocket] --> S1[Hot Spot in Active Heating Trap of Heat]
    D2[Delamination Rebar Corrosion] --> S2[Hot Spot near Rebar Corrosion Product Acts as Insulator]
    D3[Moisture Infiltration] --> S3[Cold Spot Evaporative Cooling in Passive Mode]
    D4[Honeycombing in Concrete] --> S4[Mottled Irregular Pattern Low Thermal Diffusivity Zones]
    D5[Crack Open to Surface] --> S5[Linear Cold or Hot Line depending on Air Flow]
    D6[Thermal Bridge Missing Insulation] --> S6[Cold Bridge in Winter Hot Bridge in Summer]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!NOTE]
> The question pattern below strictly follows the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination)** pattern: **Part A (3-mark short questions)** and **Part B (14-mark long-answer with internal choice, split as 7 + 7 marks)**.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define Infrared Thermography. Mention any two civil engineering applications.

**Model Answer:**

Infrared Thermography is a non-destructive, non-contact imaging technique that captures the infrared radiation emitted by a material's surface and converts it into a visible two-dimensional temperature map called a thermogram. It is based on the Stefan-Boltzmann Law, $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.

**Two civil engineering applications:**
1. Detection of delamination and voids in reinforced concrete bridge decks and parking structures.
2. Building energy audits to identify thermal bridges, missing insulation, moisture ingress, and air leakage in building envelopes.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for each application)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is emissivity? Why is it important in thermal NDE?

**Model Answer:**

**Emissivity (ε)** is the ratio of thermal radiation emitted by a real surface to that emitted by a perfect blackbody at the same temperature. It ranges from 0 (perfect reflector/polished metal) to 1 (perfect blackbody/rough concrete).

**Importance in thermal NDE:**
1. The radiometric reading of an IR camera is directly proportional to ε. An incorrect ε setting causes large errors in the reported temperature.
2. Different materials (concrete ε ≈ 0.94, polished steel ε ≈ 0.05) emit very differently. A low-ε surface reflects ambient radiation, masking true temperatures.
3. Accurate emissivity calibration is essential for quantitative thermal contrast ($\Delta T$) measurement, which is the basis of defect detection.

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with neat diagrams the **principle and working of an infrared thermal camera**. List the major components and state the spectral range used.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A concrete slab of 50 mm thickness has an air void of 3 mm at a depth of 20 mm from the heated face. The thermal conductivity of concrete is 1.4 W/mK and of air is 0.026 W/mK. A constant heat flux of 250 W/m² is applied to the front face. Calculate the **thermal contrast $\Delta T$** at the surface. Will this defect be detectable by a camera with NETD = 0.05 K?

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Principle:** Every object above absolute zero (0 K) emits electromagnetic radiation in the infrared region. The intensity and spectral distribution depend on its surface temperature and emissivity (Stefan-Boltzmann Law: $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$). An IR thermal camera captures this invisible radiation and converts it into a visible temperature map.

**Major Components (with block diagram):**

1. **Optics (Germanium Lens):** Focuses incoming IR radiation (8–14 µm) onto the detector. Germanium is transparent to LWIR.
2. **IR Detector (Microbolometer / Photon Detector):** A focal plane array (FPA) of 320×240 or 640×480 pixels that converts IR radiation into an electrical signal.
3. **Signal Processor:** Amplifies, digitises (14-bit ADC), and applies non-uniformity correction.
4. **Display:** Shows a false-colour thermogram — typically *iron palette* (cold = blue/purple, hot = red/white/yellow).
5. **Calibration Module:** Inputs for emissivity (ε), reflected temperature, atmospheric temperature, humidity, and camera-to-object distance.

**Spectral Range:** **Long-Wave Infrared (LWIR), 8 µm – 14 µm** (corresponding to peak emission of objects at room temperature per Wien's Displacement Law: $\lambda_{max} \approx 10 \;\mu\text{m}$ at 300 K).

**Working Steps:**
- Object emits IR → Lens focuses → Detector array reads → Electronics process → Software applies ε correction → Thermogram displayed → Post-processing identifies defects via $\Delta T$.

(Marks distribution: 2 principle, 3 components with diagram, 1 spectral range, 1 working steps)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $L = 50 \;\text{mm} = 0.050 \;\text{m}$
- Void thickness $d = 3 \;\text{mm} = 0.003 \;\text{m}$
- Void depth $z = 20 \;\text{mm} = 0.020 \;\text{m}$
- $k_{c} = 1.4 \;\text{W/mK}$
- $k_{air} = 0.026 \;\text{W/mK}$
- Heat flux $Q = 250 \;\text{W/m}^{2}$

**Formula (derived in Section 3.3):**

$$\Delta T = \frac{Q \cdot d}{k_{air}}$$

**Calculation:**

$$\Delta T = \frac{250 \times 0.003}{0.026} = \frac{0.75}{0.026} = 28.85 \;\text{K}$$

**Detectability Check:**

Since $\Delta T = 28.85 \;\text{K} \gg \text{NETD} = 0.05 \;\text{K}$, the contrast is **about 577 times** the noise floor of the camera.

**Conclusion:** The defect will be **clearly detectable** as a strong hot spot in the thermogram. (7 marks: 1 given data, 2 formula, 2 calculation, 1 comparison, 1 conclusion)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]* (Internal Choice)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Differentiate between **active and passive thermography**. With a neat block diagram, describe the **pulsed thermography** technique for subsurface defect detection.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A semi-infinite concrete body has thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 0.8 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$. A pulsed heat source is applied for 5 seconds. Calculate the **thermal penetration depth**. Will a defect at 8 mm depth be detectable? Justify.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Aspect | Passive Thermography | Active Thermography |
|---|---|---|
| Heat Source | Natural (sun, ambient gradients) | Artificial (lamp, flash, ultrasonic) |
| Contrast | Limited to ambient variations | High, controllable |
| Application | Building envelope, moisture, leaks | Subsurface defects, delamination, FRP debonding |
| Sensitivity | Lower | Higher |
| Cost | Lower (no source) | Higher (source + camera) |

**Pulsed Thermography — Block Diagram & Procedure:**

1. A short, high-energy heat pulse (flash lamp, halogen, or xenon, 1 ms – 10 s) is directed at the test surface.
2. The thermal wave propagates into the material by conduction.
3. An IR camera records the **transient cooling** of the surface over time (typically 0.1 s to several minutes).
4. Sound areas cool uniformly; areas above a defect retain heat longer because the defect (air/void) acts as a thermal insulator.
5. The recorded sequence of thermograms is processed using **thermal signal reconstruction (TSR)** and **pulsed phase thermography (PPT)** to extract depth and size of defects.

(7 marks: 3 differences in table, 4 for pulsed thermography)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $\alpha = 0.8 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$
- $t = 5 \;\text{s}$

**Formula (Section 3.2):**

$$\mu = \sqrt{2 \alpha t}$$

**Calculation:**

$$\mu = \sqrt{2 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 5} = \sqrt{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.828 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}$$

$$\mu \approx 2.83 \;\text{mm}$$

**Defect at 8 mm depth:** Since the depth of the defect (8 mm) is **greater** than the thermal penetration depth (2.83 mm), the thermal wave does not reach the defect within the heating time. The defect will **not produce a measurable surface thermal contrast** in 5 seconds.

**Justification:** To detect an 8 mm defect, the heating time must satisfy:

$$t \geq \frac{z^{2}}{2 \alpha} = \frac{(0.008)^{2}}{2 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{1.6 \times 10^{-6}} = 40 \;\text{s}$$

Therefore, heating for at least **40 seconds** (or using a more energetic flash source) is required. (7 marks: 1 given, 2 formula, 2 calculation, 1 comparison, 1 justification)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls — Read Carefully Before Writing the Exam:**
> 1. **Forgetting Emissivity:** Many students report raw camera readings as the "true temperature." This loses at least **1 mark per question** in Part B. Always state the assumed ε and mention the true-temperature correction formula.
> 2. **Confusing °C with K in Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** The law uses **absolute temperature (Kelvin)**. Using °C gives a wrong answer and **2 marks deducted** in derivations.
> 3. **Mixing active and passive thermography descriptions:** They are *not* interchangeable. Examiners check for the **presence/absence of an external heat source** as the key differentiator.
> 4. **Skipping the ASTM/IS standard citation:** Mentioning **ASTM D4788** (concrete bridge deck delamination) or **ASTM C1060** (thermography of building envelopes) adds +1 mark as it shows industry awareness.
> 5. **Incorrect units in thermal conductivity:** $k$ is in **W/mK**, not W/m²K. Mismatch loses 0.5 mark.
> 6. **No diagram in long answers:** A neat block diagram is **mandatory** for full marks in 7-mark sub-parts. Always include one — even a hand-drawn schematic in the answer booklet.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- **Core Principle:** Thermal camera detects **infrared radiation** (8–14 µm) emitted per **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$.
- **Emissivity (ε):** Critical input; for concrete ε ≈ 0.94, for polished metal ε ≈ 0.05. **Always calibrate ε before testing.**
- **Wien's Law:** $\lambda_{max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K → peak emission at room temperature is in **LWIR (~10 µm)**.
- **Two Modes:** **Passive** (natural heat) vs **Active** (artificial heat — pulsed, lock-in, vibro).
- **Heat Transfer Modes:** Conduction (Fourier's Law), Convection (Newton's Cooling), Radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann).
- **Thermal Diffusivity:** $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$ — defects reduce effective α.
- **Penetration Depth:** $\mu = \sqrt{2 \alpha t}$ — determines maximum inspection depth for a given heating time.
- **Thermal Contrast:** $\Delta T = T_{defect} - T_{sound}$ — the **single most important** NDE indicator; must exceed camera NETD (~0.05 K) to be detectable.
- **Typical ΔT for air void:** $\Delta T = Q d / k_{air}$ — large for air (k = 0.026 W/mK), making voids easy to detect.
- **Apparent vs True Temperature:** $T_{true} = \sqrt[4]{(T_{app}^{4} - (1-\varepsilon) T_{refl}^{4})/\varepsilon}$.
- **Civil Engineering Uses:** Bridge deck delamination (ASTM D4788), moisture/leak detection, rebar location, thermal bridges, FRP debon
- **Lab Safety:** Do not point the camera at the sun or high-intensity laser — detector damage. Wear laser safety glasses if using active laser excitation.
- **Key Standards:** ASTM D4788, ASTM C1060, ISO 6781, BS 8706.
- **Reporting:** Always include specimen ID, ε used, $T_{refl}$, ambient T/RH, camera distance, and an **annotated thermogram**.
- **Remember the $T^4$ dependence** — small temperature changes produce large radiation changes — the reason IR cameras are so sensitive.
- **Major Components of Camera:** Germanium optics → Microbolometer FPA → Signal processor → Calibrated display.
- **Typical Exam Trap:** Using °C instead of K in $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ — **always convert to Kelvin**.
